stem: A 4-year-old boy presents to the emergency department with a 24-hour history of progressively worsening shortness of breath and a tight, non-productive cough. He has a known history of atopy, including eczema and a peanut allergy, and has had two previous hospital admissions for viral-induced wheeze. His parents report he had a coryzal illness starting three days ago. He received six puffs of salbutamol via a spacer every three hours at home with minimal effect. On examination, he is alert but agitated, sitting forward and speaking in single words. He has a prominent tracheal tug, severe intercostal recessions, and widespread biphasic wheeze with reduced air entry bilaterally. He is immediately commenced on high-flow oxygen, continuous nebulised salbutamol, nebulised ipratropium bromide, and intravenous hydrocortisone. One hour later, he remains severely distressed with poor air entry.

data_table.Vital signs: Initial: HR 165 bpm, RR 48/min, SpO2 90% (room air). Current (1hr): HR 175 bpm, RR 45/min, SpO2 92% (8 L/min O2 mask)
data_table.Examination: Agitated, single words, severe work of breathing, poor air entry.
data_table.Medications: Continuous nebulised salbutamol, ipratropium, IV hydrocortisone given in ED.

lead_in: What is the single best next intervention?

options.text: Administer a stat dose of intramuscular adrenaline.
options.rationale: No anaphylaxis features (urticaria, angioedema, hypotension) despite peanut history; adrenaline is not indicated here. Would be correct only with confirmed anaphylaxis features (Source: ASCIA Acute Anaphylaxis).

options.text: Commence an intravenous continuous aminophylline infusion.
options.rationale: Aminophylline is third-line in paediatric severe asthma, only after IV magnesium and IV salbutamol have failed. Would be correct as third-line in PICU (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma Acute).

options.text: Intubate and commence mechanical ventilation immediately.
options.rationale: Intubation in severe asthma is fraught with complications (bronchospasm, hypotension, barotrauma) and reserved for absolute respiratory arrest or exhaustion. Would be correct only with impending arrest after maximal medical therapy (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Administer a single bolus of intravenous magnesium sulfate.
options.rationale: In a child with severe acute asthma failing continuous nebulised therapy and systemic corticosteroids, a bolus of intravenous magnesium sulfate is the next recommended step. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma Acute)

options.text: Administer a fluid bolus of 0.9% sodium chloride.
options.rationale: Asthmatic children may be mildly dehydrated, but fluid boluses do not relieve bronchospasm. Would be correct only for confirmed hypovolaemia, not primary asthma treatment (Source: RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: This child has severe acute asthma refractory to first-line therapies (continuous bronchodilators and corticosteroids). The next appropriate step in the Australian step-wise management of paediatric status asthmaticus is an intravenous bolus of magnesium sulfate. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma Acute)
key_points: Severe asthma is characterised by agitation, inability to talk in sentences, severe work of breathing, and SpO2 < 92%.
Initial escalation involves bursts of inhaled bronchodilators transitioning to continuous nebulisers if no improvement.
When continuous nebulisers fail, the first-line intravenous adjunct is magnesium sulfate, followed by an IV salbutamol infusion if needed in an ICU setting.
explanation.pearls: A 'silent chest' or poor air entry indicates critically severe bronchospasm where wheeze may not be audible due to minimal airflow.

stem: A 4-week-old male infant is brought to the clinic by his mother, who is concerned about his persistent yellowish skin discolouration. He was born at 39 weeks gestation. The infant is exclusively breastfed, feeding every 3 hours, and producing 6 - 8 wet nappies per day. His stools have become noticeably pale and chalky over the past week, and his urine leaves a dark yellow stain on the nappy. On examination, the infant is alert and vigorous, with a weight of 4.2 kg (birth weight 3.4 kg). He has frank scleral icterus and widespread cutaneous jaundice extending to the umbilicus. Abdominal examination reveals a smooth, firm liver edge palpable 3 cm below the right costal margin.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 140 bpm, RR 35/min, Temp 36.9 C
data_table.Investigations: Total Bilirubin 210 micromol/L, Conjugated Bilirubin 145 micromol/L, ALT 85 U/L, AST 90 U/L, GGT 410 U/L, ALP 550 U/L, Hb 145 g/L, DAT negative.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Reassure the mother and continue exclusive breastfeeding.
options.rationale: Breast milk jaundice presents with unconjugated hyperbilirubinaemia; this infant's conjugated fraction is severely elevated indicating cholestasis (biliary atresia until proven otherwise). Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: Jaundice).
source_refs: RCH CPG: Jaundice in early infancy

options.text: Commence intensive blue-light phototherapy immediately.
options.rationale: Phototherapy is only for unconjugated hyperbilirubinaemia; in cholestasis it is ineffective and causes bronze baby syndrome. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
source_refs: RCH CPG: Jaundice in early infancy

options.text: Prescribe a trial of oral ursodeoxycholic acid.
options.rationale: Empirical treatment without ruling out time-critical surgical biliary atresia is inappropriate and delays definitive care. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
source_refs: RCH CPG: Jaundice in early infancy

options.text: Arrange an outpatient hepatobiliary iminodiacetic acid scan.
options.rationale: HIDA scan is part of workup but US must be performed first to assess structural anatomy; timeline must be urgent, not routine outpatient. Would be correct only after urgent US (Source: RCH CPG).
source_refs: RCH CPG: Jaundice in early infancy

options.text: Admit for an urgent fasting abdominal ultrasound.
options.rationale: Pale stools, dark urine, and a conjugated bilirubin fraction >20% of total indicate pathological cholestasis. Biliary atresia must be urgently excluded via ultrasound as first-line imaging. (Source: RCH CPG: Jaundice in early infancy)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Jaundice in early infancy

explanation.summary: Biliary atresia is the leading cause of paediatric liver transplantation in Australia, affecting about 1 in 15,000 livebirths. It presents with jaundice persisting beyond 2 weeks, pale (acholic) stools, dark urine, and a conjugated hyperbilirubinaemia. Kasai hepatoportoenterostomy restores bile drainage but its success is highly time-sensitive: operating before 60 days of age achieves jaundice clearance in around 70% of infants and significantly delays transplantation; after 90 days outcomes collapse. Any infant with prolonged jaundice (over 14 days term, 21 days preterm) must have fractionated bilirubin, LFTs, coagulation, urgent fasting hepatobiliary ultrasound, and prompt paediatric gastroenterology or hepatology referral. Differentials include neonatal hepatitis, alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency, choledochal cyst, and congenital infection (CMV, TORCH). Breast milk jaundice is a diagnosis of exclusion that requires unconjugated hyperbilirubinaemia and a thriving infant.
key_points: Any jaundice persisting beyond 14 days (or 21 days in preterm infants) requires fractionation of bilirubin.
Conjugated hyperbilirubinaemia is always pathological and never physiological or due to breast milk.
Biliary atresia presents with conjugated jaundice, pale (acholic) stools, dark urine, and hepatomegaly, requiring a Kasai procedure ideally before 60 days of life.
explanation.pearls: Any jaundice persisting beyond 14 days in a term infant (21 days in a preterm) needs a split bilirubin; conjugated fraction >20% of total or >25 micromol/L is cholestasis and biliary atresia must be excluded urgently because Kasai portoenterostomy outcomes fall sharply after 8 weeks of age (RCH CPG).

sources.label: RCH CPG: Jaundice in early infancy

stem: A 15-year-old girl is brought to the emergency department by ambulance at 22:00 after texting her friend that she 'took a whole packet of pills'. Her mother found empty blister packs of paracetamol (500 mg tablets, 24 pack) and ibuprofen (200 mg tablets, 24 pack) on her bed. The patient is tearful and refuses to confirm exactly when she took the tablets, but her mother states she was last seen well at 15:00 when she returned from school. She has a history of major depressive disorder and is prescribed fluoxetine 20 mg daily, which she takes intermittently. On examination, she is alert and orientated. She complains of mild nausea and generalized abdominal pain, but abdominal examination is soft and non-tender. An ECG shows normal sinus rhythm with a QTc of 410 ms.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 88 bpm, BP 115/70 mmHg, RR 16/min, Temp 36.8 C, GCS 15
data_table.Examination: Alert. Mild nausea. Abdomen soft, non-tender.
data_table.Investigations: ECG: Normal sinus rhythm, QTc 410 ms.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Commence an intravenous acetylcysteine infusion immediately.
options.rationale: In a potentially toxic paracetamol ingestion (>10g or 200mg/kg) where the time of ingestion is unknown or staggered, acetylcysteine must be started immediately without waiting for levels. (Source: RCH CPG: Paracetamol poisoning)

options.text: Administer 50g of activated charcoal orally or via nasogastric tube.
options.rationale: Activated charcoal is effective within 2 h of ingestion (4 h for sustained release); 7-h unknown window makes it unlikely beneficial. Would be correct within 1-2 h of ingestion (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Measure serum paracetamol level and plot on the nomogram.
options.rationale: Rumack-Matthew nomogram is strictly for single known-time acute ingestion; cannot be used in staggered or unknown timing. Would be correct only with single known-time ingestion (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Observe closely and check liver function tests in 12 hours.
options.rationale: Waiting for hepatotoxicity to manifest (24-72 h) misses the critical NAC window to prevent damage entirely. Would never be correct (Source: Australian PIC).

options.text: Commence an intravenous infusion of sodium bicarbonate.
options.rationale: Bicarbonate is for TCA or severe salicylate overdoses; SSRIs and ibuprofen do not cause QRS widening. Would be correct only in TCA toxicity (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

explanation.summary: Paracetamol is the most common deliberate self-poisoning agent in Australian adolescents. Hepatotoxicity is from NAPQI, a reactive metabolite normally detoxified by glutathione; doses above 200 mg/kg or 10 g total deplete glutathione and produce fulminant liver failure within 72-96 hours if untreated. Acetylcysteine replenishes glutathione and is virtually 100% protective if started within 8 hours of a single ingestion. The Australian protocol is the 21-hour intravenous regimen (200 mg/kg over 4 hours, then 100 mg/kg over 16 hours), with extension based on paracetamol level, ALT and INR at end of infusion. The Rumack-Matthew nomogram applies only to single ingestions of immediate-release paracetamol with a known time and a level drawn 4-15 hours post-ingestion. For staggered ingestions, unknown time, sustained-release preparations, or any clinical concern, give acetylcysteine empirically without waiting for the level. Always involve NSW Poisons Information Centre (13 11 26).
key_points: The toxic threshold for acute paracetamol ingestion is 200 mg/kg or 10 g, whichever is less.
If the time of ingestion is unknown or staggered over more than 1 hour, start NAC immediately if the total dose ingested exceeds the toxic threshold.
The treatment nomogram is only valid for single acute ingestions presenting after 4 hours.
explanation.pearls: The Rumack-Matthew nomogram is invalid for staggered ingestions, unknown ingestion time, or sustained-release paracetamol; commence IV acetylcysteine empirically and run the 21-hour Australian regimen (200 mg/kg over 4h, then 100 mg/kg over 16h) (RCH CPG; NSW Poisons Information Centre).

stem: A 9-month-old boy is brought to the emergency department by his mother, who states he rolled off a 50 cm high bed onto a carpeted floor two hours ago. He is previously healthy and meeting appropriate developmental milestones, though he is not yet walking. Since the fall, he has been extremely irritable, refusing to bear weight, and crying whenever his right leg is moved during nappy changes. On examination, his right thigh is swollen and exquisitely tender to palpation. A secondary survey reveals multiple small bruises in varying stages of healing over his central back and abdomen. A plain radiograph of the right lower limb is obtained, revealing a classic bucket-handle (metaphyseal corner) fracture of the distal femur. No other acute injuries are identified on initial assessment.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 145 bpm, RR 30/min, Temp 37.0 C, SpO2 99% on room air
data_table.Examination: Right thigh swollen and tender. Bruising (various ages) on torso.
data_table.Investigations: XR Right Leg: Metaphyseal corner fracture of the distal femur.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Apply a hip spica cast and arrange outpatient orthopaedic follow-up.
options.rationale: While fracture needs orthopaedic management, discharging ignores highly specific NAI signs and places child at extreme risk. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: Child Abuse).

options.text: Perform a bone mineral density scan to rule out osteogenesis.
options.rationale: OI is a rare differential, but corner fracture with torso bruising strongly suggests abuse; investigating rare metabolic causes must not delay mandatory child protection workup. Would be correct only after safeguarding workup excludes abuse (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Admit for a skeletal survey and notify child protection services.
options.rationale: A metaphyseal corner fracture in a non-ambulant infant, combined with unexplained torso bruising in different stages of healing, is highly specific for non-accidental injury, requiring immediate admission, skeletal survey, and mandatory reporting. (Source: RCH CPG: Child Abuse)

options.text: Reassure the mother as this is consistent with the reported fall.
options.rationale: A 50 cm fall onto carpet generates insufficient kinetic energy for a metaphyseal corner fracture (requires violent shearing/twisting). Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Obtain an urgent CT head to rule out abusive head trauma.
options.rationale: Head imaging may be required as part of NAI workup for infants under 12 months with abuse signs, but the immediate next step is admission and notification. Would be correct as part of admission workup, not first step (Source: RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: Non-accidental injury in infants is the second most common cause of paediatric trauma death in Australia, and the index injury that brings the child to attention is often relatively minor. Red flags include any fracture in a non-ambulant child, mechanism inconsistent with developmental stage or with examination findings, delay in seeking care, inconsistent histories, bruising in pre-mobile children (TEN-4 rule: torso, ear, neck in under 4-year-olds, or any bruise in an under-4-month-old), and pathognomonic injuries (classic metaphyseal lesions, posterior rib fractures, scapular fractures, retinal haemorrhage, subdural haematoma). Mandatory reporting applies to all medical practitioners in every state and territory - thresholds vary slightly but suspicion is sufficient. Management is admission to a safe environment, full skeletal survey, CT head and ophthalmology review where indicated, social work and child protection notification, and senior paediatric review. Never accuse caregivers - document objective findings only.
key_points: Metaphyseal corner (bucket-handle) fractures are caused by violent pulling, twisting, or shaking forces and are highly specific for child abuse.
Any bruising in a non-mobile infant (TEN-4 rule: Torso, Ears, Neck in <4 months or any non-mobile child) is a major red flag.
A reported mechanism of injury that does not match the developmental stage or the severity of the injury mandates a child protection evaluation.
explanation.pearls: Classic metaphyseal lesions (corner or bucket-handle fractures) in non-ambulant infants are virtually pathognomonic of non-accidental injury; mandate a full skeletal survey, ophthalmology review for retinal haemorrhage, and an immediate child protection notification under state-specific legislation (RCH CPG).

stem: A 3-year-old girl is rushed to the emergency department by ambulance. Her parents report a 12-hour history of high fever, extreme lethargy, and vomiting. On arrival, she is poorly responsive, groaning to pain. She is notably pale with mottled skin and a non-blanching purpuric rash spreading rapidly across her lower limbs and abdomen. Her extremities are cold to the touch with a capillary refill time of 5 seconds. Two wide-bore intravenous cannulae are immediately secured. Initial point-of-care testing shows a lactate of 5.0 mmol/L. She is immediately given a 20 mL/kg rapid bolus of 0.9% sodium chloride and intravenous ceftriaxone. Reassessment 15 minutes later shows no clinical improvement, and a second 20 mL/kg bolus of 0.9% sodium chloride is administered (total 40 mL/kg given). On repeat assessment, she remains obtunded, capillary refill is 4 seconds, and her blood pressure continues to fall.

data_table.Vital signs: Initial: HR 170 bpm, BP 70/40 mmHg, RR 40/min, Temp 39.5 C, SpO2 92% (room air). Post-fluid (40mL/kg): HR 165 bpm, BP 65/35 mmHg.
data_table.Examination: Obtunded (GCS 9). Cold peripheries, mottled. Spreading purpuric rash.
data_table.Investigations: Venous gas: pH 7.20, pCO2 35 mmHg, Lactate 5.0 mmol/L, BSL 4.2 mmol/L.

lead_in: What is the single best next intervention?

options.text: Administer a third 20 mL/kg bolus of 0.9% sodium chloride.
options.rationale: Fluid-refractory shock persists after 40 mL/kg crystalloid; further boluses increase pulmonary oedema risk without improving perfusion. Would never be correct after 40 mL/kg failure (Source: Surviving Sepsis Paediatric).

options.text: Perform immediate rapid sequence endotracheal intubation.
options.rationale: Intubating a profoundly shocked child before restoring vascular tone is high risk for peri-intubation arrest. Would be correct only after inotrope established and stabilisation (Source: APLS Australia).

options.text: Commence a peripheral intravenous adrenaline infusion.
options.rationale: The child has fluid-refractory 'cold' septic shock (likely meningococcal). Australian guidelines mandate the early commencement of inotropes (adrenaline) peripherally while awaiting central access. (Source: RCH CPG: Sepsis)

options.text: Administer a 10 mL/kg bolus of 4% human albumin solution.
options.rationale: Colloids are not superior to crystalloids in initial sepsis resuscitation; shock is fluid-refractory, requiring vasoactive agents not more volume. Would never be correct (Source: Surviving Sepsis Paediatric).

options.text: Administer a stat dose of intravenous hydrocortisone sodium.
options.rationale: Corticosteroids are for catecholamine-resistant shock or suspected adrenal insufficiency, AFTER adrenaline/noradrenaline started. Would be correct only after inotrope (Source: Surviving Sepsis Paediatric).

explanation.summary: This critically ill child has classic features of meningococcal septicaemia with purpura fulminans, presenting in profound 'cold' shock (tachycardia, prolonged cap refill, hypotension, mottled skin). After receiving 40 mL/kg of crystalloid with no haemodynamic response, she is in fluid-refractory shock. The most critical next step is initiating vasoactive support with an adrenaline infusion via peripheral IV access to restore perfusion pressure. (Source: RCH CPG: Sepsis)
key_points: Paediatric septic shock is often 'cold' (low cardiac output, high systemic vascular resistance) requiring adrenaline, compared to 'warm' shock requiring noradrenaline.
Fluid-refractory shock is defined as persisting signs of poor perfusion after 40 mL/kg of fluid boluses.
Do not delay inotropes to obtain central venous access; peripheral infusions are safe as a bridge in an emergency.
Intubating a severely shocked patient precipitates cardiovascular collapse; optimise haemodynamics with fluids and inotropes first if the airway is maintainable.
explanation.pearls: Hypotension is a late and ominous sign of decompensated shock in children; rely on tachycardia and prolonged capillary refill to guide early aggressive resuscitation.

stem: A 10-year-old boy with type 1 diabetes diagnosed 18 months ago is brought to the emergency department after 24 hours of vomiting and lethargy. His insulin pump tubing disconnected overnight and he has had progressive abdominal pain and thirst since morning. He received a correction bolus by pen 2 hours ago. On arrival he is drowsy but obeys commands. During initial treatment with isotonic fluids and intravenous insulin, he becomes increasingly irritable and complains of severe headache. His mother reports he is now asking repetitive questions. Capillary glucose has fallen from 28.6 mmol/L to 18.2 mmol/L over 90 minutes.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 132 bpm, BP 108/68 mmHg, RR 30/min deep respirations, Temp 37.1 C, SpO2 99%
data_table.Examination: Dry mucosa, delayed capillary refill 3 seconds, new confusion and reduced orientation
data_table.Investigations: pH 7.08, bicarbonate 8 mmol/L, ketones 5.9 mmol/L, Na 131 mmol/L, K 4.7 mmol/L
data_table.Medications: Continuous insulin infusion commenced, isotonic fluids running

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate next step?

options.text: Reduce insulin infusion rate and repeat venous blood gas in 30 minutes before further action
options.rationale: Biochemical reassessment is important but new neurologic deterioration during DKA prompts immediate cerebral oedema treatment, not observation. Would never be correct in suspected cerebral oedema (Source: ISPAD DKA).

options.text: Administer hyperosmolar therapy and urgently escalate for presumed cerebral oedema
options.rationale: Headache, irritability, altered mental status, and repetitive questioning during DKA treatment strongly suggest cerebral oedema. Immediate hyperosmolar treatment and senior escalation are indicated. (Source: RCH CPG Diabetic Ketoacidosis)

options.text: Increase isotonic fluid replacement because persistent dehydration is the likely explanation
options.rationale: Excessive fluid may worsen cerebral oedema risk; neuro decline in this context should not be treated as dehydration. Would never be correct (Source: ISPAD DKA).

options.text: Arrange urgent CT brain before initiating further treatment to confirm diagnosis
options.rationale: Imaging must not delay treatment when cerebral oedema is clinically suspected. Would be correct as adjunct after hyperosmolar therapy started, not before (Source: RCH CPG: DKA).

options.text: Cease insulin and commence dextrose-containing fluids as the glucose is falling rapidly
options.rationale: Dextrose may be added later to allow continued insulin, but stopping insulin risks worsening ketosis and does not address acute neuro decline. Would be correct as add-on, not as primary management (Source: ISPAD DKA).

explanation.summary: The key discriminator is acute neurologic deterioration during DKA treatment. Cerebral oedema remains a clinical diagnosis and must be treated immediately with hyperosmolar therapy and escalation before confirmatory imaging. (Source: RCH CPG Diabetic Ketoacidosis)
key_points: Headache and altered mental state during treatment are red flags for cerebral oedema. (Source: RCH CPG Diabetic Ketoacidosis)
CT imaging should not delay empiric treatment. (Source: ISPAD DKA Guideline)
explanation.pearls: A falling glucose does not exclude worsening intracellular cerebral swelling. (Source: ISPAD DKA Guideline)

stem: A 10-month-old boy presents to ED with 8 hours of intermittent screaming episodes, drawing up of the legs and repeated non-bilious vomiting. Between episodes he is pale and settles briefly but has become increasingly lethargic. His mother reports a mild viral illness last week. He was born at term, has had no previous surgery and is fully immunised. He is afebrile, mildly dehydrated, with a soft but mildly distended abdomen. A small amount of red mucus is seen in the nappy after a pain episode. There is no bilious vomiting and no peritonism. HR 156, RR 30, BP 86/54, SpO2 99 percent on room air.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 37.2 deg C, HR 156 bpm, RR 30/min, BP 86/54 mmHg, SpO2 99% on room air
data_table.Examination: Intermittent colicky pain, mild abdominal distension, no guarding, no palpable hernia
data_table.Investigations: Nil yet
data_table.Medications: No regular medicines; no known drug allergies

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step and how should management proceed if the imaging is positive?

options.text: Abdominal ultrasound now; if a target sign with doughnut or pseudokidney appearance is seen and no peritonitis or perforation, proceed to air-contrast enema reduction after surgical consult
options.rationale: Episodic colicky pain, lethargy, vomiting and red-mucus stool are classic intussusception. Ultrasound is first-line (target/doughnut sign). Once confirmed without contraindications (peritonitis, perforation, shock not responsive to resuscitation), air-contrast (or saline) enema reduction by radiology with surgical standby is the AU first-line definitive management; surgical reduction is reserved for failed enema or contraindications.

options.text: Plain abdominal x-ray and treat empirically as gastroenteritis with oral rehydration and antiemetics in the short-stay observation ward area
options.rationale: AXR can show obstruction but is not the best first test for suspected intussusception; empirical gastroenteritis misses surgical emergency. Would be correct only as adjunct after US (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception).

options.text: Urgent upper GI contrast study to exclude malrotation with volvulus, bypassing ultrasound despite the classical intussusception triad presentation here
options.rationale: Upper GI contrast is for bilious vomiting concerning for malrotation; this is colicky pain with red-mucus stool. Would be correct in malrotation pattern (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: CT abdomen with IV contrast as initial imaging despite the radiation dose and the strong diagnostic utility of bedside ultrasound for this presentation
options.rationale: CT is not first-line in children with suspected intussusception due to radiation; US is preferred. Would be correct only with diagnostic uncertainty after US (Source: RANZCR).

options.text: Stool PCR for infectious gastroenteritis and observe in short stay, bypassing imaging despite the red-currant-jelly stool history described here
options.rationale: Episodic colicky pain with red mucus is not gastroenteritis; stool testing delays diagnosis of surgical emergency. Would be correct only in confirmed infectious gastroenteritis (Source: RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: This infant has a classic presentation of intussusception, and ultrasound is the preferred initial investigation. Gastroenteritis is a distractor here because the episodic pain, lethargy, and red mucus are more concerning for bowel telescoping. Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception.
key_points: Intussusception commonly presents with intermittent colicky pain, vomiting, lethargy, and red mucus or blood per rectum. Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception.
Ultrasound is the initial test of choice in a stable child. Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception.
Bilious vomiting and peritonism would raise concern for a different surgical emergency. Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception.
explanation.pearls: Between pain episodes, the child may look deceptively well, which is why the history is so important. Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception.

stem: A 7-year-old girl receiving chemotherapy for acute lymphoblastic leukaemia presents with fever and malaise. She had her last vincristine and dexamethasone cycle 6 days ago and now has worsening tiredness, reduced oral intake, and mild abdominal discomfort. Her central venous line is clean at the skin but has not been accessed in 2 days. She takes trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole prophylaxis and ondansetron as needed. Her parents report no cough, sore throat, or urinary symptoms, but she had one episode of loose stool this morning. On examination she looks unwell but is not in respiratory distress. She is pale, has cool peripheries, and appears tachycardic.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 39.1 deg C, HR 148 bpm, RR 26/min, BP 94/58 mmHg, SpO2 98% on room air
data_table.Examination: Tired, pale, cool peripheries, central line site clean, abdomen soft with mild diffuse tenderness
data_table.Investigations: FBC: WCC 0.7 x10^9/L, neutrophils 0.1 x10^9/L, platelets 62 x10^9/L, Hb 88 g/L; CRP 96 mg/L; lactate 2.7 mmol/L
data_table.Medications: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole prophylaxis, ondansetron PRN, chemotherapy 6 days ago

lead_in: What is the single best next step in management?

options.text: Take blood cultures and start broad-spectrum IV antibiotics immediately
options.rationale: This child has febrile neutropenia with systemic features, so urgent empiric intravenous antibiotics after appropriate cultures is the correct next step. Treatment should not be delayed while searching for a source. Source: RCH Fever and suspected or confirmed neutropenia.
source_refs: RCH Fever and suspected or confirmed neutropenia

options.text: Observe for 4 hours and repeat FBC before treating
options.rationale: Fever with severe neutropenia is a medical emergency; waiting for repeat counts delays time-critical antibiotics. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile Neutropenia).
source_refs: RCH Fever and suspected or confirmed neutropenia

options.text: Start oral amoxicillin-clavulanate and discharge if stable
options.rationale: Oral therapy and discharge are not appropriate for febrile child with profound neutropenia and raised inflammatory markers. Would be correct only in low-risk febrile neutropenia within research protocols (Source: RCH CPG).
source_refs: RCH Fever and suspected or confirmed neutropenia

options.text: Remove the central venous line before cultures are taken
options.rationale: Line removal is not the first step in line-preserved febrile neutropenia presentation; cultures obtained promptly, antibiotics immediately. Would be correct only with documented line sepsis after stabilisation (Source: RCH CPG).
source_refs: RCH Fever and suspected or confirmed neutropenia

options.text: Give granulocyte colony-stimulating factor and await marrow recovery
options.rationale: G-CSF does not replace urgent empirical antibiotics; the immediate priority is antimicrobial therapy. Would be correct only as adjunct after antibiotics (Source: eviQ).
source_refs: RCH Fever and suspected or confirmed neutropenia

explanation.summary: This child has febrile neutropenia with profound neutropenia and systemic features, which requires prompt cultures and immediate broad-spectrum intravenous antibiotics. Delay to repeat counts, discharge, or line removal would be unsafe. Source: RCH Fever and suspected or confirmed neutropenia.
key_points: Fever with severe neutropenia is an oncologic emergency. Source: RCH Fever and suspected or confirmed neutropenia.
Empiric IV antibiotics should be given promptly after cultures are taken. Source: RCH Fever and suspected or confirmed neutropenia.
A clear focus is not required before treatment starts. Source: RCH Fever and suspected or confirmed neutropenia.
explanation.pearls: Profound neutropenia can make a child appear less floridly septic than expected, so do not be reassured by the absence of a focal source. Source: RCH Fever and suspected or confirmed neutropenia.

sources.label: RCH Fever and suspected or confirmed neutropenia

stem: A 13-year-old girl with type 1 diabetes is brought in with 18 hours of abdominal pain, vomiting, and increasing lethargy after a viral illness. She has missed two doses of insulin because she was not eating well and thought it would be safer to stop it. Her mother says she has been drinking large volumes of water and passing urine frequently for 2 days. She is drowsy but rousable, has deep breathing, and smells strongly of ketones.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 37.4 deg C, HR 132 bpm, RR 30/min with Kussmaul pattern, BP 94/60 mmHg, SpO2 99% on room air
data_table.Examination: Drowsy, dry mucous membranes, tachypnoea, fruity breath, capillary refill 3 s, no meningism
data_table.Investigations: BGL 31.8 mmol/L, venous pH 7.14, HCO3 8 mmol/L, ketones 5.8 mmol/L, Na 130 mmol/L, K 5.5 mmol/L, creatinine 86 micromol/L

lead_in: What is the single best next step in management?

options.text: Start insulin infusion immediately without fluids
options.rationale: Insulin is essential in DKA but fluids and careful resuscitation come first/alongside; insulin without addressing intravascular depletion is unsafe. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: DKA).

options.text: Give a normal saline bolus and begin the paediatric ketoacidosis protocol
options.rationale: This child has moderate to severe DKA with dehydration, acidosis, and ketosis, so the correct next step is fluid resuscitation and a standard DKA protocol. Insulin is then started in a controlled manner after initial fluids and assessment. Source: RCH Diabetic ketoacidosis.

options.text: Administer subcutaneous rapid-acting insulin and discharge if ketones fall
options.rationale: SC insulin is not appropriate for this degree of acidosis, dehydration, and altered mental state; requires hospital-based DKA management. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Give sodium bicarbonate because the pH is below 7.20
options.rationale: Bicarbonate is not routinely used in paediatric DKA; reserved for very specific circumstances. Would be correct only in life-threatening hyperkalaemia or pH under 6.9 (Source: ISPAD DKA).

options.text: Treat hyperkalaemia with calcium gluconate before any fluids
options.rationale: K is elevated but typical DKA management focuses on controlled rehydration and insulin with monitoring; no ECG-instability indication for calcium. Would be correct only with ECG-instability (Source: ISPAD DKA).

explanation.summary: This adolescent has diabetic ketoacidosis with dehydration, acidosis, ketosis, and altered conscious state. The safest next step is initial fluid resuscitation and institution of the paediatric DKA protocol, with controlled insulin therapy and close monitoring. Source: RCH Diabetic ketoacidosis.
key_points: DKA is a medical emergency requiring structured fluid and insulin management. Source: RCH Diabetic ketoacidosis.
Insulin should not be the first standalone step before resuscitation in a dehydrated child. Source: RCH Diabetic ketoacidosis.
Bicarbonate is not routine therapy in paediatric DKA. Source: RCH Diabetic ketoacidosis.
explanation.pearls: Missing insulin during intercurrent illness is a classic trigger for DKA in adolescents with type 1 diabetes. Source: RCH Diabetic ketoacidosis.

stem: An 8-month-old ex-35-week infant is brought to the emergency department in winter with 4 days of cough, coryza, and poor feeding. He was reviewed by a GP yesterday and diagnosed with bronchiolitis. Overnight he took less than half his usual feeds and had two episodes of post-tussive vomiting. His mother reports increasing work of breathing and fewer wet nappies today. He has no history of congenital heart disease but required CPAP briefly after birth. In the emergency department he is started on low-flow oxygen via nasal prongs with transient improvement in saturations. Thirty minutes later he remains tachypnoeic with persistent subcostal and intercostal recession. Capillary blood gas shows mild hypercapnia without acidosis.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 168 bpm, RR 62/min, BP 92/58 mmHg, Temp 37.8 C, SpO2 89% room air improving to 93% on 2 L/min oxygen
data_table.Examination: Moderate subcostal and intercostal recession, diffuse crackles with scattered wheeze, dry mucous membranes, capillary refill 2 seconds
data_table.Investigations: CBG pH 7.34, pCO2 48 mmHg, HCO3 24 mmol/L
data_table.Medications: No regular medications, immunisations up to date

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Commence high-flow nasal prong therapy and continue supportive management with nasogastric hydration
options.rationale: Persistent hypoxia and moderate work of breathing despite low-flow oxygen indicate need for escalation to high-flow nasal prong therapy. Supportive care remains the cornerstone of bronchiolitis management. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Trial inhaled salbutamol and reassess respiratory rate before considering respiratory support escalation
options.rationale: Routine bronchodilator trials are not recommended in typical bronchiolitis (no reliable improvement). Would be correct only in older viral wheeze (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Commence intravenous ceftriaxone because the hypercapnia suggests developing bacterial pneumonia
options.rationale: Mild hypercapnia may occur with worsening bronchiolitis and does not suggest bacterial pneumonia; no focal findings or septic features. Would be correct with focal pneumonia features (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Arrange urgent intubation because preterm birth increases risk of sudden respiratory decompensation
options.rationale: Prematurity increases severity risk but this infant does not show impending respiratory arrest, severe acidosis or exhaustion. Would be correct only with impending failure (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Discharge home with frequent oral fluids because oxygen saturations improved on nasal prongs
options.rationale: Persistent tachypnoea, increased WOB, poor intake and oxygen requirement make discharge unsafe. Would be correct only without those features (Source: RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: This infant has moderate-to-severe bronchiolitis with persistent respiratory distress despite low-flow oxygen. Australian bronchiolitis guidance recommends escalation to high-flow nasal prong therapy with supportive hydration rather than bronchodilators or antibiotics in typical disease. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)
key_points: Bronchiolitis management is primarily supportive. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)
Poor feeding and persistent work of breathing are indications for admission and escalation of respiratory support. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Respiratory)
explanation.pearls: Scattered wheeze alone does not make bronchodilator-responsive asthma more likely in typical infant bronchiolitis. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)

stem: A 5-year-old boy is brought to a regional emergency department with fever, vomiting, and progressive lethargy over 12 hours. His parents report he complained of leg pain earlier today and became difficult to wake after an afternoon nap. On arrival he is confused and intermittently agitated. A blanching maculopapular rash on his trunk has evolved over the past hour into scattered non-blanching purpuric lesions over the lower limbs. Peripheral IV access is difficult and blood cultures have not yet been obtained. During assessment he develops cool peripheries and prolonged capillary refill.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 39.7 C, HR 168 bpm, RR 36/min, BP 76/42 mmHg, SpO2 95% room air
data_table.Examination: Confused, mottled skin, purpuric rash over legs, neck stiffness difficult to assess, capillary refill 5 seconds
data_table.Investigations: VBG pH 7.24, lactate 5.8 mmol/L, glucose 4.9 mmol/L
data_table.Medications: No regular medications, no known drug allergies

lead_in: What is the single best next step in management?

options.text: Perform urgent lumbar puncture before antibiotics because CSF confirmation will guide therapy duration
options.rationale: LP is contraindicated in haemodynamic instability and should not delay treatment of suspected meningococcal sepsis. Would never be correct in shock (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Administer empiric intravenous ceftriaxone immediately while initiating aggressive septic shock resuscitation
options.rationale: This child has meningococcal septic shock with purpura, hypotension, and elevated lactate. Empiric antibiotics and sepsis resuscitation must not be delayed for cultures or lumbar puncture. (Source: RCH CPG Meningococcal Infection)

options.text: Obtain blood cultures and CT brain before antimicrobial therapy because altered consciousness raises concern for raised ICP
options.rationale: Imaging and cultures are secondary in unstable septic shock; delaying antibiotics increases mortality in IMD. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Commence aciclovir and transfer for MRI because a viral CNS cause is more likely here
options.rationale: Purpura, shock physiology, prolonged cap refill and elevated lactate strongly support meningococcal sepsis rather than encephalitis. Would be correct only with encephalitis features without shock/purpura (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Administer intramuscular benzylpenicillin and observe response before escalating to intravenous therapy
options.rationale: IM benzylpenicillin may be considered in remote settings but this critically unwell child requires immediate IV therapy. Would be correct only in remote pre-hospital settings (Source: CDNA IMD SoNG).

explanation.summary: The critical reasoning step is prioritising immediate empiric treatment over diagnostic completeness in suspected meningococcal septic shock. Purpura, hypotension, prolonged capillary refill, elevated lactate, and altered consciousness indicate invasive meningococcal disease requiring urgent antibiotics and sepsis resuscitation. (Source: RCH CPG Meningococcal Infection)
key_points: Lumbar puncture is contraindicated in haemodynamic instability. (Source: RCH CPG Meningococcal Infection)
Antibiotics should not be delayed for imaging or cultures in septic shock. (Source: ANZCOR Paediatric Sepsis)
explanation.pearls: Leg pain and evolving purpura are early clues to meningococcal disease in children before classic meningism becomes obvious. (Source: RCH CPG Meningococcal Infection)

stem: A 13-year-old boy with moderate persistent asthma presents to the emergency department with worsening shortness of breath over 8 hours after developing rhinorrhoea and cough 2 days earlier. He used salbutamol every 2 hours overnight with progressively shorter relief. On arrival he can speak only in short phrases and prefers to sit upright. He receives inhaled salbutamol and ipratropium with oral dexamethasone. Ninety minutes later he remains tachypnoeic with increasing fatigue. Air entry is now reduced bilaterally and wheeze is quieter than at presentation. A venous blood gas is repeated because nursing staff are concerned he appears more drowsy.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 148 bpm, RR 38/min, BP 118/70 mmHg, SpO2 91% on room air improving to 94% with oxygen
data_table.Examination: Marked accessory muscle use, reduced bilateral air entry, quieter wheeze, intermittent drowsiness
data_table.Investigations: Initial VBG pH 7.37 pCO2 39 mmHg, repeat VBG pH 7.29 pCO2 56 mmHg
data_table.Medications: Repeated inhaled salbutamol and ipratropium, oral dexamethasone

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Continue intermittent salbutamol only because the oxygen saturation has improved with supplemental oxygen
options.rationale: Improved SpO2 alone does not indicate stability; rising CO2 and fatigue suggest impending failure requiring escalation. Would never be correct with rising CO2 (Source: RCH CPG: Acute Asthma).

options.text: Arrange discharge with prednisolone because reduced wheeze indicates treatment response
options.rationale: Quieter chest with worsening hypercapnia and fatigue is severe airflow limitation, not improvement; discharge unsafe. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Escalate to intensive care review for intravenous therapy and possible respiratory support
options.rationale: Rising pCO2, fatigue, altered mental state, and reduced air entry indicate life-threatening asthma with impending respiratory failure requiring PICU-level escalation. (Source: RCH CPG Acute Asthma)

options.text: Administer intravenous antibiotics because viral-triggered bacterial pneumonia is now most likely
options.rationale: No focal infective findings; the major issue is worsening ventilatory failure from severe asthma. Would be correct only with focal pneumonia features (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Cease bronchodilators and commence non-invasive ventilation on the ward because tachycardia suggests toxicity
options.rationale: Tachycardia is common after beta-agonists in severe distress; bronchodilators should not be ceased in deteriorating severe asthma. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: The key discriminator is recognising deterioration despite treatment. Rising carbon dioxide in acute asthma, especially with fatigue and reduced air entry, indicates impending respiratory failure and need for intensive care escalation. (Source: RCH CPG Acute Asthma)
key_points: Normalising or rising pCO2 in severe asthma is a dangerous sign. (Source: RCH CPG Acute Asthma)
Reduced wheeze can reflect critically poor airflow rather than improvement. (Source: ANZCOR Acute Asthma)
explanation.pearls: In severe asthma, a quieter chest can be more concerning than loud wheeze. (Source: RCH CPG Acute Asthma)

stem: A 7-week-old previously healthy girl presents with fever and reduced feeding for 10 hours. She was born at term by spontaneous vaginal delivery and required no neonatal admission. Her mother reports increased sleepiness and only two wet nappies since morning. There is no cough, rhinorrhoea, vomiting, or diarrhoea. On examination the infant briefly opens her eyes to stimulation but settles quickly back to sleep. Peripheral perfusion is mildly delayed. Urinalysis obtained by catheter specimen shows positive leukocyte esterase and nitrites.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 38.4 C, HR 178 bpm, RR 44/min, BP 82/48 mmHg, SpO2 99% room air
data_table.Examination: Mild lethargy, delayed capillary refill 3 seconds, soft fontanelle, no increased work of breathing
data_table.Investigations: WCC 17.8 x10^9/L, CRP 42 mg/L, urinalysis positive nitrites and leukocyte esterase

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Discharge with oral cephalexin because a urinary source is the likely cause of fever
options.rationale: Young febrile infants under 3 months require inpatient assessment and parenteral antibiotics due to high invasive bacterial infection risk. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile Child).

options.text: Commence empiric intravenous antibiotics and admit for full septic evaluation and observation
options.rationale: This infant is under 3 months with fever, lethargy, inflammatory marker elevation, and likely UTI. Full sepsis assessment and inpatient IV antibiotics are indicated. (Source: RCH CPG Febrile Child)

options.text: Observe in the emergency department without antibiotics until blood culture results become available
options.rationale: Delaying antibiotics in a potentially septic young infant is unsafe (rapid deterioration risk). Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
source_refs: RCH CPG Recognition of the Seriously Unwell Child

options.text: Arrange renal ultrasound before treatment because structural urinary abnormalities are likely
options.rationale: Renal imaging may be considered later but should not delay urgent evaluation and treatment of febrile UTI in a young infant. Would be correct as adjunct after stabilisation (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Repeat observations after paracetamol because fever alone may explain the tachycardia and lethargy
options.rationale: Fever contributes to tachycardia but lethargy and poor perfusion in a young infant require immediate sepsis-focused management. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
source_refs: RCH CPG Recognition of the Seriously Unwell Child

explanation.summary: Febrile infants younger than 3 months require a low threshold for full septic workup and inpatient management. This infant has lethargy, inflammatory marker elevation, and likely UTI, making IV antibiotics and admission essential. (Source: RCH CPG Febrile Child)
key_points: Young infants may deteriorate rapidly despite initially subtle examination findings. (Source: RCH CPG Recognition of the Seriously Unwell Child)
Positive nitrites on catheter urine strongly support bacterial UTI. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic)
explanation.pearls: A well-appearing moment while being comforted does not reliably exclude serious illness in young infants. (Source: RCH CPG Febrile Child)

sources.label: RCH CPG Recognition of the Seriously Unwell Child

stem: A 4-year-old boy is brought to the ED with 14 hours of fever, headache, vomiting, and increasing irritability. His parents say he was well yesterday but became sleepy and complained of pain when the room was bright. On examination he is drowsy but rousable, with neck stiffness and photophobia. He has a blanching maculopapular viral exanthem over his trunk, but no purpuric rash. His capillary refill is 3 seconds, and he is more tachycardic than expected. The registrar is considering lumbar puncture, but the child looks increasingly unwell.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 39.6 deg C, HR 154 bpm, RR 28/min, BP 94/58 mmHg, SpO2 98% on room air
data_table.Examination: Drowsy, neck stiffness, photophobia, no focal neurology, capillary refill 3 s
data_table.Investigations: Nil yet
data_table.Medications: No regular medicines; no known drug allergies

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Perform lumbar puncture immediately before any antibiotics
options.rationale: Systemically unwell with suspected meningitis and possible sepsis - treatment should not be delayed for LP; early antibiotics are time-critical. Would never be correct in unstable patient (Source: RCH CPG: Meningitis).

options.text: Take blood cultures and start empirical intravenous antibiotics
options.rationale: Suspected bacterial meningitis with evolving sepsis requires prompt blood cultures and empirical intravenous antibiotics without waiting for LP. The photophobia, neck stiffness, and altered alertness make meningitis more likely than migraine or a simple viral illness. Source: RCH Febrile child; RCH meningitis guidance.

options.text: Observe for 2 hours and repeat neurological examination
options.rationale: Observation alone is unsafe with fever, meningism, and reduced consciousness; delay increases bacterial meningitis/sepsis risk. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Give oral ibuprofen and discharge with safety-netting
options.rationale: Not a benign viral syndrome; drowsiness, neck stiffness, photophobia, and haemodynamic abnormality require urgent treatment. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Arrange CT brain before deciding on antibiotics
options.rationale: CT is not first step in unstable child with suspected meningitis unless specific raised ICP signs or focal neurology. Would be correct only with focal neuro signs (Source: RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: This child has suspected bacterial meningitis with possible sepsis, so blood cultures and immediate empirical intravenous antibiotics are the correct next step. Lumbar puncture and imaging must not delay treatment when the child is clinically worsening. Source: RCH Febrile child; RCH meningitis guidance.
key_points: Neck stiffness, photophobia, fever, and altered alertness are high-risk features for meningitis. Source: RCH meningitis guidance.
Blood cultures and early IV antibiotics should precede LP if the child is unstable. Source: RCH Febrile child; RCH meningitis guidance.
A viral rash does not exclude serious bacterial infection. Source: RCH Febrile child.
explanation.pearls: When the child is deteriorating, the question is not whether LP is eventually needed - it is whether treatment can safely wait. Source: RCH meningitis guidance.

stem: A 2-year-old boy is brought to an after-hours clinic with refusal to use his right arm since being collected from childcare this afternoon. His mother says he was playing on a low couch when he started crying, but she did not witness any major fall. He has had one previous ED attendance for a bruised cheek, explained as a collision with a toy box. He is otherwise well, with no fever or systemic symptoms. On examination he is holding the elbow slightly flexed and pronated, and he cries when the forearm is supinated. There is no obvious deformity, but there is a faint bruise on the upper arm. He is fully immunised and has no relevant past medical history. The clinician wonders whether this is a nursemaid's elbow or whether the bruising means something more concerning.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 36.8 deg C, HR 112 bpm, RR 22/min, SpO2 99% on room air
data_table.Examination: Refuses to use arm, mild tenderness, faint upper arm bruise, no swelling, no deformity
data_table.Investigations: Nil yet
data_table.Medications: No regular medicines; no known drug allergies

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Attempt reduction of a pulled elbow
options.rationale: Posture and pain fit pulled elbow, but bruise and concerning social context mean clinician should not stop at reduction alone; broader injury and safeguarding assessment required. Would be correct only without bruise/concerning context (Source: RCH CPG: Child Protection).

options.text: Order dedicated limb radiographs and document the bruising carefully
options.rationale: Unexplained bruising in a toddler with reduced limb use warrants imaging and careful documentation, because a fracture or non-accidental injury must be considered. The history is not sufficiently reassuring for a simple pulled elbow alone. Source: RCH child protection guidance; RCH upper limb injury guidance.

options.text: Discharge with reassurance because there is no deformity
options.rationale: Absence of deformity does not exclude fracture or inflicted injury; bruise and inconsistent mechanism require further assessment. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Provide oral ibuprofen and review in the morning
options.rationale: Analgesia alone is inadequate with possible fracture or safeguarding concern; delay could miss significant injury. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Apply a sling and treat as a viral synovitis
options.rationale: Viral synovitis is lower-limb or systemic picture and does not explain focal arm bruising after questionable mechanism. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: This toddler needs careful assessment for fracture and possible non-accidental injury because the bruise and history are not fully reassuring for a simple pulled elbow. Imaging and documentation are more appropriate than assuming a benign traction injury. Source: RCH child protection guidance; RCH upper limb injury guidance.
key_points: Reduced limb use plus unexplained bruising is a safeguarding red flag. Source: RCH child protection guidance.
A pulled elbow can mimic more serious injury, so the broader context matters. Source: RCH pulled elbow guidance.
If the history is inconsistent or concerning, image the limb and consider child protection review. Source: RCH child protection guidance.
explanation.pearls: The most common exam trap is to recognise the pulled elbow posture and stop thinking - the bruise is the clue that changes the pathway. Source: RCH child protection guidance.

stem: A 14-month-old boy is brought to the emergency department with episodic distress and vomiting for 9 hours. His parents report sudden periods of screaming and drawing his knees to his chest lasting 1 to 2 minutes, followed by lethargy and apparent exhaustion. Earlier today he tolerated small sips of fluid but has since vomited twice, most recently with bilious content. He had a mild viral upper respiratory illness 4 days ago. On arrival he appears pale and intermittently drowsy between episodes of pain. During examination he becomes irritable and inconsolable before settling again. Shortly afterwards the child passes a small stool mixed with blood and mucus.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 158 bpm, RR 30/min, BP 92/58 mmHg, Temp 37.2 C, SpO2 99% room air
data_table.Examination: Intermittent abdominal guarding, mild abdominal distension, palpable sausage-shaped mass in right upper quadrant
data_table.Investigations: VBG lactate 2.8 mmol/L, glucose 4.6 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Commence oral rehydration therapy and observe because viral gastroenteritis remains the most likely diagnosis
options.rationale: Intermittent severe pain, lethargy, bilious vomiting, red currant jelly stool strongly suggest intussusception not gastroenteritis. Would be correct only in proven gastroenteritis (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception).

options.text: Arrange urgent air enema reduction after surgical consultation and initial stabilisation
options.rationale: This child has classic intussusception features. Air enema is both diagnostic and therapeutic after appropriate resuscitation and surgical involvement. (Source: RCH CPG Intussusception)

options.text: Request urgent CT abdomen because the palpable abdominal mass suggests malignancy
options.rationale: US and air enema are preferred first-line investigations/management; CT is not first-line. Would be correct only after non-diagnostic US (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Proceed directly to laparotomy because bilious vomiting confirms bowel perforation
options.rationale: Bilious vomiting suggests obstruction but does not confirm perforation; non-operative reduction remains appropriate without peritonitis. Would be correct only with peritonitis or perforation (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Administer intravenous ceftriaxone because occult bacteraemia commonly presents with episodic abdominal pain
options.rationale: Episodic pain and red currant jelly stool are more consistent with intussusception than primary bacteraemia. Would never be correct as primary (Source: RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: This vignette integrates classic but evolving intussusception findings: episodic colicky pain, lethargy, bilious vomiting, sausage-shaped mass, and red currant jelly stool. The appropriate next step is stabilisation followed by air enema reduction with surgical involvement. (Source: RCH CPG Intussusception)
key_points: Children with intussusception may appear lethargic between painful episodes. (Source: RCH CPG Intussusception)
Air enema is both diagnostic and therapeutic in stable patients without perforation. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Gastrointestinal)
explanation.pearls: A single loose stool early in illness can mislead clinicians toward gastroenteritis before classic bowel obstruction features emerge. (Source: RCH CPG Intussusception)

stem: A 3-year-old boy presents with persistent fever for 6 days. He was initially diagnosed with viral infection by a local clinic after developing conjunctival injection and irritability. Over the following days he developed cracked lips, reduced oral intake, swelling of the hands and feet, and a polymorphous truncal rash. Yesterday he began refusing to walk because of discomfort in his feet. In the emergency department he is miserable and difficult to console but alert. There is no cough or coryza. Cervical lymphadenopathy is present on the left side.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 39.4 C, HR 148 bpm, RR 26/min, BP 96/58 mmHg, SpO2 99% room air
data_table.Examination: Non-purulent conjunctivitis, strawberry tongue, cracked lips, oedematous hands and feet, diffuse rash
data_table.Investigations: CRP 132 mg/L, ESR 78 mm/hr, WCC 18.2 x10^9/L, platelets 390 x10^9/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Continue supportive management because thrombocytosis has not yet developed and diagnosis remains uncertain
options.rationale: Thrombocytosis develops later in KD and treatment should not be delayed when clinical criteria are met. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki).

options.text: Commence intravenous immunoglobulin and aspirin with echocardiography assessment
options.rationale: This child meets criteria for Kawasaki disease with prolonged fever and classic mucocutaneous findings. Early IVIG reduces coronary artery complication risk. (Source: RCH CPG Kawasaki Disease)

options.text: Isolate and commence vitamin A because incomplete measles vaccination makes measles the most likely diagnosis
options.rationale: Measles has cough, coryza, conjunctivitis; this child has classic Kawasaki mucocutaneous and extremity findings. Would be correct in measles prodrome (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Commence intravenous ceftriaxone because persistent fever with cervical lymphadenopathy suggests occult sepsis
options.rationale: Combination of conjunctivitis, oral changes, extremity swelling, rash and prolonged fever is far more consistent with Kawasaki than bacterial sepsis. Would be correct in confirmed sepsis (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Delay treatment until coronary artery abnormalities are confirmed on echocardiography
options.rationale: Treatment is guided by clinical diagnosis and should occur promptly to reduce coronary aneurysm risk, not delayed for echo confirmation. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: This child has classic Kawasaki disease with prolonged fever, conjunctivitis, mucosal changes, rash, extremity changes, and lymphadenopathy. The challenge is resisting delay due to misleading alternative explanations and absence of late thrombocytosis. (Source: RCH CPG Kawasaki Disease)
key_points: IVIG reduces risk of coronary artery aneurysm when given early. (Source: American Heart Association Kawasaki Guideline)
Platelet elevation often appears later in illness. (Source: RCH CPG Kawasaki Disease)
explanation.pearls: Extreme irritability is a common but under-recognised clue to Kawasaki disease in young children. (Source: RCH CPG Kawasaki Disease)

stem: A 5-year-old boy is brought in after a generalised tonic-clonic seizure lasting 2 minutes. He has had 4 days of cough, fever, and reduced appetite, and his parents have been encouraging large volumes of water because he 'did not want to drink milk'. He has been more sleepy since morning and vomited once before the seizure. On arrival he is postictal but arousable. He is not meningitic and has no focal neurology. His chest examination suggests a lower respiratory infection, but he has no wheeze.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 38.5 deg C, HR 124 bpm, RR 30/min, BP 98/62 mmHg, SpO2 96% on room air
data_table.Examination: Postictal, no focal deficit, no meningism, chest crackles at the right base
data_table.Investigations: Serum sodium 119 mmol/L, potassium 4.1 mmol/L, glucose 5.6 mmol/L, urea 3.2 mmol/L, creatinine 28 micromol/L
data_table.Medications: No regular medicines; no known drug allergies

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Restrict fluids and repeat sodium in 6 hours
options.rationale: Fluid restriction may be later management but a child who has seized from severe hyponatraemia needs urgent active correction. Would be correct only in asymptomatic SIADH (Source: RCH CPG: Hyponatraemia).

options.text: Give 3% hypertonic saline for symptomatic sodium derangement
options.rationale: This child has symptomatic severe hyponatraemia with a seizure, so urgent hypertonic saline is indicated. The concurrent respiratory infection and excess free-water intake support a dilutional mechanism, but seizure treatment comes first. Source: RCH Hyponatraemia.

options.text: Start oral sodium chloride and observe on the ward
options.rationale: Oral salt is too slow for symptomatic severe hyponatraemia with seizure; emergency requires controlled IV correction. Would be correct only in mild chronic hyponatraemia (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Treat as febrile seizure and discharge once alert
options.rationale: Febrile seizure does not explain Na 119 mmol/L; biochemical abnormality is the key driver. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Administer desmopressin to reduce urinary water loss
options.rationale: Desmopressin would worsen water retention; not treatment for symptomatic hyponatraemic seizure. Would be correct only in central DI (Source: RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: This child has symptomatic severe hyponatraemia presenting with a seizure, so urgent hypertonic saline is the correct immediate treatment. The respiratory infection and excess free-water intake provide a plausible cause, but they do not change the emergency response. Source: RCH Hyponatraemia.
key_points: Seizure is a symptom of dangerous hyponatraemia and requires immediate treatment. Source: RCH Hyponatraemia.
Hypertonic saline is used for symptomatic severe hyponatraemia. Source: RCH Hyponatraemia.
A febrile illness may be the trigger, but the sodium level is what mandates urgent correction. Source: RCH Hyponatraemia.
explanation.pearls: In a child with both fever and a low sodium, do not stop at the fever diagnosis if the sodium is severe enough to explain the seizure. Source: RCH Hyponatraemia.

stem: A 7-year-old boy presents with worsening abdominal pain and a rash over 4 days. One week earlier he had a mild upper respiratory illness that resolved spontaneously. His parents initially noticed small red-purple spots over his ankles and buttocks, followed by swelling around both knees. Today he developed intermittent crampy abdominal pain severe enough to wake him overnight and has vomited twice. On examination he is uncomfortable but alert. The rash is palpable and non-blanching. His knees and ankles are mildly swollen but not erythematous. Urinalysis performed at triage demonstrates new microscopic haematuria and proteinuria.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 37.1 C, HR 118 bpm, BP 126/82 mmHg, RR 22/min, SpO2 99% room air
data_table.Examination: Palpable purpura over lower limbs and buttocks, mild periarticular swelling, diffuse abdominal tenderness without guarding
data_table.Investigations: Urinalysis positive blood and protein, creatinine 74 micromol/L, platelets 338 x10^9/L

lead_in: Which finding most strongly indicates need for closer inpatient monitoring in this child?

options.text: Recent upper respiratory infection preceding the onset of rash and joint symptoms
options.rationale: Preceding viral illness is common in IgA vasculitis and does not indicate severe disease. Would never be a marker for monitoring escalation (Source: RCH CPG: HSP).

options.text: Palpable purpura involving the buttocks and extensor surfaces of the lower limbs
options.rationale: Palpable purpura is characteristic but not by itself a marker of severe disease. Would never be correct as the discriminating feature (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Microscopic haematuria with elevated blood pressure despite preserved creatinine
options.rationale: Renal involvement with hypertension increases concern for significant nephritis and requires closer monitoring and follow-up even if creatinine is initially normal. (Source: RCH CPG Henoch-Schonlein Purpura)

options.text: Mild periarticular swelling affecting both knees and ankles without erythema
options.rationale: Transient arthralgia and periarticular swelling are common and self-limited. Would never be the discriminator (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Intermittent crampy abdominal pain associated with two episodes of vomiting
options.rationale: Abdominal pain is common in IgA vasculitis though severe/persistent pain may need intussusception workup; renal findings are more concerning here. Would be correct only with severe persistent abdominal pain (Source: RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: This child has IgA vasculitis with classic purpura, abdominal pain, and arthralgia. The discriminating feature is renal involvement with hypertension, which predicts greater risk of clinically significant nephritis and warrants closer inpatient monitoring and follow-up. (Source: RCH CPG Henoch-Schonlein Purpura)
key_points: Renal involvement determines long-term prognosis in IgA vasculitis. (Source: KDIGO Glomerulonephritis Guideline)
Hypertension in children with IgA vasculitis should not be dismissed even with normal creatinine. (Source: RCH CPG Henoch-Schonlein Purpura)
explanation.pearls: Abdominal pain in IgA vasculitis may precede or overshadow the rash, leading to mistaken surgical diagnoses. (Source: RCH CPG Henoch-Schonlein Purpura)

stem: A 15-year-old boy presents to the emergency department with sudden severe left scrotal pain that woke him from sleep 5 hours ago. On arrival he walks hunched over and appears distressed. Examination shows a swollen tender left hemiscrotum with the testis lying slightly higher than the right. Cremasteric reflex is absent on the left. Urinalysis is negative for leukocytes and nitrites.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 36.8 C, HR 112 bpm, BP 118/74 mmHg, RR 18/min
data_table.Examination: High-riding tender left testis, absent left cremasteric reflex, swollen hemiscrotum
data_table.Investigations: Urinalysis negative for infection markers

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Commence oral antibiotics for epididymitis and arrange outpatient ultrasound review
options.rationale: Sudden severe pain, absent cremasteric reflex, high-riding testis strongly suggest torsion requiring surgery; antibiotics for epididymitis miss the surgical emergency. Would be correct only with positive urinalysis and STI risk (Source: RCH CPG: Acute Scrotum).

options.text: Arrange urgent scrotal ultrasound before referral because imaging confirmation is required prior to surgery
options.rationale: Imaging should not delay surgical review when torsion suspicion is high; viability declines with time. Would be correct only with low clinical suspicion (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Refer immediately for urgent surgical exploration of the scrotum
options.rationale: This presentation is highly concerning for testicular torsion. Immediate surgical exploration is indicated and should not be delayed for imaging. (Source: RCH CPG Acute Scrotal Pain)

options.text: Attempt manual detorsion and discharge if the pain improves significantly afterwards
options.rationale: Manual detorsion may be attempted by experienced clinicians but does not replace urgent surgical exploration and fixation. Would be correct only as bridge to theatre (Source: EAU Paediatric).

options.text: Observe with serial examinations because the absence of fever reduces the likelihood of surgical pathology
options.rationale: Lack of fever does not reduce torsion concern; delayed treatment risks irreversible ischaemia. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: The challenge is prioritising time-critical surgical management over diagnostic certainty. Sudden severe unilateral scrotal pain, absent cremasteric reflex, and high-riding testis strongly support torsion and warrant immediate exploration. (Source: RCH CPG Acute Scrotal Pain)
key_points: Ultrasound should not delay surgery in clinically likely torsion. (Source: EAU Paediatric Urology Guideline)
Intermittent prior discomfort may reflect intermittent torsion before complete torsion occurs. (Source: RCH CPG Acute Scrotal Pain)
explanation.pearls: A normal urinalysis helps argue against epididymitis but does not itself diagnose torsion. (Source: RCH CPG Acute Scrotal Pain)

stem: A 5-month-old boy presents during winter with 3 days of cough, rhinorrhoea, and poor feeding. Today his parents noticed increased work of breathing and reduced wet nappies. He was born at term with no neonatal complications. His older sibling recently had a viral upper respiratory illness. On arrival he is alert but feeding poorly and taking only half his usual volumes. Examination reveals diffuse crackles and wheeze bilaterally with moderate subcostal recession. A medical student asks whether salbutamol nebulisers should be trialled because wheeze is prominent on auscultation.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 37.8 C, HR 154 bpm, RR 54/min, BP 88/50 mmHg, SpO2 90% room air
data_table.Examination: Moderate work of breathing, diffuse crackles and wheeze, moist mucous membranes
data_table.Investigations: No investigations performed

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Commence supplemental oxygen and supportive care with feeding assessment
options.rationale: Supportive care including oxygen and hydration assessment is the mainstay of bronchiolitis management when hypoxia and feeding difficulty are present. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Administer repeated salbutamol nebulisers because wheeze indicates likely reactive airways disease
options.rationale: Routine bronchodilators are not recommended in typical bronchiolitis (limited benefit). Would be correct only in older viral wheeze (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Commence oral prednisolone because moderate respiratory distress predicts steroid responsiveness
options.rationale: Corticosteroids not routinely recommended for uncomplicated bronchiolitis. Would be correct only in croup or asthma (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Arrange chest radiography because diffuse crackles suggest bacterial pneumonia requiring antibiotics
options.rationale: Routine CXR not indicated in typical bronchiolitis and may increase unnecessary antibiotic use. Would be correct only with focal consolidation suspicion (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Discharge home after nasal suction because oxygen saturations commonly fluctuate during viral illnesses
options.rationale: Persistent hypoxia and poor feeding warrant inpatient supportive management rather than discharge. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: This infant has typical bronchiolitis with hypoxia and feeding difficulty. The reasoning challenge is avoiding unnecessary bronchodilator or steroid use and recognising that supportive care remains the evidence-based cornerstone of management. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)
key_points: Oxygen and hydration assessment are key components of bronchiolitis management. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)
Routine bronchodilators and corticosteroids are not recommended in uncomplicated bronchiolitis. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)
explanation.pearls: Prominent wheeze in bronchiolitis often tempts clinicians toward asthma-style therapy despite limited evidence of benefit. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)

stem: A 7-year-old boy with known moderate persistent asthma presents to the emergency department with an acute exacerbation. He has had a coryzal illness for 3 days, and his asthma has been worsening despite increasing his fluticasone propionate preventer to 250 mcg BD and using salbutamol 6 puffs via MDI and spacer every 2-3 hours. On arrival, he is unable to complete sentences in one breath, speaking only 2-3 words at a time. He is sitting upright with marked suprasternal and intercostal recession, and audible expiratory wheeze. His RR is 42, HR is 135 bpm, and SpO2 is 90% on room air. His PEFR is unmeasurable due to poor technique.

data_table.Vital signs: RR 42, HR 135 bpm, SpO2 90% RA, Temp 37.2°C, BP 105/65 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Unable to complete sentences (2-3 words), sitting upright, marked suprasternal and intercostal recession, audible expiratory wheeze, PEFR unmeasurable
data_table.Investigations: Nil performed at this stage
data_table.Medications: Fluticasone propionate 125 mcg BD (usual); Salbutamol 6 puffs via MDI + spacer given by mother prior to arrival

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management in the emergency department?

options.text: Salbutamol 12 puffs (100 mcg/puff) via MDI/spacer with ipratropium 4 puffs (21 mcg/puff) via MDI/spacer, plus oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg immediately
options.rationale: MDI-spacer is acceptable delivery but severe/life-threatening asthma (SpO2 90, unable to speak, marked recession) requires OXYGEN-DRIVEN nebuliser. Would be correct only in mild-moderate asthma (Source: RCH CPG: Acute Asthma).

options.text: Hudson mask oxygen 8 L/min to SpO2 >94%, nebulised salbutamol 5 mg plus ipratropium 250 mcg by O2-driven nebuliser, oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg, repeat 20 min
options.rationale: This child has severe acute asthma (SpO2 90%, unable to speak in sentences, marked recession). Management includes: oxygen to maintain SpO2 ≥94%, oxygen-driven nebulised salbutamol (5 mg for >5 years) plus ipratropium (250 mcg) for the first hour (3 doses at 20-minute intervals), and oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg (max 50 mg). Continuous clinical assessment is essential. If there is inadequate response after the first hour, escalate to IV magnesium sulphate, IV salbutamol, or aminophylline, and involve ICU. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook; RCH CPG: Acute Asthma)

options.text: High-flow nasal prong oxygen at 2 L/kg/min, IV hydrocortisone 4 mg/kg, urgent chest X-ray to exclude pneumonia before any bronchodilator therapy commences now
options.rationale: IV hydrocortisone is alternative not superior to oral pred; bronchodilator must NOT be delayed for CXR which is not routine. Would be correct only if oral not tolerated (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: IV magnesium sulphate 50 mg/kg over 20 min as first-line, oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg, nasal prong O2 at 2 L/min to maintain SpO2 >92%
options.rationale: IV magnesium is SECOND-line after failed initial inhaled therapy. Would be correct as second-line after failure (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Nebulised salbutamol 2.5 mg alone via O2-driven nebuliser, oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg, observe one hour, then add ipratropium and consider IV therapy
options.rationale: Salbutamol 2.5 mg is for 2-5 years; a 7-year-old requires 5 mg; ipratropium must be ADDED from start in severe disease. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: This 7-year-old has acute severe (bordering on life-threatening) asthma: SpO2 90%, unable to speak in sentences, marked recession, and audible wheeze. The immediate management priorities are: oxygen to target SpO2 ≥94%, oxygen-driven nebulised salbutamol 5 mg plus ipratropium 250 mcg repeated every 20 minutes for the first hour (3 doses), and oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg. Continuous assessment of response is essential. If inadequate response after the first hour, escalation to IV magnesium sulphate 50 mg/kg, IV salbutamol infusion, or aminophylline infusion, and ICU consultation is warranted. Chest X-ray is not routinely indicated.
key_points: Classification of acute asthma severity: mild (SpO2 >92%, sentences), moderate (SpO2 >92%, phrases), severe (SpO2 <92%, words, recession), life-threatening (SpO2 <92% with silent chest, cyanosis, exhaustion). (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook)
