First-line for severe asthma: oxygen, nebulised salbutamol (5 mg if >5 yrs, 2.5 mg if 2-5 yrs) PLUS ipratropium 250 mcg, repeated every 20 min for first hour, and oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg. (Source: RCH CPG: Acute Asthma)
Ipratropium added to salbutamol in the first hour significantly reduces hospitalisation in children with moderate-severe asthma. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook)
IV magnesium sulphate 50 mg/kg (max 2 g) is second-line for severe asthma unresponsive to initial inhaled therapy. (Source: RCH CPG: Acute Asthma)
explanation.pearls: In severe paediatric asthma, a silent chest is more dangerous than a wheezy chest. A silent chest with SpO2 <90% is life-threatening asthma - call a code blue, prepare for intubation, and give IV salbutamol and magnesium.

stem: A 9-month-old boy presents with a 12-hour history of episodic severe abdominal pain. His mother describes him drawing his knees up to his chest, screaming, and then becoming pale and lethargic for 10-15 minutes between episodes. He has vomited several times, initially milk and later bile-stained fluid. He passed a small amount of dark red stool mixed with mucus an hour ago. On examination he appears pale and lethargic. His abdomen is distended with a vague sausage-shaped mass palpable in the right upper quadrant. Bowel sounds are high-pitched. His heart rate is 160 bpm, temperature 37.5°C, and capillary refill time 3 seconds. He has not received rotavirus vaccine.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 160 bpm, Temp 37.5°C, RR 40, CRT 3 sec, BP 85/50 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Pale, lethargic, distended abdomen, sausage-shaped mass RUQ, high-pitched bowel sounds, currant jelly stool described
data_table.Investigations: None performed yet

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial investigation to confirm the diagnosis?

options.text: Abdominal X-ray (supine and erect)
options.rationale: AXR may show obstruction or target sign but is neither sensitive nor specific enough; normal XR does not rule out intussusception. Would be correct only as adjunct after US (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception).

options.text: Abdominal ultrasound
options.rationale: Ultrasound is the first-line imaging study for suspected intussusception. It has a sensitivity and specificity of >97% and can identify the classic 'target sign' or 'pseudokidney sign'. It is rapid, non-invasive, and does not involve radiation. Confirmed intussusception is then managed with non-surgical reduction (air or contrast enema) if no perforation. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)

options.text: Contrast enema (barium or water-soluble)
options.rationale: Contrast enema is diagnostic and therapeutic but US should be performed first to confirm diagnosis and exclude perforation. Would be correct after US (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: CT abdomen with intravenous contrast
options.rationale: CT delivers significant radiation and is not first-line; US is preferred. Would be correct only with US non-diagnostic (Source: RANZCR).

options.text: Diagnostic laparoscopy
options.rationale: Surgery is for failed enema reduction, perforation, or instability; not diagnostic modality. Would be correct only after failed enema or perforation (Source: RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: This infant presents with classic intussusception: episodic severe colicky pain with drawing up of knees, bile-stained vomiting, currant jelly stool, and a palpable sausage-shaped abdominal mass. The initial investigation of choice is abdominal ultrasound, which is highly sensitive and specific. Abdominal X-ray may show signs but cannot exclude intussusception. If ultrasound confirms intussusception and there is no perforation, non-surgical reduction (air or contrast enema) is the next step. CT and laparoscopy are not first-line.
key_points: The classic triad of intussusception (colicky pain, currant jelly stool, palpable mass) is present in <50% of cases; high index of suspicion is needed. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)
Ultrasound is the diagnostic modality of choice with sensitivity and specificity >97%. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)
After ultrasound confirmation, non-surgical reduction (air enema preferred) is attempted if there are no signs of peritonitis or perforation. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)
Rotavirus vaccination is associated with a small increased risk of intussusception (primarily in the first week after vaccination). (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)
explanation.pearls: The 'sausage-shaped mass' of intussusception is typically in the right upper quadrant, with an empty right lower quadrant (Dance sign). Always check for this in an infant with colicky abdominal pain.

stem: A 3-year-old boy is brought to the GP with a 1-week history of progressive periorbital swelling, most noticeable in the mornings. His mother has also noticed his clothes becoming tighter and his shoes not fitting. He has had a mild upper respiratory tract infection 2 weeks ago. Urinalysis in the clinic shows 4+ protein and no blood. On examination he has periorbital oedema and pitting oedema of both legs to the knees. His blood pressure is 95/55 mmHg (50th centile). His weight is 19 kg (previously 16 kg at his last well-child check 6 months ago). He has no rash, his abdomen is distended with a fluid thrill, and his testes are normal.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 95/55 mmHg (50th centile), HR 100 bpm, RR 24
data_table.Examination: Periorbital oedema, pitting oedema to knees, abdominal distension with fluid thrill, weight gain 3 kg, normal BP, no rash, normal testes
data_table.Investigations: Urine dipstick: 4+ protein, no blood

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.text: Refer to paediatric nephrology for renal biopsy to confirm the histological subtype before commencing any therapy
options.rationale: Biopsy not required in classic nephrotic (1-10 years, oedema, heavy proteinuria, normal BP, no macroscopic haematuria). Would be correct only with atypical features (Source: KDIGO 2021).

options.text: Oral prednisolone 60 mg/m2/day (about 40 mg daily) for 4 weeks, then 40 mg/m2 alternate days for 4 weeks, with low-salt diet
options.rationale: The International Study of Kidney Disease in Children (ISKDC) regimen is the standard first-line therapy for childhood nephrotic syndrome: prednisolone 60 mg/m²/day (max 60 mg) in divided doses for 4 weeks, followed by 40 mg/m² on alternate days for 4 weeks, then taper. A low-salt diet is advised. This child's weight is 19 kg, body surface area approximately 0.8 m², so 60 mg/m² ≈ 48 mg, but guidelines often use 60 mg/m². The ISKDC regimen is endorsed by RCH. (Source: RCH CPG: Nephrotic Syndrome; ISKDC Regimen)

options.text: Oral furosemide twice daily with strict 800 mL/day fluid restriction to manage the oedema, no corticosteroid therapy
options.rationale: Diuretics alone do not treat the underlying disease and may cause electrolyte disturbance/hypovolaemia. Would be correct as adjunct for severe oedema (Source: KDIGO 2021).

options.text: IV 20% albumin over 4 hours followed by IV furosemide, then start oral prednisolone once oedema resolves
options.rationale: IV albumin and frusemide are for severe symptomatic oedema (scrotal, respiratory compromise, ascites); this child has manageable oedema. Would be correct only with severe oedema (Source: KDIGO 2021).

options.text: Oral enalapril daily to reduce proteinuria and observe for spontaneous resolution as likely post-infectious glomerulonephritis
options.rationale: ACEi is for persistent proteinuria in steroid-resistant NS, not first-line initial. Would be correct only after steroid resistance documented (Source: KDIGO 2021).

explanation.summary: This preschool-aged child has classic nephrotic syndrome: heavy proteinuria (4+), generalised oedema, ascites, weight gain, normal blood pressure, no haematuria. The most likely histology is minimal change disease (>80% in this age group). The standard of care is empiric corticosteroid therapy using the ISKDC regimen: prednisolone 60 mg/m²/day for 4 weeks, followed by 40 mg/m² alternate days for 4 weeks, with a low-salt diet. Renal biopsy is not required upfront. Diuretics and IV albumin are reserved for severe or complicated oedema. ACE inhibitors are not first-line.
key_points: Childhood nephrotic syndrome: most common 1-10 years, male predominance, minimal change disease in >80%. (Source: RCH CPG: Nephrotic Syndrome)
First-line treatment is prednisolone 60 mg/m²/day for 4 weeks, then 40 mg/m² alternate days for 4 weeks (ISKDC regimen). (Source: RCH CPG: Nephrotic Syndrome)
Renal biopsy is not required before steroid therapy in typical childhood nephrotic syndrome. (Source: RCH CPG: Nephrotic Syndrome)
Daily urine protein dipstick monitoring at home is important to assess response and detect relapse. (Source: RCH CPG: Nephrotic Syndrome)
explanation.pearls: The child with nephrotic syndrome is immunocompromised even before steroids are started. Ensure immunisations are up to date (non-live vaccines only if on high-dose steroids) and counsel about infection risk, especially spontaneous bacterial peritonitis.

stem: An 8-year-old girl presents with 2 weeks of polyuria, polydipsia and weight loss and 24 hours of vomiting, abdominal pain and drowsiness. She is thin, dehydrated and responds only to pain (GCS 11, E2V3M6). She has Kussmaul breathing with RR 36, HR 140, BP 90/55, CRT 3 seconds and very dry mucous membranes. Weight 24 kg (estimated pre-illness 28 kg). VBG: pH 7.05, pCO2 18, HCO3 5. Glucose 28 mmol/L. Ketones over 6 mmol/L. Na 130, K 5.2, urea 10.5, creatinine 90. There is no other significant history.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 140 bpm, BP 90/55 mmHg, RR 36 (Kussmaul), GCS 11, CRT 3 sec, Temp 36.5°C
data_table.Examination: Thin, severe dehydration (~14% weight loss), drowsy, Kussmaul breathing, very dry mucous membranes
data_table.Investigations: VBG: pH 7.05, pCO₂ 18, HCO₃⁻ 5; Glucose 28 mmol/L; Ketones >6 mmol/L; Na⁺ 130, K⁺ 5.2, Urea 10.5, Cr 90

lead_in: Which initial intravenous fluid plan?

options.text: 20 mL/kg bolus of 0.9% saline over 15 minutes, repeat once if still in shock, then maintenance plus deficit replacement at 10 mL/kg/hour using 0.45% saline with potassium
options.rationale: 0.45 percent saline NOT used in initial paediatric DKA (cerebral oedema risk from rapid osmolar shifts); 0.9 percent saline with K is recommended and deficit replaced over 48 h. Would never be correct (Source: ISPAD; RCH CPG).

options.text: 10 mL/kg bolus of 0.9% saline over 30 minutes, then calculate fluid deficit (about 7-10 percent of body weight cap) and replace half the deficit plus 24 hours of maintenance in the first 24 hours and the remainder over the next 24 hours, using 0.9% saline plus 40 mmol/L potassium once urine output is confirmed; start insulin at 0.05-0.1 units/kg/hour at least 1 hour after fluids begin; monitor for cerebral oedema
options.rationale: RCH and ISPAD paediatric DKA management uses a 10 mL/kg 0.9% saline bolus over 30-60 minutes (repeat once if shock; AU practice caps total resuscitation boluses to avoid cerebral oedema), then deficit-plus-maintenance over 48 hours with 0.9% saline and potassium once UOP confirmed. Insulin is started no earlier than 1 hour after fluids at 0.05-0.1 units/kg/hour. Cerebral oedema surveillance is mandatory.

options.text: Rapid 40 mL/kg bolus of 0.9% saline over 1 hour to restore circulating volume, then replace deficit over 24 hours
options.rationale: Large rapid boluses increase cerebral oedema risk; deficit is replaced over 48 h, not 24 h. Would never be correct (Source: ISPAD).

options.text: Commence IV insulin 0.1 units/kg/hour immediately before any fluid bolus, with maintenance 0.9% saline and 5% dextrose
options.rationale: Fluid resuscitation precedes insulin; insulin within first hour increases CV collapse and cerebral oedema risk. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: IV 0.9% saline at maintenance rate plus a 10 mL/kg bolus, with IV sodium bicarbonate 1 mmol/kg to correct the acidosis
options.rationale: Bicarbonate is not routinely recommended in paediatric DKA; may worsen intracellular acidosis and cerebral oedema risk. Would be correct only in life-threatening hyperkalaemia or pH under 6.9 (Source: ISPAD).

explanation.summary: This child has severe DKA (pH 7.05, bicarbonate 5, ketones >6, GCS 11) with ~14% dehydration. Paediatric DKA fluid management focuses on preventing cerebral oedema. The recommended regimen: initial 10 mL/kg 0.9% NaCl bolus over 30-60 minutes (repeat once if shocked). The total deficit is calculated and replaced evenly over 48 hours (50% deficit + maintenance in first 24h, 50% in second 24h) using 0.9% NaCl with added potassium (40 mmol/L) once urine output is confirmed. Insulin is commenced 1-2 hours after fluid resuscitation begins. Bicarbonate is not routinely used.
key_points: Paediatric DKA rehydration: replace deficit evenly over 48 hours using 0.9% NaCl, to minimise cerebral oedema risk. (Source: RCH CPG: Diabetic Ketoacidosis; ISPAD Guidelines)
Initial fluid bolus: 10 mL/kg 0.9% NaCl over 30-60 min, repeat once if shock. Avoid rapid large-volume boluses. (Source: RCH CPG: Diabetic Ketoacidosis)
Insulin infusion (0.05-0.1 units/kg/hr) is started 1-2 hours after commencing fluids. (Source: RCH CPG: Diabetic Ketoacidosis)
Sodium bicarbonate is NOT routinely recommended in DKA; it increases the risk of cerebral oedema. (Source: ISPAD Guidelines 2022)
Cerebral oedema is the leading cause of death in paediatric DKA. Warning signs: headache, bradycardia, hypertension, altered consciousness. (Source: RCH CPG: Diabetic Ketoacidosis)
explanation.pearls: In paediatric DKA, 'slow and steady wins the race': cerebral oedema typically occurs 4-12 hours after treatment begins, not at presentation. The fluid calculation should be meticulous, and neurological observations (including GCS) should be performed hourly.

stem: A 2-year-old girl presents to the emergency department with a 6-hour history of high fever, irritability, and vomiting. Her parents report she has been difficult to rouse for the past hour and has developed a new rash on her legs that does not fade when pressed with a glass. On examination she is drowsy, responding only to painful stimuli (GCS 9). She is tachycardic at 170 bpm, hypotensive with BP 70/40 mmHg, capillary refill time 5 seconds, and peripheries are cold and mottled. There is a purpuric rash over her lower limbs and buttocks, with several lesions >2 mm. She has neck stiffness but no photophobia. Her temperature is 39.8°C.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 39.8°C, HR 170 bpm, BP 70/40 mmHg, RR 40, SpO₂ 94%, GCS 9, CRT 5 sec, cold mottled peripheries
data_table.Examination: Drowsy, purpuric rash lower limbs/buttocks (non-blanching, >2 mm), neck stiffness, no focal neurology
data_table.Investigations: None performed yet
data_table.Medications: Fully immunised including MenB and MenACWY

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate next step?

options.text: Perform a lumbar puncture and blood cultures before antibiotics, then commence IV benzylpenicillin 4-hourly
options.rationale: LP is contraindicated in haemodynamically unstable patients with suspected meningococcal disease; risks herniation and delays life-saving treatment. Would be correct only after stabilisation in stable patient (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Administer IV ceftriaxone immediately (max 2 g), take blood cultures, and commence IV fluid resuscitation with 0.9% sodium chloride 20 mL/kg bolus repeated as needed
options.rationale: Suspected meningococcal septicaemia with shock is a medical emergency. Immediate actions: IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone 100 mg/kg, max 2 g, or cefotaxime 50 mg/kg) within minutes of recognition, blood cultures (ideally before antibiotics but do not delay), and aggressive IV fluid resuscitation (20 mL/kg 0.9% NaCl bolus, repeat up to 60 mL/kg). LP is deferred. The patient must be isolated with droplet precautions. (Source: RCH CPG: Meningococcal Disease; Therapeutic Guidelines: Meningitis)

options.text: Obtain an urgent CT brain to exclude cerebral oedema before lumbar puncture, then perform LP and start IV antibiotics
options.rationale: CT before LP is for focal neuro signs, papilloedema, or GCS under 9; immediate priority here is antibiotics and resuscitation. Would be correct only with focal neuro signs in stable patient (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Administer IV dexamethasone 20 minutes before antibiotics, then give IV ceftriaxone and IV fluids, to reduce the risk of neurological sequelae
options.rationale: Adjunctive dexamethasone is for confirmed Hib/pneumococcal meningitis given before/with first antibiotic; in shocked patients antibiotics and resuscitation take precedence. Would be correct only in stable patient with confirmed bacterial meningitis (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Notify the public health unit and await their advice before administering antibiotics, as this presentation is a notifiable condition requiring coordinated public health intervention
options.rationale: Notification is urgent but must NOT delay treatment; antibiotics first, notification in parallel. Would never be correct (Source: CDNA IMD).

explanation.summary: This child has meningococcal septicaemia with septic shock: purpuric rash, fever, shock (tachycardia, hypotension, prolonged CRT, mottled skin), and reduced consciousness. The priority is immediate IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone 100 mg/kg) and aggressive fluid resuscitation (20 mL/kg 0.9% NaCl boluses). Blood cultures should be taken but must not delay antibiotics. LP and neuroimaging are deferred in haemodynamically unstable patients. Dexamethasone may be considered for bacterial meningitis but is secondary to resuscitation. Notification occurs concurrently.
key_points: Meningococcal septicaemia with shock: give IV ceftriaxone 100 mg/kg (max 2 g) immediately, within minutes of recognition. (Source: RCH CPG: Meningococcal Disease)
Fluid resuscitation: 20 mL/kg 0.9% NaCl bolus, repeat up to 60 mL/kg, with early involvement of ICU. (Source: RCH CPG: Meningococcal Disease)
LP is contraindicated in shock, raised ICP, coagulopathy, or respiratory compromise. Do not delay antibiotics. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Meningitis)
Meningococcal disease is urgently notifiable to the local public health unit. Prophylaxis (rifampicin, ciprofloxacin, or ceftriaxone) is recommended for close contacts. (Source: CDNA Guidelines)
explanation.pearls: The glass test (tumbler test) is a useful bedside tool: press a clear glass against the rash; if it does not blanch, it is purpuric and meningococcal disease should be considered. However, a positive test is not required for action - treat on clinical suspicion.

stem: A 2-week-old male infant is brought to the emergency department by ambulance after his parents witnessed him stop breathing, become pale, and go limp while sleeping. The episode lasted approximately 20 seconds and resolved spontaneously when his mother picked him up and rubbed his back. He had been feeding well prior to the event and has not had any similar episodes. He was born at 35+2 weeks gestation and spent 1 week in the special care nursery for feeding difficulties and mild respiratory distress, from which he recovered fully. He has been home for 1 week. On examination, he is alert, active, and feeding normally. His observations are: temperature 36.8°C, heart rate 140 bpm, respiratory rate 38, SpO₂ 98% on room air. There are no dysmorphic features, and the neurological examination is normal. His weight is 2.6 kg (birth weight 2.5 kg). The infant is currently in the emergency department short stay unit and has been observed for 2 hours with no further events.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 36.8°C, HR 140 bpm, RR 38, SpO₂ 98% RA, BP 70/40 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Alert, active, normal neurology, no dysmorphism, well hydrated, feeding normally
data_table.Investigations: None performed yet. Parents describe a 20-second apnoeic, pale, limp episode resolved spontaneously with tactile stimulation.

lead_in: According to Australian guidelines, what is the most appropriate disposition for this infant?

options.text: Discharge home with a home apnoea monitor, education on safe sleeping and CPR, and arrange a next-day follow-up with the GP and maternal child health nurse
options.rationale: Infant is HIGH RISK (under 60 days, preterm born, BRUE); home monitor and outpatient referral are inadequate. Would never be correct in high-risk BRUE (Source: AAP BRUE Guideline).

options.text: Admit to hospital for cardiorespiratory monitoring for a minimum of 24 hours, with a 12-lead ECG, and consider pertussis PCR and other investigations only if clinically indicated
options.rationale: This infant meets high-risk BRUE criteria: age <60 days, born preterm (<37 weeks), and the event required tactile stimulation to terminate. Current guidelines recommend admission for observation and cardiorespiratory monitoring for at least 24 hours, a 12-lead ECG to exclude prolonged QT, and consideration of pertussis PCR (given age and potential for apnoeic presentation). Extensive investigations (EEG, neuroimaging, pH study) are not routinely indicated without specific clinical clues. (Source: RCH CPG: Apparent Life Threatening Event; AAP BRUE Guideline 2016)

options.text: Perform a full septic workup including lumbar puncture, commence empiric IV antibiotics, and admit to the neonatal unit for a minimum 48 hours of observation
options.rationale: Full septic workup is for clinical signs of sepsis or maternal risk factors; this infant is well-appearing. Would be correct only with sepsis features (Source: RCH CPG: BRUE).

options.text: Arrange an urgent outpatient polysomnography (sleep study) and reflux study, and discharge home with strict return precautions and a nasal cannula oxygen setup
options.rationale: Polysomnography not first-line for BRUE; may be considered for recurrent/severe events. Discharging high-risk infant home is unsafe. Would be correct as outpatient adjunct in recurrent BRUE (Source: AAP).

options.text: Refer to child protection services immediately as a BRUE in a preterm infant is highly suspicious for non-accidental injury, and admit for a skeletal survey and ophthalmology review
options.rationale: Single BRUE in preterm infant with plausible history does not automatically require child protection referral. Would be correct only with safeguarding red flags (Source: RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: This preterm (35+2 weeks), 2-week-old infant has experienced a BRUE (brief resolved unexplained event) characterised by apnoea, pallor, and limpness requiring tactile stimulation. He is classified as HIGH RISK because of age <60 days, prematurity, and the need for intervention. Current RCH and AAP guidelines recommend admission for cardiorespiratory monitoring (minimum 24 hours), a 12-lead ECG (to exclude prolonged QT syndrome), and consideration of pertussis PCR (apnoea is a known presentation in neonates). Extensive investigations are guided by clinical suspicion, not routine. Home monitoring is not recommended. Child protection referral is not automatic but should be considered if red flags are present.
key_points: BRUE definition: brief (<1 min) apnoea, colour change, altered tone, or choking, with full recovery to baseline. (Source: AAP BRUE Guideline 2016)
HIGH-risk features: age <60 days, preterm <37 weeks, multiple events, CPR required, abnormal examination. These warrant admission and monitoring. (Source: RCH CPG: BRUE; AAP Guideline)

Recommended investigations for high-risk BRUE: 12-lead ECG (prolonged QT), pertussis PCR (if respiratory symptoms), and observation for at least 24 hours. EEG, neuroimaging, and reflux studies are not routine. (Source: RCH CPG: BRUE)
Home apnoea monitors are NOT recommended; they do not reduce SIDS risk and may increase parental anxiety. (Source: AAP BRUE Guideline)
explanation.pearls: The term 'ALTE' has been replaced by 'BRUE' to better reflect the benign nature of most events when a cause is not identified. However, the 'U' stands for 'Unexplained' - always ensure the event is truly unexplained before applying the BRUE label.

stem: A 3-year-old boy with previous mild intermittent croup presents at 2200 with a barking cough and stridor at rest. He has moderate subcostal and suprasternal recessions. SpO2 93 percent on room air, HR 130, RR 32, T 37.4 deg C. The clinician calculates a Westley score of 4 (moderate). He is given oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg and nebulised adrenaline 5 mL of 1:1000. Within 30 minutes his stridor at rest resolves and he looks comfortable. He has been observed for 1 hour. The ED registrar is now considering disposition.

data_table.Vital signs: SpO₂ 93% initially, HR 130 bpm, RR 32
data_table.Examination: Stridor at rest, moderate recessions, improved after adrenaline
data_table.Investigations: None

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate minimum observation period after the LAST adrenaline dose, and what condition supports safe discharge?

options.text: 1 hour after the LAST adrenaline dose, then discharge if stridor-free at rest with no recessions or increased work of breathing on examination
options.rationale: Insufficient to capture rebound; adrenaline lasts ~2 h and rebound can occur after that. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: Croup).

options.text: 2 hours after the LAST adrenaline dose, then discharge if stridor-free at rest with no recessions or increased work of breathing on examination
options.rationale: Adrenaline effect lasts up to 2 h and rebound can occur 1-2 h after that, so 2 h is insufficient. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Minimum 4 hours after the LAST adrenaline with the child stridor-free at rest, no recessions, comfortable on room air, tolerating oral fluids; written safety-net advice before discharge
options.rationale: Australian guidance (RCH CPG: Croup, PREDICT) is a minimum 4-hour observation after the LAST adrenaline dose; discharge requires the child to be stridor-free at rest, without recessions, comfortable on room air, tolerating oral intake, and with safe representation arrangements and written safety-net advice.

options.text: 6 hours unconditionally after the LAST adrenaline dose, regardless of clinical status or work of breathing at the time of planned discharge today
options.rationale: Six hours is sometimes used with clinical concern but is not the standard minimum requirement. Would be correct only with persistent clinical concern (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: No specific observation period required; discharge once stridor-free at rest regardless of time since the last adrenaline dose or current state
options.rationale: Without adequate observation, rebound stridor can occur at home; observation is required. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: Nebulised adrenaline provides temporary relief of croup symptoms for 2-4 hours. Rebound stridor can occur as the effect wanes. Therefore, all children receiving adrenaline must be observed for at least 4 hours post-administration to ensure they remain stridor-free before discharge.
key_points: Nebulised adrenaline duration of action is 2-4 hours. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)
Minimum observation after last dose: 4 hours. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)
If stridor recurs within the observation period, repeat adrenaline and reset the 4-hour clock. (Source: PREDICT Guideline: Croup)
explanation.pearls: After nebulised adrenaline, the child may look perfect within minutes – but don't be fooled; the clock starts when the drug wears off, not when it works.

stem: A 6-year-old boy with known asthma presents with acute severe exacerbation. He has received 3 doses of nebulised salbutamol (5 mg) and ipratropium (250 mcg) over 1 hour, plus oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg. His SpO₂ remains 89% on high-flow oxygen, he speaks in single words, and has severe recessions. Chest is tight, and air entry is reduced. What is the most appropriate next step?

data_table.Vital signs: SpO₂ 89% on O₂ 15 L/min, HR 150 bpm, RR 40
data_table.Examination: Single words, severe recessions, reduced air entry bilaterally
data_table.Investigations: None
data_table.Medications: Nebulised salbutamol/ipratropium x3, oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Administer IV magnesium sulphate (max 2 g) over 20 minutes
options.rationale: For severe acute asthma unresponsive to initial inhaled bronchodilators and steroids, IV magnesium sulphate is the recommended second-line therapy. It acts as a smooth muscle relaxant and may prevent intubation. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook; RCH CPG: Acute Asthma)

options.text: Commence IV salbutamol infusion and transfer to ICU
options.rationale: IV salbutamol is third-line after magnesium failure or in life-threatening asthma; magnesium tried first. Would be correct as third-line (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook).

options.text: Repeat the nebulised salbutamol and ipratropium every 20 minutes for another hour
options.rationale: After 3 doses without improvement, further inhaled therapy alone is unlikely sufficient; escalation needed. Would be correct only with early response (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook).

options.text: Give an IV fluid bolus of 20 mL/kg and reassess
options.rationale: IV fluids may be given for hydration but will not relieve bronchospasm; magnesium is the specific bronchodilator. Would be correct as adjunct, not next step (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook).

options.text: Obtain a chest X-ray to exclude pneumothorax
options.rationale: Pneumothorax is differential but immediate priority is escalation of bronchodilator therapy; CXR can follow stabilisation. Would be correct only with high pneumothorax suspicion (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook).

explanation.summary: This child has severe life-threatening asthma not responding to maximal inhaled therapy and steroids. The next step is IV magnesium sulphate 50 mg/kg, which has evidence for reducing hospitalisation and preventing intubation. IV salbutamol is reserved for refractory cases. Chest X-ray and fluids are secondary.
key_points: IV magnesium sulphate 50 mg/kg (max 2 g) is second-line for severe acute asthma. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook)
IV salbutamol or aminophylline are third-line. (Source: RCH CPG: Acute Asthma)
Indications for escalation include persistent severe respiratory distress, SpO₂ <90% despite oxygen, and failure to improve after initial bronchodilator therapy. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook)
explanation.pearls: In severe asthma, if the patient is tiring, a silent chest is an ominous sign – call ICU immediately. Magnesium is not a substitute for intubation when it is needed.

stem: A term infant is born at 39+2 weeks by vaginal delivery to a mother who had a temperature of 38.2°C during labour, with ruptured membranes for 20 hours. GBS screening at 36 weeks was negative. Intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis (benzylpenicillin) was commenced only 2 hours before delivery. The infant appears well at birth, Apgars 9 and 9, and has remained asymptomatic for 6 hours in the postnatal ward. What is the most appropriate management plan?

data_table.Vital signs: HR 140 bpm, RR 45, Temp 36.9°C, SpO₂ 98%
data_table.Examination: Alert, normal tone, no respiratory distress, normal perfusion
data_table.Investigations: Maternal temp 38.2°C, ROM 20 hr, GBS negative, IAP given 2 hr before delivery

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management plan for this infant?

options.text: Observe in hospital for 24-48 hours with regular clinical assessments; no antibiotics or bloods required
options.rationale: Maternal intrapartum fever 38+ and prolonged ROM are EOS risk factors even with negative GBS; observation alone is insufficient. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: EOS).

options.text: Perform a full septic workup including blood culture, FBE, CRP, and lumbar puncture, and commence IV antibiotics
options.rationale: LP is not routine for EOS in well-appearing infants without meningeal signs; blood culture and CRP are appropriate. Would be correct only with meningeal signs (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Perform blood culture, FBE, and CRP at 6-12 hours of age; if CRP is normal and cultures negative at 48 hours, discontinue antibiotics if started, with close observation
options.rationale: Reasonable but maternal fever and prolonged ROM with negative GBS increases risk of non-GBS organisms; many guidelines recommend empirical therapy. Would be correct only without maternal fever (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Commence IV benzylpenicillin and gentamicin empirically after taking blood culture and CRP, monitor clinically, and review at 36-48 hours; discontinue antibiotics if cultures negative, CRP normal, and infant remains well
options.rationale: Maternal intrapartum fever (≥38°C) is a major risk factor for neonatal sepsis, independent of GBS status. The incomplete IAP duration (<4 hours) further increases risk. The RCH guideline recommends empiric antibiotics for infants with one or more major risk factors. Blood culture, CRP, and a septic screen (excluding LP if asymptomatic) are performed, and antibiotics continued until cultures are negative for 48 hours. (Source: RCH CPG: Early-Onset Neonatal Sepsis)

options.text: Observe for 12 hours; if the infant remains well, discharge home at 24 hours with an early follow-up by the maternal child health nurse
options.rationale: Early discharge with observation only is not recommended given maternal fever; infant is moderate risk. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: The key risk factor here is maternal intrapartum fever (≥38°C), which is a major risk for neonatal sepsis, along with prolonged rupture of membranes. Even with negative GBS, the infant should be investigated with blood culture, FBE, CRP, and started on empiric antibiotics (benzylpenicillin + gentamicin) until cultures are negative at 48 hours. Observation alone is insufficient because the risk of non-GBS organisms (e.g., E. coli, other streptococci) is significant. The incomplete IAP (<4 hours prior to delivery) also means the infant did not receive full protection.
key_points: Maternal intrapartum fever (≥38°C) is an independent risk factor for early-onset neonatal sepsis, requiring investigation and empiric treatment. (Source: RCH CPG: Early-Onset Neonatal Sepsis)
Empiric antibiotics: benzylpenicillin (60 mg/kg IV 12-hourly) + gentamicin (5 mg/kg IV 36-hourly) after blood culture. (Source: RCH CPG: Early-Onset Neonatal Sepsis)
Lumbar puncture is not routinely indicated in asymptomatic infants unless there is a positive blood culture or clinical suspicion of meningitis. (Source: RCH CPG: Early-Onset Neonatal Sepsis)
Observation alone without antibiotics is not recommended when one or more major risk factors are present. (Source: RCH CPG: Early-Onset Neonatal Sepsis)
explanation.pearls: Maternal chorioamnionitis (fever plus one other sign) often has polymicrobial origin; always cover with broad-spectrum antibiotics until cultures confirm the organism.

stem: A 6-year-old boy presents with a 3-day history of a palpable purpuric rash on his lower limbs and buttocks, diffuse colicky abdominal pain, and swelling of both knees and ankles. On examination, he is afebrile, HR 90 bpm, BP 110/70 mmHg (90th centile for age). The rash is non-blanching and palpable. His abdomen is tender but soft. Urinalysis shows 3+ protein and 2+ blood on dipstick. A spot urine protein-to-creatinine ratio (UPCR) is 250 mg/mmol (normal <20). Serum creatinine is 45 µmol/L, and albumin is 32 g/L. What is the most appropriate next step in management of his renal involvement?

data_table.Vital signs: BP 110/70 (90th centile), HR 90, Temp 36.8°C
data_table.Examination: Palpable purpuric rash lower limbs/buttocks, swollen knees and ankles, tender abdomen, mild periorbital oedema
data_table.Investigations: Urine: 3+ protein, 2+ blood; UPCR 250 mg/mmol; serum Cr 45, albumin 32 g/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Refer to paediatric nephrology for consideration of renal biopsy and possible corticosteroid or immunosuppressive therapy, as he has nephrotic-range proteinuria
options.rationale: Nephrotic-range proteinuria (UPCR >200 mg/mmol or >1 g/m²/day) in the setting of HSP indicates severe glomerulonephritis and warrants referral to paediatric nephrology. A renal biopsy is often performed to assess the degree of crescent formation, which guides immunosuppressive therapy (corticosteroids, cyclophosphamide, or mycophenolate). This is beyond primary care management. (Source: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schönlein Purpura; KDIGO Glomerulonephritis Guidelines)

options.text: Commence oral prednisolone daily for 4 weeks to treat the nephritis and prevent long-term renal impairment
options.rationale: Oral prednisolone alone has not been shown to prevent or treat established HSP nephritis; may be used for severe abdominal pain or joint symptoms. Would be correct only for severe abdominal pain (Source: KDIGO).

options.text: Discharge home with advice to monitor urine dipstick weekly for 6 weeks, as most cases of this nephritis resolve spontaneously without treatment
options.rationale: Mild urinary abnormalities may be monitored but nephrotic-range proteinuria requires specialist referral and investigation. Would be correct only with mild proteinuria (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Start an angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitor (enalapril) to reduce proteinuria and control blood pressure
options.rationale: ACEi is used for persistent proteinuria in CKD; in acute HSP nephritis with nephrotic-range proteinuria, it is adjunct, not primary. Would be correct as adjunct after specialist review (Source: KDIGO).

options.text: Admit for intravenous methylprednisolone pulse therapy daily for 3 days, as severe nephritis in this setting requires immediate high-dose steroids
options.rationale: IV methylprednisolone may be used after biopsy confirms crescentic nephritis but is not the first step; first is nephrology referral and biopsy. Would be correct only after biopsy confirms crescentic disease (Source: KDIGO).

explanation.summary: This child has HSP (IgA vasculitis) with nephrotic-range proteinuria (UPCR 250 mg/mmol), which is classified as severe nephritis. The appropriate next step is urgent referral to paediatric nephrology for consideration of renal biopsy. Biopsy guides immunosuppressive therapy (corticosteroids with cyclophosphamide or mycophenolate) if significant crescent formation is present. Mild cases without nephrotic-range proteinuria can be monitored, but severe cases require specialist care to prevent chronic kidney disease.
key_points: HSP nephritis with nephrotic-range proteinuria (>200 mg/mmol UPCR) requires paediatric nephrology referral and renal biopsy. (Source: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schönlein Purpura; KDIGO Guidelines)
Renal biopsy is indicated for persistent nephrotic-range proteinuria, renal impairment, or hypertension. (Source: KDIGO Glomerulonephritis Guidelines)
Corticosteroids alone do not prevent HSP nephritis; immunosuppression is reserved for biopsy-proven crescentic disease. (Source: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schönlein Purpura)
Urine dipstick and blood pressure monitoring are essential during and after HSP for early detection of renal involvement. (Source: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schönlein Purpura)
explanation.pearls: In HSP, the severity of the rash does not predict the severity of renal involvement. A child with a few spots can have severe nephritis, while a child with widespread purpura may have normal urine.

stem: A 12-year-old girl with known cystic fibrosis (ΔF508 homozygous) presents with a 2-day history of crampy abdominal pain, distension, and bilious vomiting. She has not opened her bowels for 3 days, whereas normally she passes 2-3 loose stools daily despite pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (Creon 10,000 units/kg/day). She had an admission for distal intestinal obstruction syndrome (DIOS) 2 years ago, which resolved with conservative management. On examination, she is afebrile, HR 100 bpm, BP 100/65 mmHg. The abdomen is distended, tympanic to percussion, and tender in the right lower quadrant with a palpable faecal mass. Bowel sounds are hyperactive. Erect abdominal X-ray shows faecal loading in the distal ileum and caecum with no free air and no air-fluid levels. A Gastrografin enema performed in the emergency department shows a meconium-like material obstructing the distal ileum without complete obstruction.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 100 bpm, BP 100/65 mmHg, RR 22, Temp 37.0°C
data_table.Examination: Distended, tender abdomen, palpable faecal mass RLQ, hyperactive bowel sounds
data_table.Investigations: AXR: faecal loading distal ileum/caecum, no free air, no air-fluid levels; Gastrografin enema: impacted material in distal ileum without complete obstruction
data_table.Medications: Creon 10,000 U/kg/day, inhaled tobramycin, dornase alfa

lead_in: What is the most appropriate non-operative therapy for this episode of DIOS?

options.text: Oral polyethylene glycol 3350 (Movicol) sachets daily with increased fluids and continued pancreatic enzymes
options.rationale: Oral PEG is for chronic constipation in CF; in acute DIOS with bilious vomiting and palpable mass, more aggressive bowel clearance is needed. Would be correct in chronic CF constipation (Source: RCH CPG: CF).

options.text: Oral Gastrografin (diatrizoate meglumine) 50 mL diluted in 200 mL water, with IV fluids if vomiting persists
options.rationale: Gastrografin is an osmotically active contrast agent that draws fluid into the bowel, hydrating and loosening the inspissated faecal material in DIOS. It is used both as a diagnostic enema and as an oral therapy for non-operative management. The standard dose is 50-100 mL of Gastrografin diluted in water, juice, or soft drink, with close monitoring of fluid balance. IV fluids are given to prevent dehydration from the osmotic shift. If vomiting occurs, an NG tube is placed. This approach avoids surgery in most cases. (Source: RCH CPG: Cystic Fibrosis – Gastrointestinal Complications; CF Foundation Guidelines)

options.text: Nasogastric N-acetylcysteine 10 mL of 20% solution four times daily to break down the inspissated mucus plugs
options.rationale: NAC is adjunctive to thin tenacious mucus but not primary therapy; oral Gastrografin is mainstay. Would be correct as adjunct (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Lactulose 10 mL three times daily plus a phosphate enema per rectum to stimulate colonic evacuation
options.rationale: Lactulose and phosphate enemas are not effective for DIOS (inspissated material in distal ileum/caecum, not simple constipation). Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Intravenous metoclopramide 8-hourly with continuous nasogastric suction, nil by mouth 48 hours
options.rationale: Metoclopramide is prokinetic but does not resolve impaction; NG suction relieves vomiting but does not treat obstruction. Would be correct only as supportive adjunct (Source: RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: Distal intestinal obstruction syndrome (DIOS) in CF is caused by inspissated faecal material and mucus in the distal ileum, presenting with bowel obstruction. Non-operative management with oral Gastrografin (diluted, 50-100 mL) is the cornerstone, creating an osmotic gradient that hydrates the impacted material. This is combined with IV fluids to prevent dehydration, and NG decompression if vomiting. Most episodes resolve without surgery. Polyethylene glycol is used for maintenance but not for acute DIOS. N-acetylcysteine may be used as an adjunct.
key_points: DIOS: acute distal small-bowel obstruction in CF, distinct from chronic constipation. (Source: RCH CPG: Cystic Fibrosis – GI)
First-line non-operative therapy: oral Gastrografin 50-100 mL diluted, with IV hydration and NG decompression if vomiting. (Source: CF Foundation Guidelines; RCH CPG)
Surgical intervention is reserved for complete obstruction, failed medical management, or perforation. (Source: RCH CPG: Cystic Fibrosis – GI)
Maintenance therapy to prevent DIOS: adequate hydration, pancreatic enzymes, and regular PEG if needed. (Source: CF Foundation Guidelines)
explanation.pearls: Gastrografin is both a diagnostic and therapeutic agent for DIOS. It is hyperosmolar and works by drawing fluid into the bowel lumen – ensure adequate IV fluid replacement.

stem: A 3-year-old Aboriginal girl presents with 24 hours of right ear pain, fever (38.4°C), and irritability. She attends daycare and has had three previous episodes of acute otitis media in the past 12 months, each treated with antibiotics. She is up to date with immunisations including the 13-valent pneumococcal conjugate vaccine. On otoscopy, the right tympanic membrane is bulging, erythematous, and has lost the normal light reflex. The left tympanic membrane is normal. She has a runny nose and mild cough. She is otherwise well, taking oral fluids. Her mother is concerned about recurrent infections and requests antibiotics. According to the Australian Therapeutic Guidelines and the Otitis Media Guidelines for Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander children, what is the most appropriate antibiotic choice?

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 38.4°C, HR 110 bpm, RR 26, SpO₂ 98%
data_table.Examination: Bulging, erythematous right TM, normal left TM, nasal discharge, no mastoid tenderness
data_table.Investigations: Not required
data_table.Medications: None currently; three prior AOM courses

lead_in: What is the most appropriate antibiotic choice?

options.text: No antibiotics; advise symptomatic management with paracetamol and review in 48 hours if not improved
options.rationale: In Aboriginal children AOM should be treated with antibiotics due to high risk of persistent infection, CSOM, and long-term hearing impact. Would never be correct in Aboriginal child (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Oral amoxicillin three times daily for 5 days
options.rationale: Amoxicillin is first-line for non-Indigenous children; Aboriginal children, especially otitis-prone, often need amoxicillin-clavulanate for broader cover. Would be correct only in non-Indigenous low-risk (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate twice daily for 5 days
options.rationale: Augmentin is recommended for those with recent amoxicillin or recurrent AOM, but duration for Aboriginal children with chronic features should be 7 days, not 5. Would be correct with 7-day duration (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate twice daily for 7 days
options.rationale: According to the Otitis Media Guidelines for Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Children, AOM in Aboriginal children should be treated with antibiotics for 7 days. Amoxicillin-clavulanate is recommended if the child has received amoxicillin in the past 3 months or has recurrent AOM (≥3 episodes in 12 months). The dose is 22.5 mg/kg twice daily. This covers beta-lactamase-producing organisms common in recurrent infections. A 7-day course reduces the risk of relapse and chronic suppurative otitis media. (Source: OM Guidelines for Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Children; Therapeutic Guidelines: Acute Otitis Media)

options.text: Intramuscular ceftriaxone daily for 3 days
options.rationale: IM ceftriaxone is reserved for severe infections, oral failure, or compliance concerns; not first-line for uncomplicated AOM. Would be correct only in those specific situations (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

explanation.summary: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander children have a disproportionate burden of otitis media and its complications. Guidelines recommend prompt antibiotic treatment for AOM, with a 7-day course. For otitis-prone children or recent amoxicillin exposure, amoxicillin-clavulanate 22.5 mg/kg twice daily for 7 days is the appropriate choice to cover beta-lactamase-producing Haemophilus influenzae and Moraxella catarrhalis. Watchful waiting is not recommended. IM ceftriaxone is reserved for more severe or refractory cases.
key_points: All Aboriginal children with AOM should receive antibiotic therapy; watchful waiting is not appropriate. (Source: OM Guidelines for Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Children)
For otitis-prone children or those with recent amoxicillin use, amoxicillin-clavulanate 22.5 mg/kg BD for 7 days is recommended. (Source: OM Guidelines; Therapeutic Guidelines: AOM)
The recommended first-line for non-Indigenous low-risk children is amoxicillin 15 mg/kg TDS for 5 days. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: AOM)
Hearing assessment and follow-up are crucial after AOM in Aboriginal children to detect persistent effusion and prevent chronic suppurative otitis media. (Source: OM Guidelines)
explanation.pearls: In Aboriginal children, otitis media is a notifiable condition in some jurisdictions. Always follow local guidelines for duration and follow-up, and involve Aboriginal health workers when possible.

stem: A 4-week-old male infant presents with a 10-day history of cough that began as mild coryza and has progressed to paroxysms of coughing followed by apnoeic pauses and post-tussive vomiting. The cough is non-productive, and he becomes cyanotic during the spells. On examination, the infant is alert but tachypnoeic with RR 50, SpO₂ 90% during a coughing paroxysm, HR 180 bpm. Between paroxysms, SpO₂ is 97%. Chest auscultation reveals transmitted upper airway sounds but no crackles. There is no fever. A nasopharyngeal aspirate for Bordetella pertussis PCR is positive. Full blood count shows WCC 45 ×10⁹/L with lymphocyte predominance (70%), haemoglobin 140 g/L, platelets 480 ×10⁹/L.

data_table.Vital signs: RR 50, SpO₂ 90% during paroxysm, HR 180, Temp 36.9°C
data_table.Examination: Paroxysmal cough with apnoea and cyanosis, post-tussive vomiting, transmitted upper airway sounds, no crackles
data_table.Investigations: NP aspirate: B. pertussis PCR positive; WCC 45, lymphocytes 70%, Hb 140, Plt 480

lead_in: What is the most important aspect of management?

options.text: Commence oral azithromycin daily for 5 days to reduce infectivity, and manage apnoeic episodes with careful monitoring in hospital
options.rationale: Azithromycin eliminates organism and limits spread but does not alter clinical course once paroxysmal phase started; primary issue is leukocytosis-driven pulmonary hypertension. Would be correct in early-phase pertussis (Source: CDNA Pertussis).

options.text: Initiate exchange transfusion or leukapheresis to reduce the white cell count, as extreme leukocytosis (>50 x10^9/L) is associated with fatal pulmonary hypertension
options.rationale: Severe pertussis in infants can cause extreme leukocytosis (WBC >50-100 ×10⁹/L), which is an independent risk factor for pulmonary hypertension and death. Hyperviscosity from lymphocytosis leads to pulmonary vascular obstruction. Exchange transfusion or leukapheresis is recommended to rapidly reduce the WBC count and improve pulmonary blood flow. Supportive care for apnoeas and prevention of secondary infection are important, but the leukocytosis-directed therapy is the critical life-saving intervention. (Source: RCH CPG: Pertussis; CDC Pertussis Guidelines)

options.text: Commence IV immunoglobulin 2 g/kg to neutralise pertussis toxin, as the toxin mediates the severe disease
options.rationale: No evidence IVIG alters course of pertussis; management is supportive plus antibiotics. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Start oral prednisolone daily to reduce airway inflammation and prevent apnoeic spells
options.rationale: Corticosteroids not recommended for pertussis and may worsen immunosuppression; no proven benefit. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Provide continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) via nasal prongs to splint the airways and reduce apnoea frequency
options.rationale: CPAP may be used for apnoea but immediate life-threatening problem is extreme leukocytosis causing pulmonary hypertension; CPAP does not address pathology. Would be correct as adjunct (Source: RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: This 4-week-old infant has confirmed pertussis with extreme leukocytosis (WCC 45, predominantly lymphocytes). In neonates and young infants, severe pertussis can lead to hyperleukocytosis (WCC >30-50 ×10⁹/L), which causes pulmonary vascular leukostasis, pulmonary hypertension, and cardiogenic shock. The most critical intervention is to reduce the white cell count via exchange transfusion or leukapheresis. Azithromycin is given to eliminate Bordetella pertussis and prevent spread but does not alter established disease. Supportive care for apnoeas and nutritional support are essential, but the leukocytosis-directed therapy is life-saving.
key_points: Extreme leukocytosis (WCC >30-50 ×10⁹/L) in infant pertussis is a major risk factor for pulmonary hypertension and mortality. (Source: RCH CPG: Pertussis; CDC Guidelines)
Exchange transfusion or leukapheresis is recommended to rapidly reduce the WBC count. (Source: RCH CPG: Pertussis)
Azithromycin 10 mg/kg daily for 5 days is given to eliminate nasopharyngeal carriage and reduce transmission. (Source: RCH CPG: Pertussis)
Do not delay ICU referral for severe pertussis; early intubation and ventilation may be required. (Source: RCH CPG: Pertussis)
explanation.pearls: In a young infant with pertussis, the white cell count is a prognostic marker. A WCC >30 ×10⁹/L should trigger immediate ICU review and consideration of exchange transfusion.

stem: A 22-month-old fully immunised boy is brought to his GP with 12 hours of fever (38.6 deg C) and increasing irritability with right ear pulling. He has had no vomiting and is drinking reasonably. Otoscopy of the right ear shows a bulging, erythematous tympanic membrane with loss of the light reflex and absent mobility on pneumatic otoscopy; the left tympanic membrane is normal. There is no otorrhoea, no mastoid tenderness and no neck stiffness. He has no risk factors for severe AOM (not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander, no cleft palate, no immunodeficiency, no cochlear implant). His mother asks whether he needs antibiotics or analgesia alone.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 38.6°C, HR 130 bpm
data_table.Examination: Bulging, red, immobile right tympanic membrane; normal left
data_table.Investigations: None required

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate diagnosis and management plan?

options.text: Otitis media with effusion (glue ear); reassurance, monitor hearing and arrange a routine review at 6 weeks with no antibiotic course needed
options.rationale: OME has retracted/neutral TM without acute symptoms; this child has acutely bulging immobile TM with fever. Would be correct in dull retracted TM without fever (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Acute otitis media; immediate amoxicillin three times daily for 5 days because antibiotics are mandatory for any AOM diagnosis under age 2
options.rationale: Antibiotics not mandatory for every AOM in low-risk over-2s; AU-specific cut-offs: bilateral disease, otorrhoea, severe symptoms, age under 2 with bilateral. Would be correct only with those features (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Acute otitis media, low-risk over 6 months with unilateral disease and intact TM; regular analgesia plus safety-net delayed amoxicillin prescription if not improving
options.rationale: Bulging erythematous immobile TM with acute fever meets diagnostic criteria for AOM. Australian guidance (eTG, RCH CPG) for a well, low-risk over-6-month child with unilateral non-perforated AOM is analgesia and watchful waiting or delayed prescription, with immediate antibiotics reserved for under 6 months, bilateral disease in under-2, otorrhoea, severe systemic symptoms, or high-risk groups (Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander, immunocompromised, cochlear implant).

options.text: Chronic suppurative otitis media; refer to ENT for tympanostomy tubes and culture-directed topical therapy with regular hearing follow-up
options.rationale: CSOM requires persistent perforation with discharge over 6 weeks; this is acute with intact TM. Would be correct in chronic perforation with discharge (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Otitis externa from recent swimming; commence topical ciprofloxacin drops with a wick if the canal is oedematous on otoscopy today
options.rationale: Otitis externa shows canal inflammation with normal TM; this child has bulging immobile TM. Would be correct in pure canal disease (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

explanation.summary: AOM is a clinical diagnosis based on acute onset, bulging of the tympanic membrane with erythema and reduced mobility. This child has all three cardinal signs.
key_points: AOM requires a bulging tympanic membrane plus at least one of: ear pain, fever, or otorrhoea. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Acute Otitis Media)
Pneumatic otoscopy to assess mobility is key. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Acute Otitis Media)
explanation.pearls: A red ear alone is not AOM – crying or fever can cause redness. The membrane must be bulging and immobile.

stem: A term infant (birth weight 3.2 kg) is now 4 hours old on the postnatal ward. He is being exclusively breastfed but his mother is concerned he is sleepy and not latching well. Bedside blood glucose is 2.2 mmol/L. He has no clinical signs of hypoglycaemia (no jitteriness, no seizure, no respiratory distress, normal tone). T 36.8 deg C, HR 140, RR 45. He is at risk because of suboptimal feeding.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 140, RR 45, Temp 36.8°C
data_table.Examination: Sleepy but rousable, normal tone
data_table.Investigations: BGL 2.2 mmol/L

lead_in: Which combination of immediate management and threshold for escalation is most appropriate per Queensland and ANZNN paediatric guidance?

options.text: Feed breast milk (or formula if breastfeeding inadequate) AND buccal dextrose 40% gel 200 mg/kg; recheck BGL at 30 min; escalate to IV 10% dextrose 2 mL/kg if still under 2.6 mmol/L
options.rationale: AU/QLD paediatric guidance: for an asymptomatic at-risk infant with BGL under 2.6 mmol/L (the AU threshold), give buccal dextrose 40% gel 200 mg/kg combined with a feed, recheck at 30 minutes, and escalate to IV 10% dextrose for failure to respond or any symptoms. IV dextrose is also first-line if BGL is under 1.5 mmol/L or any symptomatic hypoglycaemia.

options.text: Give an IV bolus of 2 mL/kg of 10% dextrose immediately as first-line without trying enteral feed plus buccal gel first in this stable neonate
options.rationale: IV dextrose is for severe (under 1.5 mmol/L) or symptomatic hypoglycaemia, or after failure of feed plus dextrose gel. Would be correct in severe/symptomatic hypoglycaemia (Source: Queensland Clinical Guidelines).

options.text: Observe and repeat BGL in 2 hours without specific intervention, given the absence of overt neuroglycopaenic symptoms in this neonate today
options.rationale: BSL 2.2 is below 2.6 threshold and requires intervention, not passive observation. Would never be correct (Source: Queensland Clinical Guidelines).

options.text: Commence IV 10% dextrose maintenance at 60 mL/kg/day without a bolus, accepting slow correction without enteral feed or buccal gel administration
options.rationale: Maintenance IV is for persistent/severe hypoglycaemia after failure of feed plus gel. Would be correct after failure of first-line (Source: Queensland).

options.text: Administer IM glucagon 0.1 mg/kg as first-line, deferring oral feed and buccal dextrose gel pending the glucagon-mediated glycaemic response
options.rationale: Glucagon is for refractory hypoglycaemia where IV access delayed/failed. Would be correct only with delayed/failed IV access (Source: Queensland).

explanation.summary: A term infant with BGL 2.2 mmol/L requires feeding as the first intervention. If BGL remains <2.6 mmol/L, buccal dextrose gel is the next step. IV dextrose is reserved for severe or persistent hypoglycaemia.
key_points: Treatment threshold for intervention in healthy term infants is BGL <2.6 mmol/L. (Source: Queensland Clinical Guideline: Neonatal Hypoglycaemia)
Stepwise: feed, recheck, buccal dextrose gel, then IV dextrose if needed. (Source: Queensland Clinical Guideline: Neonatal Hypoglycaemia)
explanation.pearls: Buccal dextrose gel is absorbed through the oral mucosa and has replaced IV bolus as the first-line pharmacological treatment for moderate hypoglycaemia.

stem: An 8-year-old girl with asthma on fluticasone propionate 125 mcg twice daily via MDI and spacer has been waking with cough 3 nights per week and using salbutamol 4-5 times per week for the past month. She has no daytime symptoms outside of PE class, where she takes pre-exercise salbutamol but still develops cough, chest tightness and a 25% drop in peak expiratory flow within 10 minutes of starting running. There has been no exacerbation requiring oral prednisolone in the last 12 months. Her parents have observed her use the spacer technique correctly and her pharmacy refill record confirms adherence to fluticasone. She is afebrile and her chest is clear on auscultation today. Her growth has been on the 25th centile and stable; her parents are explicitly anxious about further increasing the ICS dose because of growth concerns. She has known seasonal allergic rhinitis poorly controlled on intranasal fluticasone, and the family GP has asked you, the paediatric registrar, which preventer change best fits the Australian Asthma Handbook 6-11 year stepwise pathway given the exercise-induced pattern and the parental concern about ICS dose escalation.

data_table.Vital signs: SpO₂ 98%, RR 22, HR 90
data_table.Examination: Normal breath sounds, no wheeze
data_table.Investigations: PEFR 85% predicted

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step in her preventer therapy?

options.text: Step up to fluticasone 250 mcg twice daily (moderate-dose ICS) and pre-treat with salbutamol 10 minutes before PE
options.rationale: Per the Australian Asthma Handbook 6-11 year stepwise pathway, the first step when low-dose ICS plus pre-exercise SABA fails to control both nocturnal symptoms (>2 nights/week) and exercise-induced bronchoconstriction is to escalate to moderate-dose ICS rather than add a LABA or LTRA. The exercise pattern is best treated by improved baseline ICS control rather than direct add-on at this step.

options.text: Maintain low-dose fluticasone and add montelukast 5 mg daily as preferred add-on for the exercise-induced pattern
options.rationale: LTRA is an alternative add-on at the next step but the Australian Asthma Handbook prioritises ICS dose escalation BEFORE add-on therapy in 6-11 year olds, even with prominent exercise-induced bronchoconstriction. LTRA also has a black-box neuropsychiatric warning relevant in school-aged children.

options.text: Maintain low-dose fluticasone and add salmeterol 50 mcg twice daily (LABA) as combination therapy
options.rationale: LABA as add-on is not first-line in the 6-11 year stepwise pathway in Australia; ICS dose escalation is preferred before LABA. LABA monotherapy is contraindicated. Would be correct only after moderate-dose ICS plus LTRA has failed.

options.text: Step up to high-dose fluticasone 500 mcg twice daily given persistent nocturnal symptoms despite low-dose ICS
options.rationale: High-dose ICS is reserved for failure of moderate-dose ICS plus add-on (LTRA or LABA), not as a single jump from low-dose. Also accentuates the parental growth concern unnecessarily.

options.text: Maintain current ICS and add a 3-day course of oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg to regain control of nocturnal symptoms
options.rationale: Oral corticosteroids are reserved for acute exacerbations, not chronic preventer escalation. There is no current exacerbation.

explanation.summary: The Australian Asthma Handbook 6-11 year stepwise pathway escalates ICS dose BEFORE adding LABA or LTRA, even when the exercise-induced pattern is prominent. Step 3 (moderate-dose ICS) precedes step 4 (moderate ICS plus LTRA or LABA add-on).
key_points: Poor control = night waking >2/week, daily reliever use, or exercise-induced symptoms despite pre-exercise SABA.
Step 2 (low-dose ICS) -> Step 3 (moderate-dose ICS) is the next correct step before any add-on in 6-11 year olds.
LTRA is an add-on, not an ICS replacement, and carries an FDA/TGA neuropsychiatric warning.
LABA add-on in 6-11 year olds follows moderate-dose ICS failure, not low-dose failure.
explanation.pearls: Address adherence and technique (already confirmed here), then escalate ICS dose before any add-on. Pre-exercise salbutamol remains appropriate alongside moderate-dose ICS.

stem: A term infant born to a mother on buprenorphine for opioid dependence develops irritability, high-pitched cry, tremors, and diarrhoea at 48 hours of life. The Finnegan score is 12. What is the most appropriate management?

data_table.Vital signs: HR 170, RR 60, Temp 37.8°C
data_table.Examination: Irritable, high-pitched cry, tremors, diarrhoea, excoriations
data_table.Investigations: Finnegan score 12 (requires treatment)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Commence oral morphine sulphate in divided doses titrated to Finnegan scores
options.rationale: Oral morphine is the first-line pharmacological treatment for neonatal abstinence syndrome when non-pharmacological measures fail. Dosing is titrated to clinical scores. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal Abstinence Syndrome)

options.text: Commence oral phenobarbitone as monotherapy first-line for neonatal abstinence
options.rationale: Phenobarbitone is second-line, usually added if morphine alone insufficient. Would be correct as add-on (Source: RCH CPG: NAS).

options.text: Administer a single dose of IV naloxone via peripheral line
options.rationale: Naloxone is contraindicated in neonatal opioid withdrawal (precipitates acute withdrawal/seizures). Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Provide supportive care only (swaddling, low stimulation, small frequent feeds, skin contact)
options.rationale: Finnegan over 8 on two consecutive scores is indication for pharmacological treatment; supportive care alone is insufficient. Would be correct only with Finnegan under 8 (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Commence oral clonidine six-hourly as the first-line pharmacological agent
options.rationale: Clonidine is sometimes adjunct but not first-line; morphine is standard. Would be correct as adjunct (Source: RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: Neonatal abstinence syndrome (NAS) is symptomatic withdrawal from in-utero exposure to opioids, benzodiazepines, SSRIs, or nicotine, and is common enough in Australian birthing services that all hospitals use a standardised scoring tool (Finnegan or MOTHER). Onset is within 24-72 hours for short-acting opioids and up to 7 days for methadone or buprenorphine. First-line management is non-pharmacological: skin-to-skin, swaddling, low-stimulus environment, on-demand breastfeeding (encouraged unless HIV-positive or active drug use), small frequent feeds. Pharmacological treatment is triggered by three consecutive Finnegan scores of 8 or more, or any score of 12 or more. Oral morphine is first-line in Australia, with phenobarbitone added if morphine alone is insufficient or for non-opioid polysubstance exposure. Naloxone is contraindicated because precipitated withdrawal can be lethal.
key_points: First-line pharmacotherapy for NAS: oral morphine. (Source: RCH CPG: NAS)
Finnegan score ≥8 on two occasions indicates need for treatment. (Source: RCH CPG: NAS)
Naloxone is contraindicated in neonates with opioid dependence. (Source: RCH CPG: NAS)
explanation.pearls: In NAS, naloxone can precipitate a Finnegan score above 20, seizures and apnoea within minutes; first-line is oral morphine 0.04-0.1 mg/kg every 3-4 hours titrated to a Finnegan below 8 (RCH CPG; Australian Neonatal Medicines Formulary).

stem: A 3-year-old girl with severe atopic dermatitis presents with a 5-day flare that has not responded to her usual topical corticosteroid (mometasone furoate 0.1% ointment twice daily) and moisturisers. Her mother reports she has been scratching intensely and not sleeping. On examination, there is extensive erythema, lichenification, and weeping of the flexural areas of both arms and legs. There are also pustules on the cheeks and perioral area. She is afebrile. The lesions are not vesicular and do not look punched-out.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 37.1°C, HR 100, RR 22
data_table.Examination: Weeping erythematous flexural eczema, pustules on cheeks and perioral area, no vesicles, no systemic signs
data_table.Investigations: Not performed

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence oral cephalexin three times daily for secondary bacterial infection, and apply wet dressings with diluted corticosteroids
options.rationale: Pustular lesions on a background of severe eczema suggest secondary Staphylococcus aureus infection (impetiginised eczema). Oral cephalexin or flucloxacillin is indicated. Wet dressings with diluted topical corticosteroid will cool the skin, reduce inflammation, and increase steroid penetration. Eczema herpeticum (viral) should be considered if vesicles, punched-out ulcers, or fever are present, but it is not typical here. (Source: RCH CPG: Atopic Dermatitis; Therapeutic Guidelines: Atopic Dermatitis)

options.text: Start oral aciclovir four times daily for suspected eczema herpeticum
options.rationale: Eczema herpeticum presents with monomorphic punched-out vesicles, often systemic symptoms; this child has pustules suggesting staphylococcal infection. Would be correct with grouped vesicles and HSV contact (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Prescribe a stronger topical corticosteroid (e.g., betamethasone dipropionate 0.05% ointment) and add a calcineurin inhibitor
options.rationale: Short course stronger topical steroid may be used, but pustules indicate infection requiring antibiotic before escalating steroid. Would be correct only after infection treated (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Refer to dermatology for consideration of oral prednisolone or cyclosporine
options.rationale: Systemic immunosuppression is for severe refractory atopic dermatitis; inappropriate before treating acute bacterial superinfection. Would be correct only after failed maximised topicals without infection (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Perform a skin swab for culture and await results before prescribing antibiotics
options.rationale: Swabs may be taken but treatment must not be delayed; empirical antibiotics started immediately. Would be correct as adjunct alongside immediate antibiotic (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

explanation.summary: This child has a severe flare of atopic dermatitis with secondary bacterial infection (pustules, weeping, lichenification). Appropriate management includes oral anti-staphylococcal antibiotics (cephalexin or flucloxacillin) and wet dressings with diluted topical corticosteroids to rapidly control inflammation. Eczema herpeticum should be suspected if vesicles or punched-out ulcers, but pustules suggest bacterial infection.
key_points: Impetiginised eczema is treated with oral cephalexin 25 mg/kg TDS or flucloxacillin. (Source: RCH CPG: Atopic Dermatitis)
Wet dressings applied over topical steroids improve drug penetration and cooling. (Source: RCH CPG: Atopic Dermatitis)
Eczema herpeticum: look for monomorphic vesicles, fever, and systemic illness; treat with IV aciclovir. (Source: RCH CPG: Eczema Herpeticum)
explanation.pearls: In a child with severe eczema and pustules, always consider Staphylococcus aureus first, but if vesicles appear monomorphic, think herpes and start aciclovir.

stem: A 4-year-old girl presents with a limp and a swollen right knee of 6 weeks' duration, worse in the morning with stiffness lasting 30 minutes. She is otherwise well, afebrile, and has no rash. The right knee is warm, swollen, and has a small effusion but is not erythematous. The left knee and other joints are normal. Investigations show: WCC 9.5 ×10⁹/L, CRP 15 mg/L, ESR 35 mm/hr, ANA positive (1:640). What is the most important associated investigation or examination?

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 37.0°C, HR 95, RR 22
data_table.Examination: Swollen, warm right knee with effusion, no other joint involvement, no rash
data_table.Investigations: WCC 9.5, CRP 15, ESR 35, ANA 1:640, X-ray: soft tissue swelling, no bony erosion

lead_in: What is the most important associated investigation or examination?

options.text: Slit-lamp examination by ophthalmologist to screen for chronic anterior uveitis
options.rationale: Oligoarticular JIA, especially in young girls with positive ANA, carries a high risk (up to 30%) of asymptomatic chronic anterior uveitis, which can lead to blindness if untreated. Regular slit-lamp examinations are mandatory. (Source: RCH CPG: Juvenile Idiopathic Arthritis)

options.text: MRI of the right knee with intravenous gadolinium contrast enhancement
options.rationale: MRI not routinely required for oligoarticular JIA diagnosis; clinically based. Would be correct only with diagnostic uncertainty or atypical features (Source: RCH CPG: JIA).

options.text: Transthoracic echocardiogram to assess for occult pericarditis or valvulitis
options.rationale: Pericarditis is associated with systemic JIA, not oligoarticular. Would be correct only in systemic JIA (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Anti-streptolysin O titre to exclude acute post-streptococcal rheumatic fever
options.rationale: Prolonged duration (6 weeks) and lack of migratory arthritis/cardiac signs make ARF unlikely. Would be correct in suspected ARF with carditis (Source: RHDAustralia).

options.text: Bone marrow aspirate and trephine biopsy to exclude acute leukaemia
options.rationale: Isolated joint involvement, normal WCC and positive ANA are consistent with JIA; leukaemia would show systemic symptoms and haematological abnormalities. Would be correct only with cytopenias or systemic features (Source: RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: Oligoarticular JIA in ANA-positive young girls is strongly associated with chronic asymptomatic anterior uveitis. Regular slit-lamp eye examinations are the standard of care to prevent irreversible visual loss. Arthritis management includes NSAIDs and intra-articular corticosteroids.
key_points: ANA-positive oligoarticular JIA: highest risk for uveitis; slit-lamp screening every 3-4 months. (Source: RCH CPG: JIA)
Uveitis is usually asymptomatic; blindness can occur before redness or pain. (Source: RCH CPG: JIA)
explanation.pearls: A limp and swollen joint in a preschool girl with positive ANA = JIA until proven otherwise; eye screening is critical.

stem: A 13-year-old girl presents with 3 months of bloody diarrhoea, abdominal pain and 6 kg weight loss. She has noticed a painful perianal lump that discharges pus. On examination she is pale, BMI under the 5th centile, with a perianal fistula draining pus and no mouth ulcers. Bloods: Hb 92, CRP 58, albumin 28, faecal calprotectin over 2000 mcg/g. Colonoscopy shows recto-sigmoid inflammation with skip lesions in the caecum and deep serpiginous ulcers in the terminal ileum; biopsies are pending. HR 105, BP 100/65, T 37.3 deg C.

data_table.Examination: Pale, thin, perianal fistula, no mouth ulcers
data_table.Investigations: Hb 92, CRP 58, albumin 28, faecal calprotectin >2000; colonoscopy: continuous recto-sigmoid inflammation, skip lesions caecum, terminal ileal ulcers

lead_in: Which combination of most likely diagnosis and most discriminating endoscopic feature is correct?

options.text: Crohn's disease; perianal fistula together with terminal ileal involvement and skip lesions is the discriminating finding
options.rationale: Perianal fistula, terminal ileal deep serpiginous ulcers and skip lesions of the colon are characteristic of Crohn's. Ulcerative colitis lacks perianal fistulae, is continuous from the rectum without skip lesions, and does not involve the small bowel. Backwash ileitis in UC affects the caecum only with surface inflammation, not deep serpiginous ulcers.

options.text: Ulcerative colitis; perianal fistula reflects ongoing severe colitis
options.rationale: UC does not cause perianal fistula and is continuous without skip lesions; perianal fistula essentially excludes UC. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: IBD).

options.text: Infectious colitis with secondary perianal abscess
options.rationale: Chronicity (3 months), weight loss, anaemia, fistula and endoscopic pattern point to Crohn's, not acute infection. Would be correct only with acute presentation and confirmed pathogen (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Coeliac disease with secondary anaemia
options.rationale: Coeliac does not produce bloody diarrhoea or perianal fistula. Would be correct only with serology-positive coeliac picture (Source: RACGP).

options.text: Behcet disease with intestinal involvement
options.rationale: Behcet has recurrent oral/genital ulcers (absent here); endoscopic distribution and perianal fistula favour Crohn's. Would be correct with classic Behcet triad (Source: ACR).

explanation.summary: Paediatric inflammatory bowel disease in Australia is increasingly common, with prevalence around 1 in 2500 children. Crohn's disease and ulcerative colitis are distinguished on history, endoscopy and histology. Crohn's involves any part of the GI tract, with skip lesions, transmural inflammation, granulomas, fistulae and perianal disease (fissures, fistulae, tags, abscesses) - perianal involvement is a phenotype marker for aggressive disease and predicts the need for biologic therapy. Ulcerative colitis is limited to colon, with continuous inflammation from the rectum, mucosal disease only and no granulomas. Initial workup: FBC, CRP, ESR, faecal calprotectin, coeliac serology, stool MCS plus C difficile, then ileocolonoscopy plus upper GI endoscopy with biopsies, plus MR enterography. Induction of Crohn's is exclusive enteral nutrition (8 weeks) or corticosteroids; maintenance is thiopurine or methotrexate, escalating to anti-TNF (infliximab or adalimumab) for perianal disease or steroid-dependence.
key_points: Crohn's disease: skip lesions, terminal ileal involvement, perianal fistulae, transmural ulcers. (Source: RCH CPG: IBD)
Ulcerative colitis: continuous colonic inflammation from rectum, no small bowel or perianal disease. (Source: RCH CPG: IBD)
Perianal disease is almost pathognomonic for Crohn's in children. (Source: RCH CPG: IBD)
explanation.pearls: Perianal fistula, terminal ileal involvement and skip lesions are pathognomonic of Crohn's disease; ulcerative colitis is restricted to colon with continuous mucosal inflammation. Faecal calprotectin over 250 micrograms/g supports IBD over IBS for triage (RCH CPG IBD).

stem: A 2-year-old boy is brought to a GP by his parents who are concerned about his language. He says only 5 single words, does not combine words, and does not point to share interest. He rarely responds to his name and spends long periods lining up his toy cars. He was born at term and walked at 14 months. His newborn hearing screen was normal. There has been no developmental regression. The GP wishes to start the developmental concern assessment pathway.

data_table.Vital signs: Normal for age
data_table.Examination: Reduced eye contact, solitary play, no pretend play, repetitive car lining
data_table.Investigations: Hearing test normal at birth

lead_in: Which combination of investigation and next-step referral is most appropriate?
