options.text: Repeat formal audiology assessment AND administer the M-CHAT-R/F autism-specific screening tool; if either is abnormal, refer to a developmental paediatrician or community paediatric service via the public pathway; the AGPAR-funded diagnostic assessment requires a paediatrician or psychologist using DSM-5 criteria
options.rationale: Australian autism diagnostic pathway begins with parallel audiology (always exclude hearing loss in a language-delayed child) and an ASD-specific screen (M-CHAT-R/F is the validated tool for 16-30 months). Positive screen plus delayed language triggers referral to a developmental paediatrician or psychologist for a formal multi-disciplinary diagnostic assessment under DSM-5, which is the pathway that unlocks NDIS access.

options.text: Administer the Ages and Stages Questionnaire (ASQ-3) alone and reassess at age 3 if delayed
options.rationale: ASQ-3 is general developmental screen not autism-specific; in a child with multiple ASD red flags an ASD-specific tool plus audiology is required. Would be correct as general screen only without ASD red flags (Source: Autism CRC).

options.text: Administer the Childhood Autism Rating Scale (CARS) in the GP rooms today and provide a diagnosis
options.rationale: CARS is a diagnostic instrument used by trained clinicians as part of multi-disciplinary assessment, not first-line GP screening. Would be correct only in specialist MDT (Source: Autism CRC).

options.text: Order WPPSI cognitive assessment to establish intellectual level before any autism workup
options.rationale: WPPSI is a cognitive battery not ASD screen; cognitive assessment is part of later MDT diagnostic, not GP screening step. Would be correct in later MDT (Source: Autism CRC).

options.text: Refer directly to a developmental paediatrician without screening because referrals are accepted on clinical concern alone
options.rationale: Documenting ASD-specific screen and audiology strengthens referral and ensures correct triage/NDIS eligibility; direct referral without screening is suboptimal. Would never be correct as direct without documentation (Source: Autism CRC).

explanation.summary: For a child with red flags for ASD (limited language, lack of joint attention, repetitive play, poor name response), the M-CHAT-R/F is the appropriate screening tool at 16-30 months. A positive screen prompts referral to a paediatrician or diagnostic service.
key_points: M-CHAT-R/F is the recommended ASD screen at 18-24 months. (Source: RCH CPG: ASD)
Red flags: no pointing to share interest by 18 months, no response to name, repetitive behaviours. (Source: RCH CPG: ASD)
explanation.pearls: A child who does not point to share interest by 18 months should always be screened for ASD, even if language is otherwise on track.

stem: A 9-year-old girl is referred by her GP with a 2-week history of polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, and fatigue. There is no vomiting, no abdominal pain, and she is clinically well. Blood glucose is 22 mmol/L, and ketones are 1.2 mmol/L. Venous pH is 7.35, bicarbonate 20 mmol/L. She is not dehydrated. What is the most appropriate initial insulin regimen?

data_table.Examination: Mildly dry mucous membranes, no dehydration, alert
data_table.Investigations: BGL 22 mmol/L, ketones 1.2, pH 7.35, HCO₃⁻ 20

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial insulin regimen?

options.text: Commence subcutaneous basal-bolus insulin (e.g., insulin glargine once daily plus insulin aspart before meals) with age-appropriate carbohydrate counting education and diabetes team involvement
options.rationale: A child with new-onset type 1 diabetes who is not in DKA can start subcutaneous basal-bolus insulin as an outpatient or with a short hospital stay. Basal-bolus regimens mimic physiological insulin secretion. (Source: RCH CPG: Type 1 Diabetes – New Diagnosis)

options.text: Start an intravenous insulin infusion at 0.05 units/kg/hour until blood glucose is below 10 mmol/L, then transition to subcutaneous insulin
options.rationale: IV insulin is for DKA or severe hyperglycaemic hyperosmolar states, not stable new diagnosis. Would be correct in DKA (Source: RCH CPG: T1DM).

options.text: Prescribe oral metformin 500 mg BD to improve insulin sensitivity and delay insulin requirement
options.rationale: Metformin not indicated for T1DM; insulin is mandatory. Would be correct in confirmed T2DM (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Administer a single dose of subcutaneous insulin glargine 0.3 units/kg and monitor for 24 hours
options.rationale: Basal alone is insufficient; mealtime boluses needed to cover carbohydrate intake. Would be correct only as bridging or in T2DM (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Start a twice-daily premixed insulin (e.g., 30/70) twice daily
options.rationale: Premixed insulins less flexible and not recommended for initial paediatric management in AU where basal-bolus is standard. Would be correct only in specific clinical situations (Source: RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: A clinically well child with new type 1 diabetes and no DKA can be started on subcutaneous basal-bolus insulin (e.g., glargine + rapid-acting analogue before meals) with education and support from a paediatric diabetes team. IV insulin is unnecessary. Metformin has no role.
key_points: New T1DM without DKA: start SC basal-bolus insulin and refer to diabetes team. (Source: RCH CPG: Type 1 Diabetes)
Basal-bolus: long-acting insulin (glargine/detemir) once daily + rapid-acting insulin before meals. (Source: RCH CPG: Type 1 Diabetes)
explanation.pearls: The most dangerous time in new T1DM is the first year; comprehensive education from a multidisciplinary team reduces DKA readmissions.

stem: A 3-week-old male infant presents with a 4-day history of progressively worsening non-bilious vomiting, now projectile after every feed. He is alert but appears dehydrated with sunken eyes and dry mucous membranes. His weight is 3.4 kg, down from a birth weight of 3.8 kg. A test feed reveals a palpable 'olive-shaped' mass in the right upper quadrant and visible gastric peristalsis. Venous blood gas shows: pH 7.49, pCO₂ 46 mmHg, HCO₃⁻ 32 mmol/L, chloride 89 mmol/L, sodium 134 mmol/L, potassium 4.8 mmol/L. What is the most appropriate initial intravenous fluid for correcting the dehydration and electrolyte abnormalities?

data_table.Vital signs: HR 160 bpm, BP 70/45 mmHg, RR 44, Temp 36.9°C
data_table.Examination: Dehydrated, sunken eyes, dry mucosae, palpable olive mass RUQ, visible peristalsis

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial intravenous fluid?

options.text: 0.45% sodium chloride with 5% dextrose and 20 mmol/L potassium chloride at maintenance rate
options.rationale: Hypotonic fluids not appropriate for initial volume resuscitation in pyloric stenosis; isotonic required to correct hypochloraemia first; K only added once urine output established. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: Pyloric Stenosis).

options.text: Hartmann's (Ringer's lactate) solution at 1.5 times maintenance rate via peripheral IV cannula
options.rationale: Ringer's lactate has less chloride than 0.9 percent saline; may not rapidly correct severe hypochloraemic alkalosis. Would be correct as alternative when 0.9 saline not preferred (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: 0.9% sodium chloride 20 mL/kg boluses until perfusion improves, then 0.9% saline plus 5% dextrose
options.rationale: The priority is correction of hypovolaemia and chloride depletion with isotonic 0.9% NaCl boluses. Once adequately perfused, maintenance with 0.9% NaCl/5% dextrose continues chloride repletion and corrects the alkalosis. Potassium is added only after confirming adequate urine output. (Source: RCH CPG: Hypertrophic Pyloric Stenosis)

options.text: 0.9% sodium chloride with 5% dextrose and 40 mmol/L potassium chloride at maintenance rate
options.rationale: K should not be added until urine output confirmed and serum K known; hyperkalaemia risk. Initial resuscitation should be chloride-rich isotonic. Would be correct only after urine output confirmed (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: 5% dextrose with 0.2% sodium chloride at twice maintenance rate via peripheral IV cannula
options.rationale: Hypotonic fluids inadequate for chloride depletion and may worsen hyponatraemia. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: Pyloric stenosis causes persistent non-bilious projectile vomiting in a 3-6 week-old infant, classically male and first-born. Loss of HCl in vomit and water depletion produces the hallmark hypochloraemic, hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis with paradoxical aciduria. Initial fluid resuscitation is 10-20 mL/kg 0.9% saline boluses for hypovolaemia, then maintenance with 0.9% saline plus 5% dextrose at 1.25-1.5x maintenance until biochemistry corrects: chloride above 100, bicarbonate below 28, potassium above 3.5. Potassium is added once urine output is confirmed (typically 20 mmol KCl per litre). Ramstedt pyloromyotomy is a semi-elective procedure deferred until biochemistry normalises - usually 24-72 hours. Operating on an alkalotic infant risks postoperative apnoea because metabolic alkalosis suppresses central respiratory drive. Feeds are reintroduced within 6 hours post-op and most infants discharge within 48 hours.
key_points: Fluid resuscitation in pyloric stenosis: isotonic NaCl boluses, then maintenance 0.9% NaCl + dextrose. (Source: RCH CPG: Hypertrophic Pyloric Stenosis)
Hypochloraemic alkalosis corrects with chloride and volume repletion. (Source: RCH CPG: Pyloric Stenosis)
Surgery is delayed until acid-base and electrolyte abnormalities are corrected. (Source: RCH CPG: Pyloric Stenosis)
explanation.pearls: Pyloric stenosis biochemistry must be corrected (chloride >100, bicarbonate <28, potassium >3.5) before pyloromyotomy - operate on an alkalotic infant and they meet postoperative apnoea from suppressed central respiratory drive (RCH CPG Pyloric Stenosis).

stem: A term female infant is born to a 32-year-old mother with SLE and known anti-Ro/SSA and anti-La/SSB antibodies. At 2 hours of age, an annular erythematous rash is noted on the face and scalp. Heart rate is 70 bpm and regular; she is pink, well-perfused and in no respiratory distress. ECG shows third-degree AV block with atrial rate 140, ventricular rate 70 and narrow QRS. Bedside echo shows normal cardiac anatomy and good biventricular function; there is no hydrops. BP 65/35, SpO2 96 percent on room air. There are no clinical signs of low cardiac output.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 70 bpm (ventricular), RR 45, SpO₂ 96% RA, BP 65/35 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Erythematous annular rash face/scalp, bradycardia, no signs of heart failure
data_table.Investigations: ECG: complete AV block, atrial rate 140, ventricular rate 70, narrow QRS; Echo: normal anatomy, good function; maternal anti-Ro/SSA positive

lead_in: Which combination of immediate management and longer-term plan is most appropriate?

options.text: Empirical IV immunoglobulin 2 g/kg to clear maternal autoantibodies and reverse the conduction block in this currently asymptomatic neonate
options.rationale: IVIG does not reverse established complete heart block in neonatal lupus; antibody injury is permanent once block established. Would never be correct in established block (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Admit for continuous cardiac monitoring with PICU input; do NOT pace acutely as he is asymptomatic with adequate escape; pacemaker if rate falls under 50-55 bpm or low output
options.rationale: Anti-Ro/SSA mediated complete heart block is permanent. In an asymptomatic neonate with a ventricular rate over 55 bpm and normal function, immediate management is monitoring; permanent pacing is reserved for symptomatic bradycardia, low cardiac output, evolving cardiomyopathy, or ventricular rate under 50-55 bpm. The skin lesions resolve as maternal antibodies clear (around 6 months), per RCH CPG and AAP guidance.

options.text: Oral prednisolone daily to suppress autoimmune injury to the conduction system, continued for at least 6 months until maternal antibodies clear from circulation
options.rationale: Postnatal steroids do not reverse established complete heart block; not standard for asymptomatic infant. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Observe without specific monitoring as the block typically resolves in 6 months once maternal autoantibodies clear from neonatal circulation completely
options.rationale: Block is permanent; only skin rash resolves with antibody clearance. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Insert a temporary transvenous pacemaker immediately to target a rate of 100 bpm despite the adequate ventricular escape rhythm currently
options.rationale: Temporary pacing is for symptomatic bradycardia or ventricular rate under 50 with haemodynamic compromise. Would be correct only with symptoms or HR under 50 (Source: RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: Congenital complete heart block due to maternal anti-Ro antibodies is a permanent condition. In an asymptomatic infant with an adequate escape rhythm (ventricular rate 70 bpm), initial management is close monitoring with paediatric cardiology input. A permanent pacemaker is indicated if the infant becomes symptomatic or the ventricular rate is inadequate. The rash is self-limiting.
key_points: Maternal anti-Ro/SSA antibodies can cause congenital complete heart block in 1–5% of exposed foetuses. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal Lupus)
Established complete heart block is irreversible; management focuses on monitoring and pacemaker if needed. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal Lupus)
The neonatal lupus rash is transient and resolves without treatment. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal Lupus)
explanation.pearls: Mothers with anti-Ro antibodies should have foetal echocardiography starting at 16–18 weeks to detect emerging heart block, as early treatment of incomplete block may prevent progression.

stem: A 4-year-old boy was diagnosed with Kawasaki disease on day 8 of fever. He received intravenous immunoglobulin 2 g/kg as a single infusion and high-dose aspirin 30 mg/kg/day. The fever defervesced within 48 hours, and he was transitioned to low-dose aspirin 3 mg/kg/day. A follow-up echocardiogram at 3 weeks shows a giant aneurysm of the left anterior descending artery measuring 8 mm in diameter (z-score +10) and a smaller aneurysm of the right coronary artery (4 mm, z-score +4).

data_table.Vital signs: HR 110 bpm, BP 95/60 mmHg, Temp 36.7°C
data_table.Examination: Well, no mucocutaneous signs, no signs of heart failure
data_table.Investigations: Echo: giant LAD aneurysm 8 mm (z +10), RCA aneurysm 4 mm (z +4); Plt 350; INR 1.0

lead_in: What is the most important additional medication?

options.text: Add clopidogrel to dual antiplatelet therapy
options.rationale: DAPT is for medium-sized aneurysms; for giant aneurysms (8+ mm or z over 10) systemic anticoagulation with warfarin is preferred. Would be correct in medium aneurysms (Source: AHA Kawasaki).

options.text: Add warfarin with a target INR of 2.0-3.0 in addition to low-dose aspirin
options.rationale: Giant coronary artery aneurysms (≥8 mm or z-score ≥10) carry a high risk of thrombosis and myocardial infarction. The AHA and RCH guidelines recommend systemic anticoagulation with warfarin (target INR 2.0–3.0) plus low-dose aspirin. If warfarin is contraindicated, low-molecular-weight heparin or clopidogrel may be considered. (Source: AHA Kawasaki Disease Guideline; RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease)

options.text: Increase aspirin dose back to high-dose as monotherapy is sufficient for thromboprophylaxis
options.rationale: High-dose aspirin is anti-inflammatory in acute phase, not thromboprophylaxis for giant aneurysms; systemic anticoagulation is required. Would never be correct (Source: AHA Kawasaki).

options.text: Administer a second dose of intravenous immunoglobulin 2 g/kg to accelerate aneurysm resolution
options.rationale: Second IVIG may be used for persistent fever or inflammation but does not reduce established aneurysm size or thrombosis risk; this child is afebrile. Would be correct in persistent inflammation (Source: AHA Kawasaki).

options.text: No additional medication; low-dose aspirin alone is sufficient for giant aneurysms
options.rationale: Aspirin alone is insufficient for thrombosis prevention in giant aneurysms. Would never be correct (Source: AHA Kawasaki).

explanation.summary: Giant coronary artery aneurysms (≥8 mm or z-score ≥10) after Kawasaki disease require systemic anticoagulation with warfarin (target INR 2.0–3.0) in addition to low-dose aspirin to prevent thrombosis. Dual antiplatelet therapy with clopidogrel is an alternative if warfarin is not possible. Close echocardiographic follow-up is essential.
key_points: Giant aneurysms (≥8 mm or z ≥10): anticoagulation with warfarin (INR 2.0–3.0) plus aspirin. (Source: AHA Kawasaki Disease Guideline; RCH CPG)
Medium aneurysms (5–7 mm): aspirin alone or aspirin + clopidogrel. (Source: AHA Kawasaki Disease Guideline)
Small aneurysms (<5 mm): aspirin alone. (Source: AHA Kawasaki Disease Guideline)
explanation.pearls: Giant aneurysms in Kawasaki disease have the highest risk of myocardial infarction – lifelong anticoagulation is the standard of care.

stem: A 3-month-old female presents to ED with fever (38.6 deg C), irritability and poor feeding for 24 hours. She has no cough, rash or focal infection. Bag urine on the ward showed nitrites positive, leukocyte esterase 3+ and white cells over 100 x 10^6/L. The on-call paediatric registrar is reviewing the next step. The infant is irritable, has a normal fontanelle, and HR 170, RR 44, SpO2 97 percent on room air.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 38.6°C, HR 170 bpm, RR 44, SpO₂ 97%
data_table.Examination: Irritable, no focus of infection, normal fontanelle
data_table.Investigations: Urine dipstick: nitrites+, LE 3+, WBC >100 ×10⁶/L

lead_in: Which combination of confirmatory sampling, empirical therapy and imaging is most appropriate?

options.text: Commence oral trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole based on the bag urine and arrange outpatient renal ultrasound within the next 6 weeks of presentation
options.rationale: Bag urine has high contamination rates and is screening only; under-3-months requires hospital admission for parenteral antibiotics with sterile sample. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: UTI).

options.text: Catheter (or SPA) urine for culture before antibiotics; admit and commence IV benzylpenicillin (or ampicillin) plus IV gentamicin; arrange renal ultrasound during admission
options.rationale: RCH CPG: UTI requires a STERILE specimen (catheter or suprapubic aspirate) before treatment when bag urine is suggestive, because bag urine can over-diagnose and contaminate cultures. For febrile UTI in an infant under 3 months, admission with IV ampicillin plus gentamicin is recommended (avoiding ceftriaxone in neonates), with renal tract ultrasound during admission to detect structural abnormality; DMSA or MCUG are considered later based on results.

options.text: Trust the bag urine and commence IV antibiotics immediately without obtaining a sterile catheter or SPA specimen first in this febrile neonate
options.rationale: Bag urine has too high false-positive rate to direct treatment; sterile sampling required. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Treat with antipyretics and review in 24 hours assuming viral illness despite the positive bag urinalysis result in this febrile neonate today
options.rationale: Urinalysis strongly suggests UTI; untreated infant UTI risks pyelonephritis and scarring. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Perform lumbar puncture before any antibiotics in this febrile neonate despite the absence of meningism or sustained encephalopathy on examination
options.rationale: LP not routinely required for isolated UTI in well-appearing 3-month-old; considered if sepsis/meningitis suspected. Would be correct only with sepsis features (Source: RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: Urinary tract infection in infants under 3 months presents non-specifically with fever, irritability, poor feeding, and sometimes jaundice or sepsis; localising signs are usually absent. Bag urine is a screening test only - false positive rates exceed 80% due to skin contamination - so a positive bag must be confirmed with a clean catheter specimen or suprapubic aspirate before antibiotics. Suprapubic aspirate is gold standard but ED-dependent. Empirical Australian therapy is IV benzylpenicillin (or ampicillin) plus gentamicin pending culture; switch to oral on susceptibility once afebrile 48 hours. All infants under 6 months with first UTI need a renal tract ultrasound within 48 hours (or sooner if atypical or severe). DMSA scan at 4-6 months identifies cortical scarring; MCUG is reserved for recurrent UTI or atypical course. Constipation, dysfunctional voiding and family vesicoureteric reflux are common contributors.
key_points: Infants <3 months with febrile UTI: admit, IV antibiotics, renal ultrasound. (Source: RCH CPG: UTI)
Ampicillin + gentamicin is a typical empiric regimen. (Source: RCH CPG: UTI)
explanation.pearls: In a febrile infant under 3 months, bag urine is for screening only - confirmation requires catheter or suprapubic aspirate before antibiotics. Empirical cover is IV benzylpenicillin plus gentamicin; renal tract ultrasound within 48 hours (RCH CPG UTI).

stem: A 7-year-old boy presents with a 3-month history of excessive thirst and frequent urination, including nocturia. He drinks 4–5 L of water per day. His serum sodium is 148 mmol/L, serum osmolality 305 mOsm/kg, and urine osmolality is 150 mOsm/kg. After 4 hours of water deprivation, his serum osmolality rises to 310 mOsm/kg and urine osmolality remains at 160 mOsm/kg. After administration of subcutaneous desmopressin, urine osmolality increases to 650 mOsm/kg. What type of diabetes insipidus does this child have?

data_table.Vital signs: HR 90 bpm, BP 100/60 mmHg, RR 20
data_table.Examination: Normal, no signs of dehydration
data_table.Investigations: Serum Na 148, serum Osm 305, urine Osm 150; after water depr: serum Osm 310, urine Osm 160; after desmopressin: urine Osm 650

lead_in: What type of diabetes insipidus does this child have?

options.text: Central (cranial) diabetes insipidus
options.rationale: The urine fails to concentrate with water deprivation, but concentrates appropriately after exogenous desmopressin, indicating a deficiency of endogenous ADH. This is classical central diabetes insipidus. (Source: RCH CPG: Diabetes Insipidus)

options.text: Nephrogenic diabetes insipidus
options.rationale: Nephrogenic DI: urine does not concentrate after water deprivation OR after desmopressin (kidney resistant). Would be correct in nephrogenic pattern (Source: RCH CPG: DI).

options.text: Primary polydipsia (psychogenic polydipsia)
options.rationale: Primary polydipsia: water deprivation leads to appropriate urine concentration; serum osmolality remains normal. Would be correct in normal water deprivation response (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Diabetes mellitus
options.rationale: Diabetes mellitus has hyperglycaemia and glucosuria, not dilute urine with high serum osmolality and normal glucose. Would be correct with hyperglycaemia (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Syndrome of inappropriate ADH (SIADH)
options.rationale: SIADH has concentrated urine and hyponatraemia (opposite of this presentation). Would be correct in SIADH biochemistry (Source: RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: The water deprivation test with desmopressin challenge differentiates central DI (responds to desmopressin) from nephrogenic DI (no response) and primary polydipsia (concentrates with water deprivation alone). This child's response confirms central DI.
key_points: Central DI: dilute urine, hypernatraemia, urine concentrates after desmopressin. (Source: RCH CPG: Diabetes Insipidus)
Nephrogenic DI: dilute urine, hypernatraemia, no response to desmopressin. (Source: RCH CPG: Diabetes Insipidus)
explanation.pearls: The water deprivation test is risky if the child is already hypernatraemic – it should be performed under close supervision.

stem: A 6-year-old boy with palpable purpura on the legs and buttocks, colicky abdominal pain, and knee swelling presents with 3+ protein and 3+ blood on urine dipstick. His blood pressure is 112/72 mmHg (90th centile). He is otherwise well. Spot urine protein-to-creatinine ratio (UPCR) is 280 mg/mmol. What is the most appropriate next step?

data_table.Vital signs: BP 112/72 (90th centile), HR 95 bpm
data_table.Examination: Palpable purpura lower limbs, swollen knees, soft abdomen
data_table.Investigations: Urine: 3+ protein, 3+ blood; UPCR 280 mg/mmol; serum creatinine 50 µmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Refer to paediatric nephrology for consideration of renal biopsy and possible immunosuppressive therapy
options.rationale: Nephrotic-range proteinuria (UPCR >200 mg/mmol) in the setting of HSP indicates severe glomerulonephritis that requires specialist assessment and likely renal biopsy. (Source: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schönlein Purpura; KDIGO Glomerulonephritis Guideline)

options.text: Discharge with oral prednisolone daily and a follow-up urine dipstick in 1 week
options.rationale: Steroids alone are not sufficient for severe HSP nephritis; nephrology input required. Would be correct only in mild HSP nephritis (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Start an ACE inhibitor (enalapril) and monitor urine protein weekly
options.rationale: ACEi is for persistent proteinuria but does not address acute glomerular inflammation; biopsy-guided therapy needed. Would be correct as adjunct after biopsy (Source: KDIGO).

options.text: Reassure that the renal involvement is mild and will resolve spontaneously
options.rationale: UPCR over 200 mg/mmol is nephrotic-range proteinuria, severe and unlikely to resolve without intervention. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Order a renal ultrasound and voiding cystourethrogram
options.rationale: These investigations are for structural abnormalities, not HSP nephritis. Would be correct only with suspected structural anomaly (Source: RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: Nephrotic-range proteinuria in HSP signals severe glomerulonephritis and requires urgent paediatric nephrology referral and consideration of renal biopsy to guide immunosuppressive therapy.
key_points: Nephrotic-range proteinuria (UPCR >200 mg/mmol) in HSP: refer to nephrology for biopsy. (Source: RCH CPG: HSP; KDIGO Guidelines)
Mild urinary abnormalities (microscopic haematuria, mild proteinuria) can be monitored in primary care. (Source: RCH CPG: HSP)
explanation.pearls: In HSP, the severity of renal involvement does not correlate with the severity of skin or joint symptoms.

stem: A 9-month-old boy presents with intermittent colicky abdominal pain, red-currant-jelly stool and a palpable sausage-shaped mass in the right upper quadrant. Ultrasound confirms an ileocolic intussusception. He is haemodynamically stable (HR 150, BP 85/55, RR 36, T 37.2 deg C), afebrile and has a soft abdomen without peritonism. IV fluid resuscitation is running. Abdominal x-ray shows no pneumoperitoneum. There is no evidence of small-bowel perforation. Paediatric surgery is on standby. The radiology team is available for fluoroscopic-guided reduction.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 150 bpm, BP 85/55 mmHg, RR 36, Temp 37.2°C
data_table.Examination: Soft abdomen, no peritonism, palpable mass RUQ
data_table.Investigations: Ultrasound: ileocolic intussusception; AXR: no free air

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate definitive management and what is the safety prerequisite for that procedure?

options.text: Non-surgical reduction by air enema under fluoroscopic guidance, with paediatric surgery on standby; proceed only if peritonitis, perforation, or shock are ABSENT
options.rationale: In a stable child without peritonitis or perforation, air-contrast enema reduction is first-line in Australia, with paediatric surgery on standby in case of perforation or failed reduction. Contraindications (peritonitis, pneumoperitoneum, refractory shock) must be absent; attempting enema in those circumstances is the anti-pattern. Surgical reduction is reserved for failed enema, contraindications, or pathological lead point.

options.text: Emergency laparotomy with manual reduction as first-line, bypassing imaging and non-operative options regardless of clinical stability
options.rationale: Surgery is for failed enema, perforation or peritonitis. Would be correct only after enema failure or peritonitis (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Observation with serial abdominal examinations only, deferring all imaging and intervention pending presumed spontaneous resolution over hours
options.rationale: Untreated intussusception does not reliably resolve and risks ischaemia and perforation. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Diagnostic laparoscopy and laparoscopic reduction as first-line, bypassing non-operative air enema in this clinically stable child without imaging
options.rationale: Surgical (open or lap) reduction is second-line in AU after failed enema or when contraindicated. Would be correct after failed enema (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: IV antibiotics and bowel rest alone, treating as presumed gastroenteritis without addressing the obstructing lesion or arranging imaging
options.rationale: Mechanical reduction is required; antibiotics alone do not reduce intussusception. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: In a stable child with confirmed intussusception and no signs of perforation or peritonitis, air enema reduction is the standard first-line treatment. Surgery is reserved for failed reduction or contraindications.
key_points: Air enema is first-line for uncomplicated intussusception. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)
Contraindications: peritonitis, perforation, shock. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)
explanation.pearls: Air enema is both diagnostic and therapeutic; success rates exceed 80% in experienced centres.

stem: A 13-year-old girl is brought to the ED by her parents after they discovered she ingested approximately 50 tablets of paracetamol 500 mg (total 25 g) following an argument at school. The ingestion occurred approximately 10 hours ago. She complains of nausea and mild right upper quadrant discomfort. She weighs 45 kg. She is alert and oriented with slight epigastric tenderness. HR 100, BP 105/65, RR 22, SpO2 99 percent, T 36.9 deg C. A paracetamol level drawn 9 hours post-ingestion is 1800 micromol/L. ALT 45 U/L, AST 50 U/L, total bilirubin 18, INR 1.1, creatinine 60. She has no known medical history and no other ingestions.

data_table.Examination: Alert, mild RUQ tenderness, no jaundice, no encephalopathy
data_table.Investigations: Paracetamol level 1800 µmol/L at 9h; ALT 45, AST 50, bilirubin 18, INR 1.1, Cr 60

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate intervention?

options.text: Commence IV N-acetylcysteine using the AU two-bag regimen (200 mg/kg over 4 hours then 100 mg/kg over 16 hours) without nomogram comparison given dose >200 mg/kg
options.rationale: Australian guidance (RCH CPG, TG Toxicology) uses a paracetamol level plotted against the Rumack-Matthew nomogram for single acute ingestions 4-15 hours post-ingestion; her 9-hour level of 1800 micromol/L is well above the treatment line. AU practice has transitioned to a two-bag NAC regimen (200 mg/kg over 4 hours then 100 mg/kg over 16 hours) which reduces anaphylactoid reactions vs the older three-bag protocol. Treatment is started immediately and NAC is continued if LFTs or INR show hepatotoxicity at the end of the initial course.

options.text: Activated charcoal 50 g orally now and reassess the need for NAC at 4 hours despite the late presentation and the supratherapeutic dose ingested
options.rationale: Activated charcoal is effective only within 1-2 h of ingestion; at 10 h no role. Would be correct only within 1-2 h (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Observe without treatment as ALT and INR are currently normal; repeat bloods at 12 hours to detect delayed hepatotoxicity before considering NAC initiation
options.rationale: Normal initial ALT does not exclude impending hepatotoxicity; level is above nomogram line and NAC must start promptly. Would never be correct (Source: Australian PIC).

options.text: Refer for urgent liver transplantation evaluation given the high paracetamol level despite the absence of established acute liver failure currently
options.rationale: Transplantation is for established fulminant failure unresponsive to NAC, not initial presentation with normal LFTs. Would be correct only with established fulminant failure (Source: King's College criteria).

options.text: IV pantoprazole 40 mg for gastric protection and immediate psychiatric review without addressing the paracetamol toxicity itself through NAC therapy
options.rationale: Psychiatric review is essential after medical stabilisation; immediate priority is NAC. Would be correct in parallel, not as primary (Source: Australian PIC).

explanation.summary: Paracetamol poisoning with a toxic blood level requires immediate N-acetylcysteine infusion to prevent hepatocyte necrosis. The Rumack-Matthew nomogram guides treatment; this level is above the treatment threshold. Normal transaminases do not exclude the need for NAC. Activated charcoal is ineffective after 2 hours. Liver transplant is only for fulminant failure.
key_points: Administer NAC if paracetamol level is above the treatment line on the Australian nomogram (e.g., approximately 1000 micromol/L at 4 hours, dropping to approximately 500 micromol/L at 10 hours). (Source: RCH CPG: Paracetamol Poisoning)
The standard IV NAC regimen in Australia is 300 mg/kg over 20 hours (three-bag protocol). (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)
Normal ALT/AST at presentation does NOT rule out impending hepatotoxicity. (Source: RCH CPG: Paracetamol Poisoning)
explanation.pearls: Time is liver: NAC is most effective within 8 hours of ingestion, but still beneficial later. Start NAC while awaiting levels if the history suggests a significant overdose.

stem: A term infant is born by emergency caesarean section for suspected placental abruption. Apgars are 2 at 5 minutes and 4 at 10 minutes. Cord blood pH is 6.85, base deficit 18 mmol/L. At 2 hours of age, the infant is hypotonic, has intermittent apnoea, and a weak cry. Neurological examination shows reduced consciousness, decreased spontaneous movements, and an absent suck reflex. The infant meets criteria for moderate hypoxic-ischaemic encephalopathy (HIE) by clinical staging. Amplitude-integrated EEG (aEEG) shows moderately abnormal background activity.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 120 bpm, BP 55/30 mmHg, RR assisted, SpO₂ 95%
data_table.Examination: Hypotonic, reduced consciousness, absent suck, intermittent apnoea
data_table.Investigations: Cord pH 6.85, BD 18; aEEG moderately abnormal; no seizures on aEEG

lead_in: What is the most appropriate core temperature target and duration?

options.text: Cool to 33.5 deg C (range 33-34 deg C) for 72 hours, followed by slow rewarming at 0.5 deg C per hour
options.rationale: Therapeutic hypothermia for moderate to severe HIE in term infants involves whole-body cooling to a rectal temperature of 33.5°C (range 33–34°C) for 72 hours, followed by controlled rewarming. This reduces death and disability. (Source: RCH Neonatal Encephalopathy Guideline; TOBY Trial; ILCOR Guidelines)

options.text: Cool to 32 deg C for 24 hours, then actively rewarm over 6 hours
options.rationale: Standard protocol is 33.5 degC for 72 h, not 32 degC for 24 h; deeper cooling has not shown benefit and increases adverse effects. Would never be correct (Source: ILCOR).

options.text: Provide only supportive care with maintenance of normothermia (36.5-37.5 deg C), as cooling is not indicated for moderate HIE
options.rationale: Moderate HIE with abnormal aEEG is indication for therapeutic hypothermia which improves outcomes. Would be correct only in mild HIE without indication (Source: RCH Neonatal Encephalopathy).

options.text: Cool to 34.5 deg C for 48 hours and reassess neurological status before continuing
options.rationale: Evidence supports 72 h at 33.5 degC; shorter durations or higher temperatures not standard. Would never be correct (Source: TOBY Trial; ILCOR).

options.text: Administer intravenous phenobarbitone and maintain normothermia, as seizures are the primary concern
options.rationale: Phenobarbitone is for clinical/electrographic seizures, but primary neuroprotective is hypothermia. Would be correct only with seizures (Source: RCH Neonatal Encephalopathy).

explanation.summary: Therapeutic hypothermia for HIE is provided at 33.5°C (range 33–34°C) for 72 hours, followed by controlled rewarming. It is indicated for term infants with moderate to severe HIE and abnormal aEEG. The intervention reduces death and major neurodevelopmental disability.
key_points: Cooling criteria: ≥36 weeks, evidence of perinatal hypoxia-ischaemia, moderate to severe HIE, abnormal aEEG. (Source: RCH Neonatal Encephalopathy Guideline)
Temperature target: 33.5°C (33–34°C) for 72 hours, rewarm at 0.5°C/hr. (Source: ILCOR Guidelines)
explanation.pearls: Cooling should be initiated within 6 hours of birth for maximum benefit. Every hour of delay reduces neuroprotection.

stem: A 12-year-old girl with known ileocolonic Crohn's disease on oral azathioprine 2 mg/kg/day and mesalazine presents with a 2-week history of painful perianal swelling and purulent discharge. She has no abdominal pain or diarrhoea. Examination reveals an indurated perianal fistula with expressible pus. A pelvic MRI confirms a transsphincteric fistula with an associated small abscess.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 85 bpm, BP 110/65 mmHg, Temp 37.0°C
data_table.Examination: Perianal fistula with purulent discharge, no abdominal tenderness
data_table.Investigations: MRI: transsphincteric fistula, small abscess; CRP 35 mg/L; Hb 105 g/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate definitive management in addition to antibiotics and surgical drainage?

options.text: Commence TNF inhibitor infliximab IV at 0, 2, and 6 weeks, then 8-weekly, with continued immunomodulator
options.rationale: Perianal fistulising Crohn's disease is an indication for anti-TNF therapy (infliximab or adalimumab) in combination with an immunomodulator (azathioprine or methotrexate). Antibiotics and surgical drainage treat the acute abscess, but anti-TNF therapy is needed for fistula closure. (Source: RCH CPG: Inflammatory Bowel Disease; ECCO/ESPGHAN Guidelines)

options.text: Increase azathioprine dose and continue mesalazine, expecting the fistula to heal with medical therapy alone
options.rationale: Perianal fistulae rarely close with azathioprine alone; biologics are far more effective. Would never be correct (Source: ECCO/ESPGHAN).

options.text: Perform a fistulotomy with primary closure under general anaesthesia and continue current azathioprine
options.rationale: Fistulotomy alone in Crohn's may result in non-healing and incontinence risk; combined medical therapy with biologics is standard. Would be correct only in non-Crohn's simple fistula (Source: RCH CPG: IBD).

options.text: Insert a Seton drain and treat with oral metronidazole three times daily for 6 months
options.rationale: Seton placement controls drainage but definitive closure requires biologic therapy. Would be correct as adjunct, not definitive (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Refer for diverting colostomy as perianal Crohn's disease is refractory to all medical therapy
options.rationale: Colostomy is last resort for severe refractory disease; medical therapy with biologics is effective in most. Would be correct only after biologic failure (Source: RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: Perianal fistulising Crohn's disease requires a combination of surgical drainage (if abscess), antibiotics, and escalation to anti-TNF therapy (infliximab) combined with an immunomodulator. Anti-TNF agents are the most effective medical therapy for fistula closure.
key_points: Anti-TNF therapy (infliximab or adalimumab) is indicated for fistulising Crohn's disease. (Source: ECCO/ESPGHAN Guidelines)
Continue azathioprine as combination therapy to reduce immunogenicity. (Source: RCH CPG: IBD)
explanation.pearls: A perianal fistula in a child with Crohn's disease is a sign of aggressive disease phenotype and warrants early biologic therapy.

stem: A previously well 18-month-old boy presents to the emergency department with a 24-hour history of vomiting, increasing lethargy, and now a generalised tonic-clonic seizure. His parents report that he had an upper respiratory tract infection 3 days ago and had been eating less. On arrival, the child is obtunded with a Glasgow Coma Scale of 7, responding only to pain. Vital signs: heart rate 160 bpm, blood pressure 90/60 mmHg, respiratory rate 40 and deep, temperature 37.8°C. He is intubated for airway protection. Initial venous blood gas shows pH 7.48, pCO₂ 24 mmHg, bicarbonate 18 mmol/L. Ammonia level is 420 µmol/L (normal <60). Blood glucose is 5.5 mmol/L, lactate 1.2 mmol/L, liver function tests and renal function are normal. Urinalysis is negative for ketones. Plasma amino acids and urine orotic acid are pending. A CT brain is normal.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 160, BP 90/60, RR 40 (deep), Temp 37.8°C, GCS 7, intubated
data_table.Examination: Obtunded, no focal neurology, no organomegaly
data_table.Investigations: Ammonia 420 µmol/L, pH 7.48, pCO₂ 24, HCO₃⁻ 18, glucose 5.5, lactate 1.2, normal LFTs and Cr; CT brain normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate pharmacological therapy to lower the ammonia level?

options.text: Intravenous sodium benzoate and sodium phenylacetate, with intravenous arginine hydrochloride
options.rationale: The presentation is classical for an acute hyperammonaemic crisis due to a urea cycle defect (most likely ornithine transcarbamylase deficiency in a male). Immediate therapy includes nitrogen-scavenging drugs (sodium benzoate and sodium phenylacetate) and arginine to provide substrate for the alternative pathway. This must be started urgently to prevent irreversible neurological injury. (Source: RCH CPG: Hyperammonaemia; NIH Urea Cycle Disorders Consortium Guidelines)

options.text: Intravenous lactulose 10 mL hourly via nasogastric tube to trap ammonia in the gut
options.rationale: Lactulose is for hepatic encephalopathy not urea cycle disorders; hyperammonaemia here is genetic urea cycle defect, not liver failure. Would be correct in hepatic encephalopathy (Source: RCH CPG: Hyperammonaemia).

options.text: Intravenous N-acetylcysteine loading dose as a hepatoprotective agent
options.rationale: NAC is for paracetamol toxicity, not urea cycle disorders. Would be correct in paracetamol overdose (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Continuous veno-venous haemodialysis (CVVHD) without nitrogen scavenging drugs, as pharmacological therapy is ineffective
options.rationale: Haemodialysis often needed for very high ammonia but nitrogen-scavenging drugs are first-line medical therapy; dialysis is adjunctive. Would be correct as adjunct, not sole therapy (Source: HMSANZ).

options.text: Oral neomycin daily to eliminate ammonia-producing gut flora
options.rationale: Neomycin is non-absorbable antibiotic used in hepatic encephalopathy, not urea cycle defects. Would be correct only in hepatic encephalopathy (Source: RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: Acute hyperammonaemia in a previously well child with respiratory alkalosis and normal glucose suggests a urea cycle disorder, most commonly OTC deficiency. Immediate treatment with nitrogen scavengers (sodium benzoate, sodium phenylacetate) and arginine is essential. Dialysis may be required for levels >400–500 µmol/L or failure to respond, but medical therapy must begin immediately.
key_points: Neonatal or infant hyperammonaemia with respiratory alkalosis, normal glucose, and no ketones: suspect urea cycle defect. (Source: RCH CPG: Hyperammonaemia)
Emergency treatment: IV sodium benzoate, sodium phenylacetate, arginine. (Source: NIH UCDC Guidelines)
Dialysis is added if ammonia >400–500 µmol/L or no rapid drop with medical therapy. (Source: RCH CPG: Hyperammonaemia)
explanation.pearls: The earliest clue to a urea cycle disorder is respiratory alkalosis with hyperammonaemia – the brainstem's respiratory centre is stimulated by ammonia.

stem: A 7-year-old girl with genetic generalised epilepsy presents with convulsive status epilepticus. She received buccal midazolam 0.5 mg/kg 20 minutes ago without effect, then IV levetiracetam 40 mg/kg 15 minutes ago with ongoing seizures. She has now been convulsing continuously for 45 minutes. IV access is established and she is intubated and ventilated by the ED team. BP 90/55, HR 130, SpO2 96 percent. BGL 6.0, Na 138, ionised calcium normal. CT brain performed earlier is normal. There is no eclampsia, no hyponatraemia and no hypoglycaemia. The paediatric neurologist is unavailable and PICU is preparing to admit.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 90/55, HR 130, ventilated, SpO₂ 96%
data_table.Examination: Generalised tonic-clonic seizure ongoing, no focal signs
data_table.Investigations: BGL 6.0, Na⁺ 138, Ca²⁺ normal, CT brain (performed earlier) normal

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next anticonvulsant under the APLS Australia status epilepticus pathway?

options.text: IV midazolam infusion titrated to seizure suppression with PICU monitoring; consider thiopentone or phenobarbitone if midazolam ineffective
options.rationale: Following first-line benzodiazepines and a second-line agent (levetiracetam), refractory paediatric convulsive status epilepticus is managed with a benzodiazepine infusion (midazolam) or thiopentone or phenobarbitone, in PICU with continuous EEG where available. Midazolam infusion is the most commonly used step at this point in AU paediatric ICUs.

options.text: IV phenytoin loading 20 mg/kg over 20 minutes as third-line therapy with cardiac monitoring for arrhythmias and infusion-site irritation
options.rationale: Levetiracetam already given as second-line; another similar agent is less likely effective and risks toxicity. Would be correct only if first second-line agent not yet tried (Source: APLS Australia).

options.text: IV propofol bolus then prolonged infusion in this non-intubated child without dedicated anaesthetic support or airway readiness
options.rationale: Propofol infusion is avoided in children for prolonged sedation due to propofol-infusion syndrome risk. Would be correct only briefly for short-duration sedation (Source: APLS Australia).

options.text: Intramuscular paraldehyde as the next-line anticonvulsant via the existing peripheral IV cannula
options.rationale: Paraldehyde is largely historical in Australian paediatric practice and is not part of the current APLS Australia refractory status pathway when IV access is already secured; midazolam infusion or thiopentone is preferred at this step (Source: RCH CPG: Status Epilepticus; APLS Australia).

options.text: IV magnesium sulphate 40 mg/kg over 20 minutes as the next-line anticonvulsant for refractory status
options.rationale: IV magnesium has no role in the general paediatric status epilepticus pathway: it is indicated only for eclampsia, documented hypomagnesaemia, or torsades de pointes, none of which are present here (ionised Ca normal, no pregnancy, no arrhythmia). The next step in this refractory paediatric case is a midazolam infusion (Source: RCH CPG: Status Epilepticus; APLS Australia).

explanation.summary: Refractory status epilepticus that fails benzodiazepines and a second-line agent (levetiracetam or phenytoin) should be managed with an IV midazolam infusion or phenobarbitone. Thiopentone infusion with continuous EEG monitoring is reserved for refractory cases. Propofol is avoided in children for prolonged sedation.
key_points: After failure of benzodiazepines and levetiracetam/phenytoin, start midazolam infusion or phenobarbitone. (Source: RCH CPG: Status Epilepticus)
Propofol is contraindicated for prolonged infusions in children due to propofol infusion syndrome. (Source: RCH CPG: Status Epilepticus)
explanation.pearls: A convulsion lasting beyond 45 minutes is refractory status epilepticus; an anaesthetic agent is often required, but midazolam infusion is a reasonable next step before thiopentone.

stem: A 10-year-old boy presents with generalised oedema, weight gain, and frothy urine of 10 days' duration. Urine dipstick shows 4+ protein, and a spot protein-to-creatinine ratio is 320 mg/mmol. Blood tests show serum albumin 18 g/L, creatinine 55 µmol/L, and total cholesterol 8.5 mmol/L. There is microscopic haematuria (+++ on dipstick), but no visible blood. Blood pressure is 118/80 mmHg (95th centile). Complement C3 level is low at 0.3 g/L (normal 0.9–1.8). Antinuclear antibody is negative.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/80 (95th centile), HR 90 bpm
data_table.Examination: Oedematous, no rash, no arthritis
data_table.Investigations: Urine: 4+ protein, PCR 320 mg/mmol, +++ haematuria; Albumin 18 g/L, Cr 55 µmol/L, cholesterol 8.5; C3 0.3 g/L, ANA negative

lead_in: Which feature most strongly indicates the need for a renal biopsy before starting corticosteroids?

options.text: Low serum complement C3 level
options.rationale: Low complement levels in a child with nephrotic syndrome strongly suggest membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis or lupus nephritis, which do not respond to corticosteroids alone and require biopsy to guide therapy. (Source: RCH CPG: Nephrotic Syndrome; KDIGO Glomerulonephritis Guidelines)

options.text: Microscopic haematuria
options.rationale: Microscopic haematuria can occur in MCD and is not absolute biopsy indication, especially in typical age group. Would be correct only with persistent macroscopic haematuria (Source: KDIGO 2021).

options.text: Age of 10 years
options.rationale: MCD is most common in younger children but 10 years is within typical range; age alone does not mandate biopsy. Would be correct only outside typical age (under 1 or over 12) (Source: KDIGO).

options.text: Blood pressure at the 95th centile
options.rationale: Hypertension can occur in MCD and is not definitive biopsy indication unless severe or persistent. Would be correct only with persistent severe HTN (Source: KDIGO).

options.text: Nephrotic-range proteinuria (PCR >200 mg/mmol)
options.rationale: Heavy proteinuria is the definition of nephrotic syndrome and does not independently indicate biopsy. Would never be correct as biopsy indication (Source: KDIGO).

explanation.summary: In childhood nephrotic syndrome, a low complement C3 level raises the suspicion of post-infectious glomerulonephritis, membranoproliferative GN, or lupus nephritis, all of which require biopsy and specific management. Microscopic haematuria and hypertension can be present in minimal change disease, but low complement is a red flag.
key_points: Low C3 in nephrotic syndrome is an indication for renal biopsy before steroids. (Source: KDIGO Guidelines)
Typical minimal change disease: normal complement, no significant haematuria or hypertension. (Source: RCH CPG: Nephrotic Syndrome)
explanation.pearls: When in doubt about the need for biopsy in nephrotic syndrome, check complement levels – a low C3 changes the game.

stem: A 4-week-old male infant with projectile non-bilious vomiting has ultrasound-confirmed hypertrophic pyloric stenosis. He was clinically dehydrated on arrival. Initial VBG: pH 7.52, pCO2 46, HCO3 34, Cl 82, Na 130, K 3.2. He received 0.9% saline 20 mL/kg bolus and is now on 0.9% saline plus 5% dextrose plus 40 mmol/L potassium chloride at maintenance. Twenty-four hours later: Cl 96, HCO3 27, urine output adequate. The surgical registrar is preparing for Ramstedt pyloromyotomy and the anaesthetist is reviewing operative readiness.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 150 bpm, BP 70/45, RR 40
data_table.Examination: Dehydrated, palpable olive mass
data_table.Investigations: Initial: pH 7.52, HCO₃⁻ 34, Cl⁻ 82, Na⁺ 130, K⁺ 3.2; after 24h: Cl⁻ 96, HCO₃⁻ 27, UOP adequate

lead_in: Which biochemical thresholds must be met before proceeding to pyloromyotomy?

options.text: Chloride over 95 mmol/L AND bicarbonate under 30 mmol/L AND potassium over 3.5 mmol/L with adequate urine output
options.rationale: The AU preoperative thresholds for pyloromyotomy in HPS are Cl over 95 (often quoted over 100), HCO3 under 30 and K over 3.5 mmol/L with adequate UOP. Operating before correction increases perioperative apnoea risk from persistent CSF alkalosis and risks arrhythmia from hypokalaemia, per RCH CPG: Pyloric Stenosis.
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Paediatric Fluid and Electrolyte

options.text: Sodium over 135 mmol/L AND potassium over 3.5 mmol/L only, without any requirement for chloride or bicarbonate correction
options.rationale: Na and K matter but the primary indices of alkalosis correction are chloride and bicarbonate. Would be correct only as one of several parameters (Source: RCH CPG: Pyloric Stenosis).

options.text: Venous pH below 7.40 only, regardless of electrolyte derangement or current intravascular volume status today
options.rationale: pH may normalise before chloride deficit is fully corrected; chloride and bicarbonate are the specific indices. Would never be sufficient alone (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Base excess below +5 only, regardless of sodium, potassium, chloride, bicarbonate or urine output status
options.rationale: Base excess tracks bicarbonate but does not address chloride deficit specifically. Would never be sufficient alone (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Normal serum creatinine alone, regardless of any acid-base, electrolyte or volume derangement currently
options.rationale: Creatinine reflects dehydration but is not the key operative-readiness parameter. Would never be sufficient alone (Source: RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: The metabolic derangement of pyloric stenosis is a hypochloraemic metabolic alkalosis. Surgery should be delayed until the chloride is >95 mmol/L and bicarbonate <30 mmol/L to avoid perioperative complications such as apnoea. Potassium and sodium corrections are also monitored but are secondary.
key_points: Preoperative correction target: Cl⁻ >95, HCO₃⁻ <30. (Source: RCH CPG: Pyloric Stenosis)
Surgery before correction risks perioperative apnoea due to metabolic alkalosis. (Source: RCH CPG: Pyloric Stenosis)
explanation.pearls: Pyloric stenosis is a medical emergency before it is a surgical one – correct the biochemistry first.

stem: A term infant is born through thick meconium-stained liquor with poor respiratory effort and a heart rate of 60 bpm. The infant is placed under a radiant warmer, and the airway is suctioned with a large-bore suction catheter under direct vision. Despite this, the infant remains bradycardic with a heart rate of 60 bpm and is gasping. Positive pressure ventilation with a T-piece device is commenced. The heart rate improves to 100 bpm after 30 seconds of effective ventilation. The infant begins to breathe spontaneously but remains cyanotic with an SpO₂ of 70% on air.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 100 bpm, SpO₂ 70%, respiratory effort present
data_table.Examination: Thick meconium, suctioned, now breathing, cyanotic
data_table.Investigations: None

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue positive pressure ventilation with supplemental oxygen, titrating FiO2 to achieve target SpO2 of 80-85% by 5 minutes
options.rationale: According to ANZCOR guidelines, after initial steps and with heart rate >100 bpm but persistent cyanosis, the next step is to provide supplemental oxygen during ventilation or spontaneous breathing, aiming for preductal SpO₂ targets (e.g., 80–85% at 5 minutes). Routine tracheal suctioning of meconium is no longer recommended if the infant is vigorous or has been adequately suctioned initially. (Source: ANZCOR Guideline 13.4: Newborn Resuscitation)

options.text: Perform immediate tracheal intubation and suction the trachea for meconium before continuing ventilation
options.rationale: Routine tracheal suctioning for meconium is not indicated if perineal suction adequate and no airway obstruction. Would be correct only with non-vigorous infant and obstructed airway (Source: ANZCOR 13.4).

options.text: Commence chest compressions as the SpO2 remains below 80% despite ventilation
options.rationale: Chest compressions are for HR under 60 after 30 sec effective ventilation; HR is 100 here. Would be correct only with HR under 60 (Source: ANZCOR 13.4).

options.text: Administer intravenous adrenaline at the neonatal resuscitation dose as the infant remains cyanotic
options.rationale: Adrenaline is for HR under 60 despite ventilation and compressions; HR is 100 here. Would be correct only with HR under 60 (Source: ANZCOR 13.4).

options.text: Provide blow-by oxygen at 10 L/min and observe, as the heart rate is above 100 bpm and the infant is breathing
options.rationale: Infant remains significantly cyanotic with SpO2 70 percent, requiring PPV with O2, not just blow-by. Would be correct only with mild cyanosis and good HR (Source: ANZCOR 13.4).

explanation.summary: Neonatal resuscitation follows the ANZCOR pathway: dry, warm, stimulate (most term babies cry within 30 seconds), assess heart rate and respiratory effort. If apnoeic, gasping or heart rate under 100, begin positive-pressure ventilation with mask and T-piece (PIP 30 cmH2O, PEEP 5, rate 40-60/minute) for 30 seconds in air for term babies. Reassess after 30 seconds: if heart rate over 100 and good respiratory effort, transition to skin contact; if 60-100 continue ventilation; if under 60 start chest compressions at 3:1 ratio with ventilation, escalate FiO2, gain IV/IO access for adrenaline 10-30 micrograms/kg. Preductal SpO2 targets rise over the first 10 minutes (60% at 1 min, 65% at 2 min, 70% at 3 min, 85% at 5 min, 90% at 10 min). Meconium suctioning is no longer routine since 2015 - effective ventilation takes priority. Cord clamping is delayed at least 60 seconds in vigorous term newborns.
key_points: If heart rate >100 bpm but SpO₂ low, titrate oxygen to meet target SpO₂. (Source: ANZCOR Guideline 13.4)
Routine intubation for tracheal suction of meconium is no longer recommended. (Source: ANZCOR Guideline 13.4)
explanation.pearls: Routine tracheal suctioning of meconium in a non-vigorous newborn was abandoned in 2015 ILCOR/ANZCOR guidelines - it delays effective ventilation. Begin positive-pressure ventilation immediately, suction only for visible airway obstruction (ANZCOR Neonatal Resuscitation).

stem: A 7-year-old boy with new-onset T1DM is being treated for severe DKA with IV insulin 0.1 units/kg/hr and IV 0.9% saline. Initial: pH 6.98, HCO3 4, glucose 32, ketones over 6. After 4 hours: glucose 18, pH 7.12, HCO3 7, ketones 4.5. He has received 1.5 L of 0.9% saline. He suddenly develops a severe headache, becomes bradycardic (HR 55), hypertensive (BP 130/90), and his GCS drops from 15 to 9. Pupils are equal but sluggish. Na 130, K 4.8. Glucose remains 18 (NOT hypoglycaemia).

data_table.Vital signs: HR 55 bpm, BP 130/90, RR irregular, SpO₂ 96%, GCS 9
data_table.Examination: Sudden headache, bradycardia, hypertension, deteriorating consciousness
data_table.Investigations: Na⁺ 130, K⁺ 4.8, glucose 18, pH 7.12, HCO₃⁻ 7, ketones 4.5
data_table.Medications: IV insulin 0.1 U/kg/hr, 0.9% NaCl at 1.5x maintenance

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate intervention?

options.text: IV 3% hypertonic saline 5 mL/kg over 10-20 minutes OR IV mannitol 0.5-1 g/kg; reduce IV fluid rate by one-third; arrange urgent CT and PICU input
options.rationale: The triad of headache, bradycardia, hypertension and falling GCS during DKA treatment is the Cushing reflex of cerebral oedema. Australian and ISPAD guidance is immediate osmotherapy (3 percent saline 5 mL/kg or mannitol 0.5-1 g/kg), fluid rate reduction by about a third, head elevation, urgent imaging and PICU transfer. Routine hyperventilation is avoided because of cerebral ischaemia risk.

options.text: Stop insulin, give 2 mL/kg of 50% dextrose IV and IV hydrocortisone 50 mg without addressing the raised intracranial pressure or cerebral oedema
options.rationale: Glucose 18 is not hypoglycaemia and no adrenal crisis; cerebral oedema requires osmotherapy. Would be correct only in hypoglycaemia or adrenal crisis (Source: ISPAD).

options.text: Intubate immediately and hyperventilate to pCO2 25-30 mmHg as a primary intervention to lower intracranial pressure in this child today urgently
options.rationale: Routine hyperventilation not recommended; brief hyperventilation only as temporising during herniation. Osmotherapy is immediate intervention. Would be correct only briefly during herniation (Source: ISPAD).

options.text: Increase 0.9% saline rate to 20 mL/kg/hour to improve cerebral perfusion despite the established cerebral oedema seen on current imaging studies
options.rationale: Rapid fluid administration can worsen cerebral oedema; rate should be REDUCED, not increased. Would never be correct (Source: ISPAD).

options.text: Reduce insulin to 0.05 units/kg/hour and continue current fluid management without any specific neuroprotective intervention at this point today
options.rationale: Insulin reduction alone does not address cerebral oedema; immediate osmotherapy required. Would be correct only as adjunct (Source: ISPAD).

explanation.summary: Cerebral oedema is the most feared complication of paediatric DKA. When clinical signs develop (headache, bradycardia, hypertension, altered consciousness), immediate osmotherapy with mannitol or hypertonic saline is critical. CT brain should be obtained urgently but must not delay treatment.
key_points: Cerebral oedema signs: headache, bradycardia, hypertension, altered GCS. (Source: RCH CPG: DKA)
Immediate treatment: mannitol 0.5–1 g/kg IV or hypertonic saline 3% 5 mL/kg. (Source: ISPAD Guidelines)
explanation.pearls: In DKA, a falling heart rate is a red flag – bradycardia in a child with DKA means cerebral oedema until proven otherwise.

stem: A 16-year-old girl with severe anorexia nervosa and BMI 14 presents with 4 weeks of postprandial epigastric pain, early satiety and bilious vomiting; she has lost a further 3 kg. Examination: scaphoid abdomen, visible aortic pulsation and succussion splash. Upper GI contrast: dilated proximal duodenum with abrupt linear cut-off at the third part. CT angiography: aortomesenteric angle 6 degrees (normal 28-65). She is haemodynamically stable. BP 90/60, HR 100. She has no other identified GI disease.

data_table.Examination: Scaphoid abdomen, visible aortic pulsation, succession splash
data_table.Investigations: UGI: dilated duodenum, linear cut-off; CT: aortomesenteric angle 6°

lead_in: Which combination of diagnosis and management plan is most appropriate?

options.text: Superior mesenteric artery (SMA) syndrome from severe weight loss; nasojejunal feeds with cautious eating-disorder team refeeding
options.rationale: SMA syndrome (Wilkie's) occurs when the aortomesenteric angle is reduced (often under 25 degrees) compressing the third part of the duodenum, typically after rapid weight loss as in anorexia nervosa. AU paediatric multi-step management: nasojejunal feeds (bypass the obstruction) with structured refeeding under MARSIPAN-AU principles (electrolyte and refeeding-syndrome surveillance), with surgery (duodenojejunostomy) reserved for failure to gain weight or anatomical persistence.

options.text: Chronic duodenal atresia with neonatal-onset bilious vomiting and a high duodenal cut-off on contrast
options.rationale: Duodenal atresia presents in neonatal period with bilious vomiting on day 1. Would be correct in neonatal presentation (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Annular pancreas causing congenital duodenal compression in infancy with chronic feeding intolerance
options.rationale: Annular pancreas typically presents in infancy or early childhood. Would be correct only in young age (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Eosinophilic oesophagitis with dysphagia, food impaction and ringed oesophageal mucosa endoscopically
options.rationale: EoE presents with dysphagia and food impaction, not duodenal-level obstruction. Would be correct with dysphagia/impaction (Source: RACP).

options.text: Median arcuate ligament syndrome causing post-prandial coeliac artery compression and epigastric pain
options.rationale: MALS causes coeliac artery compression and post-prandial epigastric pain but does not cause duodenal cut-off. Would be correct in median arcuate compression (Source: RACP).

explanation.summary: SMA syndrome results from loss of the mesenteric fat pad, causing a narrowed aortomesenteric angle and duodenal compression. It presents with bilious vomiting, postprandial pain, and weight loss. Treatment includes nutritional support and sometimes surgery.
key_points: SMA syndrome: aortomesenteric angle <25°, duodenal compression. (Source: StatPearls: SMA Syndrome)
Common in rapid weight loss, anorexia nervosa. (Source: RCH CPG: SMA Syndrome)
explanation.pearls: Think SMA syndrome in a young patient with severe weight loss and bilious vomiting.

stem: A 6-week-old term infant presents with persistent jaundice and acholic stools since 3 weeks of age. Total bilirubin is 160 µmol/L, conjugated bilirubin 100 µmol/L, AST 150 U/L, GGT 1200 U/L. Abdominal ultrasound shows an absent gallbladder after a 4-hour fast and the 'triangular cord' sign. A hepatobiliary iminodiacetic acid (HIDA) scan after phenobarbitone priming shows no tracer excretion into the bowel at 24 hours.

data_table.Vital signs: Normal for age
data_table.Examination: Jaundiced, pale stools, liver 2 cm below RCM
data_table.Investigations: Conjugated bili 100, GGT 1200, USS absent GB, triangular cord sign, HIDA no excretion

lead_in: What is the most critical factor for prognosis?

options.text: Performing the Kasai portoenterostomy before 60 days of age
options.rationale: The success of the Kasai procedure in restoring bile flow and preserving native liver function is highly time-dependent. Surgery performed before 60 days of age results in significantly better outcomes than later surgery. (Source: RCH CPG: Biliary Atresia; AASLD Guidelines)

options.text: Pretreatment with ursodeoxycholic acid for 2 weeks before surgery
options.rationale: UDCA is used after surgery to promote bile flow but does not affect the need for timely Kasai. Would be correct as post-op adjunct (Source: RCH CPG: Biliary Atresia).

options.text: Administration of intravenous immunoglobulin
options.rationale: IVIG not used in biliary atresia. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Liver biopsy before surgery to confirm the diagnosis
options.rationale: Biopsy is part of workup but should not delay surgery. Would be correct as part of workup, not delay (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Genotyping to identify the specific gene mutation
options.rationale: Biliary atresia is not typically genetic; priority is early surgery. Would never be the critical factor (Source: RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: Biliary atresia requires early diagnosis and Kasai portoenterostomy ideally before 60 days of age to maximise the chance of successful bile drainage and preserve liver function. Late referral leads to progressive cirrhosis and need for liver transplantation.
key_points: Kasai surgery before 60 days: 70–80% success in bile drainage; after 90 days: <20% success. (Source: RCH CPG: Biliary Atresia)
Acholic stools and conjugated hyperbilirubinemia in a term infant >2 weeks: urgent referral. (Source: RCH CPG: Biliary Atresia)
explanation.pearls: In biliary atresia, time is liver. Every day of delay increases the risk of cirrhosis and transplant.

stem: A 10-year-old boy with sickle cell disease (HbSS) presents with a 2-day history of fever, cough, and pleuritic chest pain. He has been hospitalised for a pain crisis affecting his arms and legs. His oxygen saturation is 88% on room air, and a chest X-ray shows a new infiltrate in the right lower lobe. Despite initiation of intravenous antibiotics, supplemental oxygen, and incentive spirometry, his SpO₂ drops to 85% on 4 L of oxygen, and his respiratory rate increases to 45. Haemoglobin is 65 g/L (baseline 80 g/L), and reticulocyte count is low.

data_table.Vital signs: SpO₂ 85% on O₂, RR 45, HR 130
data_table.Examination: Pleuritic pain, right-sided crackles
data_table.Investigations: Hb 65 (baseline 80), reticulocytopenia, CXR: new infiltrate

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Perform an exchange transfusion to reduce the percentage of haemoglobin S and improve oxygenation
options.rationale: Severe acute chest syndrome with respiratory failure and worsening anaemia is an indication for exchange transfusion to lower HbS levels and improve oxygen-carrying capacity. Simple transfusion may be insufficient and increase viscosity. (Source: RCH CPG: Sickle Cell Disease; NHLBI Guidelines)

options.text: Administer a simple top-up transfusion of packed red blood cells
options.rationale: Simple transfusion can increase blood viscosity and risk further sickling if HbS remains high; exchange preferred in severe ACS. Would be correct only in mild ACS (Source: NHLBI).

options.text: Start hydroxycarbamide (hydroxyurea) daily
options.rationale: Hydroxyurea is long-term therapy, not acute treatment for ACS. Would be correct for chronic prevention (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Administer intravenous immunoglobulin 1 g/kg
options.rationale: IVIG not indicated for ACS. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Increase oxygen flow and continue observation
options.rationale: With worsening respiratory failure, more aggressive therapy needed (exchange transfusion). Would be correct only in mild ACS (Source: NHLBI).

explanation.summary: Acute chest syndrome is a life-threatening complication of sickle cell disease. When there is respiratory failure, exchange transfusion is the recommended treatment to rapidly reduce HbS and improve oxygenation. Simple transfusion may be used in milder cases.
key_points: Severe ACS: exchange transfusion to lower HbS <30%. (Source: NHLBI Guidelines)
Reticulocytopenia and falling Hb suggest acute sequestration or aplastic crisis; exchange transfusion can address both. (Source: RCH CPG: Sickle Cell Disease)
explanation.pearls: In sickle cell disease, a drop in SpO₂ with new infiltrate should trigger urgent consideration of exchange transfusion.

stem: A 7-year-old boy is brought to ED after being struck by a car. He was intubated on scene for GCS 5 (E1V2M2) with a left fixed dilated pupil. On arrival BP 85/50, HR 140, SpO2 98 percent on ventilation. Non-contrast CT shows a large left subdural haematoma with 8 mm midline shift, effaced basal cisterns and early uncal herniation. The neurosurgical team is 20 minutes away by retrieval. He has decerebrate posturing on the right and the left pupil remains fixed at 6 mm.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 85/50, HR 140, GCS 5T, left fixed dilated pupil, ventilated SpO₂ 98%
data_table.Examination: Unresponsive, left pupil 6 mm fixed, right 4 mm reactive, decerebrate posturing
data_table.Investigations: CT: large left subdural, midline shift 8 mm, uncal herniation, effaced cisterns

lead_in: Which medical intervention is most appropriate to reduce ICP while awaiting transfer, AND why is it preferred over the alternative osmotherapy in this context?

options.text: IV 3% hypertonic saline 5 mL/kg over 10-20 minutes (preferred over mannitol in hypotension), with normocapnia and head-up positioning
options.rationale: BTF paediatric and RCH guidance: in a child with herniation signs AND hypotension, hypertonic saline is preferred over mannitol because mannitol can worsen the hypotension via osmotic diuresis. Hypertonic saline reduces ICP and expands intravascular volume. Head positioning, normocapnia and CPP maintenance are concurrent measures; brief mild hyperventilation is only a temporising rescue for active herniation.

options.text: IV mannitol 1 g/kg over 20 minutes as the primary osmotherapy agent despite his hypotension and ongoing intravascular depletion
options.rationale: Mannitol effective osmotherapy but causes osmotic diuresis and can worsen hypotension; hypertonic saline preferred in shock. Would be correct in normotensive patient (Source: RCH CPG: TBI).

options.text: Immediately hyperventilate to pCO2 25-30 mmHg as the primary intervention to reduce intracranial pressure ahead of any osmotherapy
options.rationale: Prophylactic aggressive hyperventilation can cause cerebral ischaemia; osmotherapy is first-line. Would be correct only briefly during herniation (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Insert ICP monitor and start barbiturate (thiopentone) coma immediately before any osmotic agent or definitive neurosurgical intervention
options.rationale: ICP monitor and barbiturate coma are later steps after initial stabilisation, neurosurgical consultation and surgical decompression. Would be correct after stabilisation (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: IV dexamethasone to reduce cerebral oedema from his traumatic brain injury as the first-line measure ahead of osmotherapy or imaging
options.rationale: Steroids not indicated in TBI and may worsen outcomes. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: In a child with severe TBI and signs of herniation, immediate osmotherapy with hypertonic saline is the standard, especially with hypotension. Mannitol may be used if euvolemic. Hyperventilation is reserved for acute deterioration not responding to osmotherapy. Steroids are contraindicated.
key_points: Herniation signs: fixed dilated pupil, decerebrate posturing, Cushing's triad. (Source: RCH CPG: TBI)
Hypertonic saline 3% 5 mL/kg is first-line osmotherapy in paediatric TBI with shock. (Source: BTF Guidelines)
explanation.pearls: In paediatric TBI with shock, hypertonic saline is both a volume expander and ICP reducer.

stem: A 5-year-old girl is admitted with a 5-day history of fever, cough, and right-sided pleuritic chest pain. Chest X-ray shows a right lower lobe consolidation with a moderate-sized pleural effusion. She is commenced on intravenous ampicillin. Despite 48 hours of intravenous antibiotics, she remains febrile at 39.0°C, and chest ultrasound reveals a loculated pleural effusion with septations and anechoic fluid. A chest drain is inserted, but only 50 mL of purulent fluid is drained.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 39.0°C, HR 130, RR 35, SpO₂ 92% RA
data_table.Examination: Right-sided crackles, reduced breath sounds, bronchial breathing
data_table.Investigations: CXR: RLL consolidation, effusion; US: loculated effusion with septations, chest drain placed

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Administer intrapleural urokinase 10,000 units in 30 mL normal saline twice daily via the chest drain
options.rationale: In loculated empyema, intrapleural fibrinolytics (urokinase or alteplase) can break down septations and improve drainage, avoiding the need for video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery (VATS) in many cases. The Multicentre Intrapleural Sepsis Trial (MIST2) supports this approach. (Source: RCH CPG: Complicated Pneumonia and Empyema; BTS Guidelines)

options.text: Proceed directly to VATS for decortication and drainage under general anaesthesia
options.rationale: VATS is effective but more invasive; reserved for failure of fibrinolytic therapy or severe disease. Would be correct after fibrinolytic failure (Source: RCH CPG: Empyema).

options.text: Change antibiotics to intravenous cefotaxime and metronidazole and continue observation for a further 48 hours
options.rationale: Antibiotics alone insufficient for loculated empyema; mechanical drainage needed. Would be correct only in mild non-loculated disease (Source: BTS).

options.text: Insert a second chest drain in the posterior axillary line under ultrasound guidance
options.rationale: Second drain is not first-line; fibrinolysis should be attempted first. Would be correct only after fibrinolysis failure (Source: BTS).

options.text: Administer oral prednisolone daily for 5 days to reduce inflammation
options.rationale: Corticosteroids not routinely indicated in empyema. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: Loculated empyema that does not drain with a chest drain alone is best managed with intrapleural fibrinolytics (urokinase or alteplase) to disrupt septations. VATS is reserved for failure of medical therapy or severe cases. Changing antibiotics or inserting a second drain is less effective.
key_points: For loculated empyema: intrapleural fibrinolytics as first-line after chest drain. (Source: RCH CPG: Empyema)
VATS is used if fibrinolytics fail or for persistent sepsis. (Source: BTS Guidelines)
explanation.pearls: Urokinase is more available in Australia than alteplase for intrapleural use and is equally effective.

stem: A 12-year-old boy with known peanut allergy develops anaphylaxis within minutes of ingesting a cookie containing peanut. He received intramuscular adrenaline 0.5 mg twice (5 minutes apart), intravenous normal saline boluses (total 60 mL/kg), and an intravenous adrenaline infusion at 0.1 mcg/kg/min, which has been titrated to 0.5 mcg/kg/min. Despite this, he remains hypotensive at 70/40 mmHg, tachycardic at 150 bpm, and has cold, mottled extremities with an SpO₂ of 85% on high-flow oxygen.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 70/40, HR 150, SpO₂ 85% ventilated, mottled
data_table.Examination: Ventilated, cold extremities, no urticaria now, poor perfusion
data_table.Investigations: Normal glucose, electrolytes; pH 7.15, lactate 6 mmol/L
data_table.Medications: Adrenaline infusion 0.5 mcg/kg/min, IV fluids, IM adrenaline x2

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next pharmacological intervention?

options.text: Administer intravenous methylene blue over 20 minutes, as it may improve refractory vasoplegic shock in anaphylaxis
options.rationale: Methylene blue has case reports in refractory anaphylactic shock but is not first-line rescue in AU; vasopressin preferred. Would be correct as later salvage (Source: ASCIA).

options.text: Give intravenous glucagon 1 mg bolus, as the patient is likely on beta-blocker therapy
options.rationale: Glucagon is useful in beta-blocker patients with anaphylaxis; no beta-blocker history here. Would be correct with beta-blocker history (Source: ASCIA).

options.text: Administer intravenous hydrocortisone 200 mg for its rapid vasopressor effect
options.rationale: Corticosteroids have delayed onset (hours) and do not provide immediate haemodynamic support. Would be correct only as adjunct, not for acute haemodynamics (Source: ASCIA).

options.text: Start intravenous vasopressin infusion at 0.01 units/min
options.rationale: Adding a second vasopressor such as vasopressin or noradrenaline is recommended in refractory anaphylactic shock when adrenaline infusion and fluid resuscitation have not restored perfusion; vasopressin counters the vasoplegia from histamine release. (Source: ASCIA Anaphylaxis Guidelines; ANZCOR Anaphylaxis)

options.text: Increase the adrenaline infusion to a higher rate
options.rationale: Higher adrenaline doses may cause arrhythmias and are less effective in anaphylactic vasoplegia; vasopressin provides different mechanism. Would be correct only at moderate doses (Source: ASCIA).
