explanation.summary: Refractory anaphylactic shock not responding to maximised adrenaline infusion and aggressive fluid resuscitation warrants addition of a second vasopressor (vasopressin or noradrenaline) while continuing adrenaline. Glucagon is for patients on beta-blockers. Methylene blue is a published rescue option but is not first-line Australian therapy. ECMO should be considered if pharmacological measures fail. (Source: ASCIA; ANZCOR)
key_points: Methylene blue 2 mg/kg IV for refractory anaphylactic shock. (Source: ASCIA Anaphylaxis)
Adrenaline remains first-line; methylene blue is rescue therapy. (Source: UpToDate)
explanation.pearls: In refractory anaphylaxis, consider methylene blue if the shock is vasoplegic and not responding to high-dose adrenaline.

stem: A 2-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department after his mother saw him swallow a 20 mm button battery from a remote control 30 minutes ago. He is currently asymptomatic, alert, and tolerating his saliva. Vital signs are normal. A chest X-ray shows a round radio-opaque object in the oesophagus at the level of the thoracic inlet with a double-ring sign.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 110 bpm, RR 24, SpO₂ 98%, Temp 36.8°C
data_table.Examination: Alert, no drooling, normal breath sounds, no stridor
data_table.Investigations: CXR: 20 mm button battery in oesophagus, double-ring sign

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Administer honey (or sucralfate) 10 mL orally to coat the battery and arrange urgent endoscopy for removal within 2 hours
options.rationale: Button batteries in the oesophagus can cause severe injury within 2 hours. Honey or sucralfate may help reduce injury, but urgent endoscopic removal is the priority. (Source: RCH CPG: Button Battery Ingestion; National Capital Poison Center Guidelines)

options.text: Observe in the emergency department for 6 hours and repeat X-ray to ensure passage into the stomach
options.rationale: Oesophageal button battery is emergency; observation not appropriate. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: Button Battery).

options.text: Induce vomiting with ipecac syrup to expel the battery
options.rationale: Inducing vomiting is contraindicated (re-impaction, further injury). Would never be correct (Source: NCPC).

options.text: Administer intravenous glucagon 0.5 mg to relax the oesophagus and allow passage
options.rationale: Glucagon not recommended for button battery impaction; endoscopic removal required. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Give a carbonated beverage to drink to push the battery into the stomach
options.rationale: Carbonated beverage is dangerous and not recommended. Would never be correct (Source: NCPC).

explanation.summary: Button battery ingestion is one of the most under-appreciated paediatric emergencies in Australian practice. Lithium 20-mm cells generate hydroxide ions at the negative pole within minutes of mucosal contact, producing liquefactive necrosis through the oesophageal wall within 2-4 hours and potentially fatal aorto-oesophageal fistula days later. AP and lateral chest X-rays distinguish battery (double-ring or step-off sign) from coin (single ring, smooth edge). Oesophageal impaction is an emergency requiring endoscopic removal within 2 hours irrespective of fasting status. While awaiting endoscopy in children over 12 months, honey or sucralfate 10 mL orally every 10 minutes neutralises some of the alkali burn. After removal, look for and monitor for mediastinitis, oesophageal perforation, tracheo-oesophageal fistula and delayed aortic erosion - patients need repeat endoscopy and serial CT angiography over the following weeks.
key_points: Oesophageal button battery: urgent endoscopy within 2 hours. (Source: RCH CPG: Button Battery)
Honey 10 mL may reduce injury before removal. (Source: NCPC Guidelines)
explanation.pearls: A button battery in the oesophagus causes hydroxide-mediated liquefactive necrosis within 2 hours; while awaiting endoscopy, give 10 mL honey or sucralfate every 10 minutes (over age 1) - these slow the alkali burn by about 70% (NSW Health Button Battery; ABBA).

stem: A 7-year-old boy presents to the emergency department with a 5-day history of high fever (daily maximum 39.5°C), conjunctival injection, cracked red lips, abdominal pain, vomiting, and a diffuse maculopapular rash on his trunk. His parents report that he had a mild febrile illness with cough 4 weeks ago from which he recovered fully. On examination, he is irritable, tachycardic at 140 bpm, hypotensive at 80/50 mmHg, with cool extremities and a capillary refill time of 4 seconds. He has bilateral bulbar conjunctival injection, a strawberry tongue, and swollen hands and feet. There is no lymphadenopathy. An echocardiogram shows moderate left ventricular dysfunction with an ejection fraction of 38% and mild mitral regurgitation. Laboratory tests show: white cell count 2.8 ×10⁹/L with lymphopenia (0.5 ×10⁹/L), platelets 85 ×10⁹/L, C-reactive protein 280 mg/L, ferritin 1200 mcg/L, troponin I 850 ng/L, N-terminal pro-B-type natriuretic peptide (NT-proBNP) 15,000 pg/mL, D-dimer 4.5 mg/L, and hypoalbuminaemia 22 g/L. SARS-CoV-2 serology is positive for IgG.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 39.2°C, HR 140, BP 80/50, CRT 4 sec, SpO₂ 94%
data_table.Examination: Irritable, conjunctival injection, strawberry tongue, rash, swollen hands/feet, no lymphadenopathy, cool extremities
data_table.Investigations: WCC 2.8, lymph 0.5, Plt 85, CRP 280, ferritin 1200, troponin I 850, NT-proBNP 15,000, D-dimer 4.5, albumin 22; Echo: LVEF 38%, mild MR; SARS-CoV-2 IgG positive

lead_in: What is the most likely diagnosis and appropriate first-line therapy?

options.text: Multisystem inflammatory syndrome in children (MIS-C); administer intravenous immunoglobulin 2 g/kg and intravenous methylprednisolone daily, with vasoactive support for shock
options.rationale: The constellation of prolonged fever, mucocutaneous features, multisystem involvement with cardiac dysfunction, recent SARS-CoV-2 infection, and evidence of hyperinflammation with lymphopenia and thrombocytopenia is classic for MIS-C. First-line therapy per RCH and international guidelines is IVIG 2 g/kg plus corticosteroids (methylprednisolone). Vasoactive support for shock and close cardiac monitoring are essential. (Source: RCH CPG: Paediatric Inflammatory Multisystem Syndrome; WHO MIS-C Criteria; Best et al., NEJM 2021)

options.text: Kawasaki disease shock syndrome; administer IVIG 2 g/kg and high-dose aspirin daily, and commence a heparin infusion for coronary artery thrombosis
options.rationale: Phenotypic overlap with KD but age (7, older than typical KD), lymphopenia, thrombocytopenia (not thrombocytosis), markedly elevated inflammatory markers and recent SARS-CoV-2 exposure favour MIS-C. Would be correct in classic KD age 6 months-5 years (Source: RCH CPG: MIS-C).

options.text: Septic shock of bacterial origin; obtain blood cultures and commence broad-spectrum antibiotics (cefotaxime, vancomycin, clindamycin) and a noradrenaline infusion, deferring immunomodulatory therapy
options.rationale: Sepsis is always differential but mucocutaneous features, cardiac dysfunction, markedly elevated markers and recent SARS-CoV-2 exposure make MIS-C far more likely. Would be correct with positive cultures (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Acute rheumatic fever; commence benzathine penicillin G 900 mg IM, oral prednisolone, and aspirin
options.rationale: ARF has migratory polyarthritis, erythema marginatum, subcutaneous nodules or Sydenham chorea with preceding GAS evidence. Would be correct with Jones criteria met (Source: RHDAustralia).

options.text: Haemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis (HLH); commence the HLH-2004 protocol with etoposide, dexamethasone, and cyclosporine
options.rationale: HLH typically has higher ferritin (over 5000), cytopenias in 2+ lineages, and haemophagocytosis on BMA. MIS-C shares some HLH features but management differs. Would be correct with HLH-2004 criteria met (Source: RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: MIS-C is a post-infectious hyperinflammatory syndrome temporally associated with SARS-CoV-2. It presents with fever, mucocutaneous features, cardiac dysfunction, and laboratory evidence of hyperinflammation. First-line therapy is IVIG 2 g/kg plus methylprednisolone, with supportive care for shock. Distinction from Kawasaki disease, sepsis, and HLH is critical, as management differs.
key_points: MIS-C criteria: fever ≥24h, multisystem involvement (≥2 systems), evidence of inflammation, and positive SARS-CoV-2 exposure or serology. (Source: WHO MIS-C Criteria)
First-line: IVIG 2 g/kg + methylprednisolone 10–30 mg/kg/day. (Source: RCH CPG: PIMS-TS)
Key lab features: lymphopenia, thrombocytopenia, high CRP, elevated ferritin, elevated troponin and NT-proBNP. (Source: Best et al., NEJM 2021)
Distinguish from Kawasaki disease by age, thrombocytopenia, lymphopenia, and SARS-CoV-2 serology. (Source: RCH CPG: PIMS-TS)
explanation.pearls: In MIS-C, the heart is the target organ – always obtain an echocardiogram and troponin early. Cardiac dysfunction can progress rapidly.

stem: A 14-year-old girl with ileocolonic Crohn's disease has been in clinical remission on maintenance infliximab 5 mg/kg every 8 weeks plus azathioprine 1.5 mg/kg/day. Over the past 3 months she has developed abdominal pain, 4-5 loose stools per day and a rising faecal calprotectin from 50 to 850 mcg/g. She is due for her infliximab infusion today. Pre-infusion bloods: infliximab trough under 1 mcg/mL (therapeutic over 3), anti-infliximab antibodies (ATI) HIGH at 120 ng/mL, CRP 35, albumin 30. There is no perianal disease and no surgical emergency. She has no contraindications to anti-TNF or methotrexate.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 85, BP 110/65, Temp 36.9°C, weight 45 kg
data_table.Examination: Mild RLQ tenderness, no perianal disease
data_table.Investigations: Infliximab trough <1, anti-infliximab antibodies 120, faecal calprotectin 850, CRP 35, albumin 30

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step given the therapeutic drug monitoring result?

options.text: Switch to adalimumab (second anti-TNF) with loading 160/80 mg at 2 weeks then 40 mg fortnightly AND add immunomodulator (methotrexate or continued azathioprine) to reduce immunogenicity
options.rationale: ECCO/ESPGHAN and AU IBD practice: subtherapeutic trough with HIGH ATI indicates immunogenic loss of response; dose escalation will not overcome neutralising antibodies. The recommended step is to switch within class (to adalimumab) and add or continue an immunomodulator (methotrexate or azathioprine) to prevent immunogenicity against the second anti-TNF.

options.text: Increase infliximab to a higher dose every 4 weeks despite the high anti-drug antibody titre suggesting immunogenicity-driven loss of response to current biologic
options.rationale: Dose escalation for low trough without significant ATI; with HIGH neutralising antibodies, escalation unlikely to work and risks infusion reactions. Would be correct only with low/absent ATI (Source: ESPGHAN).

options.text: Switch to ustekinumab as a first-line out-of-class agent without trialling a second anti-TNF biologic first, despite available within-class options
options.rationale: Ustekinumab is valid alternative but AU practice reserves out-of-class switching until after second anti-TNF failure. Would be correct after second anti-TNF failure (Source: ESPGHAN).

options.text: Add oral prednisolone 40 mg daily and continue the same infliximab regimen despite established immunogenicity to the current anti-TNF therapy here
options.rationale: Steroids may bridge symptoms but do not address underlying immunogenic loss of response. Would be correct only as bridge to new biologic (Source: ESPGHAN).

options.text: Stop all biologic therapy and refer for elective colectomy despite available second-line medical options including out-of-class biologic alternatives
options.rationale: Surgery is for medically refractory or complicated disease; multiple medical options remain. Would be correct after biologic failures (Source: ESPGHAN).

explanation.summary: When high anti-infliximab antibodies with undetectable trough levels cause loss of response, switching to another anti-TNF agent (adalimumab) is appropriate, especially with the addition of an immunomodulator to reduce future immunogenicity. Dose escalation of the original agent is unlikely to overcome antibodies.
key_points: Immunogenic loss of response: high anti-drug antibodies, low/undetectable trough. (Source: ECCO/ESPGHAN TDM Guidelines)
Switch within class (infliximab → adalimumab) is effective in many patients. (Source: ECCO/ESPGHAN Guidelines)
Adding methotrexate may reduce antibody formation to the second anti-TNF. (Source: RCH CPG: IBD)
explanation.pearls: Therapeutic drug monitoring in IBD allows precision medicine: if antibodies are high, switch; if trough is low without antibodies, escalate.

stem: A 14-year-old previously well girl presents with a 4-week history of progressive psychiatric symptoms. Initially, she became anxious, withdrawn, and developed insomnia. Over the past week, she has developed rapid, jerky speech, orofacial dyskinesias, and episodes of agitation alternating with catatonic posturing. She has had three generalised tonic-clonic seizures in the past 24 hours. On examination, she has autonomic instability with labile blood pressure (ranging 160/100 to 90/50), tachycardia, and hyperthermia (38.3°C). She exhibits florid orofacial and limb dyskinesias, echolalia, and periods of mutism. MRI brain with contrast is normal. Lumbar puncture shows mild lymphocytic pleocytosis (WCC 25 ×10⁶/L, predominantly lymphocytes), normal glucose, and mildly elevated protein. Electroencephalogram shows extreme delta brush pattern. Serum and CSF anti-NMDA receptor antibodies are strongly positive. A pelvic ultrasound reveals a 5 cm right ovarian teratoma.

data_table.Vital signs: BP labile, HR 120–150, Temp 38.3°C, RR 20–30
data_table.Examination: Orofacial and limb dyskinesias, echolalia, mutism, catatonic posturing, autonomic instability
data_table.Investigations: CSF: 25 WCC, lymphocytic; EEG: extreme delta brush; anti-NMDAR antibodies positive; Pelvic USS: 5 cm ovarian teratoma

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate treatment?

options.text: IV methylprednisolone 1 g daily for 5 days plus IVIG 2 g/kg over 5 days, urgent laparoscopic oophorectomy
options.rationale: Anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis is a paraneoplastic autoimmune disorder commonly associated with ovarian teratoma. First-line immunotherapy (high-dose corticosteroids plus IVIG or plasma exchange) should be commenced immediately, along with tumour removal. The combination of teratoma resection and immunotherapy improves outcomes and reduces relapse risk. (Source: RCH CPG: Autoimmune Encephalitis; Titulaer et al., Lancet Neurology 2013)

options.text: Start oral risperidone 2 mg twice daily for psychosis and schedule routine outpatient psychiatric review next week
options.rationale: Antipsychotics may worsen dyskinesias and do not address underlying autoimmune process. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: Autoimmune Encephalitis).

options.text: Initiate IV aciclovir 8-hourly for presumed herpes simplex CNS infection until CSF PCR returns
options.rationale: Aciclovir should be commenced empirically in suspected viral encephalitis, but positive anti-NMDAR, EEG extreme delta brush and ovarian teratoma confirm autoimmune cause. Would be correct as initial empirical until PCR results (Source: Titulaer 2013).

options.text: Perform therapeutic plasma exchange alone as sole immunomodulatory therapy, without corticosteroids, to avoid psychiatric worsening
options.rationale: Plasma exchange is option but typically combined with corticosteroids, not alone; tumour removal is essential. Would be correct as adjunct, not sole (Source: Titulaer 2013).

options.text: Administer intravenous rituximab weekly for 4 weeks as the first-line immunomodulatory therapy
options.rationale: Rituximab is second-line for refractory cases, not first-line. Would be correct after first-line failure (Source: Titulaer 2013).

explanation.summary: Anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis is a treatable autoimmune encephalitis often associated with ovarian teratoma. First-line therapy is high-dose corticosteroids plus IVIG or plasma exchange, along with urgent tumour removal. Early treatment improves outcomes and reduces relapse.
key_points: Anti-NMDAR encephalitis: psychiatric symptoms, dyskinesias, autonomic instability, seizures, extreme delta brush on EEG. (Source: RCH CPG: Autoimmune Encephalitis)
First-line treatment: corticosteroids + IVIG or plasma exchange + tumour resection. (Source: Titulaer et al., Lancet Neurology 2013)
Rituximab and cyclophosphamide are second-line for refractory disease. (Source: RCH CPG: Autoimmune Encephalitis)
explanation.pearls: In a teenage girl with acute psychosis, dyskinesias, and seizures, always check for anti-NMDAR antibodies and perform a pelvic ultrasound for teratoma.

stem: A 13-year-old previously well girl presents with a 2-week history of jaundice, malaise, and abdominal distension. On examination, she is icteric, has ascites, and the liver is palpable 3 cm below the costal margin with a firm edge. Laboratory tests show: total bilirubin 280 µmol/L, conjugated bilirubin 180 µmol/L, alanine aminotransferase 95 U/L, aspartate aminotransferase 120 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 85 U/L (low for age), international normalised ratio 3.5, albumin 22 g/L, haemoglobin 85 g/L, reticulocytes 12%, direct Coombs test negative.

data_table.Examination: Icteric, ascites, firm hepatomegaly, no splenomegaly, no Kayser-Fleischer rings on slit-lamp (pending)
data_table.Investigations: Bili 280/180, ALT 95, AST 120, ALP 85, INR 3.5, albumin 22, Hb 85, retic 12%, Coombs negative

lead_in: What is the most likely diagnosis and the most useful diagnostic test?

options.text: Wilson disease with acute liver failure and Coombs-negative haemolysis; check ceruloplasmin and 24-hour urinary copper
options.rationale: The combination of acute liver failure with Coombs-negative haemolytic anaemia, a low alkaline phosphatase (relative to the degree of jaundice), and an AST:ALT ratio >2 is classic for Wilson disease. Low ceruloplasmin, high 24-hour urinary copper, and high serum-free copper confirm the diagnosis. (Source: RCH CPG: Acute Liver Failure; EASL Wilson Disease Guidelines)

options.text: Autoimmune hepatitis; test for ANA, anti-smooth muscle antibody, and liver-kidney microsomal type 1 antibody
options.rationale: AIH typically has higher transaminases, positive autoantibodies, and does not cause Coombs-negative haemolytic anaemia. Would be correct with autoantibodies and no haemolysis (Source: RCH CPG: ALF).

options.text: Acute paracetamol toxicity; send serum paracetamol level and commence N-acetylcysteine without delay
options.rationale: No history of paracetamol ingestion and haemolytic anaemia is not typical. Would be correct with paracetamol history (Source: Australian PIC).

options.text: Hepatitis A infection; test serum hepatitis A IgM antibodies and arrange supportive care
options.rationale: Hep A can cause ALF but does not cause haemolytic anaemia; low ALP more typical of Wilson. Would be correct with positive serology and no haemolysis (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Budd-Chiari syndrome; arrange Doppler ultrasound of the hepatic veins for confirmation
options.rationale: Budd-Chiari has ascites, hepatomegaly and abdominal pain but haemolysis and characteristic Wilson biochemistry are absent. Would be correct with abdominal pain and ascites (Source: RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: Wilson disease presenting as acute liver failure often includes Coombs-negative haemolytic anaemia, a low or normal alkaline phosphatase, and an AST:ALT ratio >2. Diagnosis relies on serum ceruloplasmin, total copper, and 24-hour urinary copper. Early recognition is critical as liver transplantation may be needed.
key_points: Wilson disease in acute liver failure: Coombs-negative haemolytic anaemia, low ALP, AST:ALT >2. (Source: EASL Wilson Disease Guidelines)
Diagnosis: low ceruloplasmin, high 24-hour urinary copper, high serum-free copper. (Source: RCH CPG: Acute Liver Failure)
Liver transplantation may be required for acute liver failure due to Wilson disease. (Source: RCH CPG: Acute Liver Failure)
explanation.pearls: In a teenager with acute liver failure and a disproportionately low alkaline phosphatase, think Wilson disease.
